summary: As of mid-2026 the AI coding-agent market has converged on one architecture: parallel agents, each isolated in its own git worktree/branch, with a plan-then-act gate, review/merge UI, and increasingly cloud/background execution. Cursor 3 (8 parallel agents, /multitask, worktrees, cloud handoff), Conductor (Mac orchestrator running parallel Claude Code/Codex/Cursor agents in worktrees, BYO subscription, free app), Factory Droids ($1.5B, multi-surface), and Warp 2.0 (terminal-as-agent-workbench) are the leaders for the multi-agent pattern; Devin and Replit Agent 3 push full autonomy; Cline, Aider, OpenHands, Continue, and Zed anchor the open-source/model-agnostic side. The frontier engine is Claude Opus 4.8 (claude-opus-4-8, $5/$25 per MTok, 1M context) with GPT-5.3-Codex ($1.75/$14) as the de-facto review/second-opinion model — exactly the engine+reviewer split this app proposes. Two facts dominate strategy: (1) every serious tool now standardizes on the worktree-isolation + plan-mode + PR-review loop, so a "universal agent OS" must beat them on cross-surface (desktop+mobile+P2P), scheduling, messaging (WhatsApp/Telegram), and a skills marketplace rather than on the core loop; (2) Anthropic's open SKILL.md standard and the Claude Agent SDK make Claude Code a viable embeddable primary engine, but ToS/usage-limit and BYO-subscription terms are the biggest reliability and ban risks. The space moves fast — model IDs and pricing shifted multiple times in H1 2026 (Cursor moved to credits, Windsurf retired credits for quotas in March, Replit's effort pricing caused $1k/week bill shocks), so any pricing here is a mid-2026 snapshot.

keyFindings.point: Worktree-per-agent isolation is now table stakes, not a differentiator
keyFindings.detail: Cursor 3 runs up to 8 agents in parallel across isolated git worktrees via /multitask, /worktree, /best-of-n, /apply-worktree, /delete-worktree. Conductor spins a git worktree + branch + setup script per workspace in ~10s with zero cross-conflict. Both expose review-then-merge-to-PR. A universal agent OS cannot win on this loop alone; it must win on orchestration across surfaces, scheduling, and messaging.

keyFindings.point: Conductor is the closest existing product to the app's core, and a direct competitive reference
keyFindings.detail: Conductor (Melty Labs) is a FREE Mac app orchestrating parallel Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor agents in isolated worktrees; BYO Claude/Codex subscription. Has Plan Mode (0.21.0 Nov 2025), Code Review + historical diffs (0.22.0), multi-repo/fork workspaces (0.25.6), MCP + message queues (0.1.0), Bedrock/Vertex/custom providers (0.13.6), Codex Skills+Plan+Fast Mode (0.43.0 Mar 2026), and tracks frontier models within days (Opus 4.8 added May 28 2026; Sonnet 4.6 1M June 4 2026). Gaps the app can beat: Mac-only, no mobile, no native scheduler, no WhatsApp/Telegram, no image/video, no P2P.

keyFindings.point: Engine choice: Claude Opus 4.8 is the strongest primary coding engine, with clear model IDs
keyFindings.detail: claude-opus-4-8: $5/$25 per MTok, 1M context (200k on MS Foundry), 128k max output, effort defaults to 'high', Jan 2026 knowledge cutoff. claude-sonnet-4-6: $3/$15, 1M context, 64k output (cheaper default driver). claude-haiku-4-5(-20251001): $1/$5, 200k context (fast/cheap subagent tier). New top model Claude Fable 5 (claude-fable-5) GA June 9 2026 at $10/$50, 1M context, adaptive thinking always-on. Batch API = 50% discount; prompt caching available.

keyFindings.point: GPT-5.3-Codex is the natural GPT review model — released for exactly this role
keyFindings.detail: GPT-5.3-Codex (released Feb 24 2026) is OpenAI's most advanced agentic coding model and is what OpenAI's own Codex 'Code review' feature runs on. API pricing ~$1.75 input / $14.00 output per MTok. GPT-5.2-Codex (Jan 14 2026) is the cheaper sibling at the same rate card. This validates the app's Claude-as-builder + GPT-as-reviewer split; running a different vendor's model as critic catches blind spots a single-vendor pipeline misses.

keyFindings.point: Claude Agent SDK + open SKILL.md standard make Claude Code embeddable as the app's engine
keyFindings.detail: Claude Agent SDK (renamed from Claude Code SDK, Sept 2025) is the same harness powering Claude Code: file ops, shell, web, MCP, subagents, hooks. Pro plan ships $20/mo of Agent SDK usage; Max 20x ships $200. Skills are folders with SKILL.md (open standard, anti-fragmentation); plugins bundle skills+hooks+MCP. Anthropic runs an official marketplace (anthropics/skills, claude-plugins-official) plus community marketplaces (Agensi, SkillsMP) — a ready-made model for the app's 'dynamic skills marketplace'.

keyFindings.point: Pricing models are volatile and a real planning risk
keyFindings.detail: H1 2026 saw repeated repricing: Cursor tiers Hobby $0/Pro $20/Pro+ $60/Ultra $200; Windsurf retired credits for daily+weekly quotas on Mar 19 2026 and raised Pro $15→$20; Replit 'effort-based' checkpoints produced documented $1,000/week bill shocks; Codex moved from per-message to API-token billing Apr 2 2026. Devin bills in ACUs (~15 min work each) at ~$2.00–2.25/ACU. Any per-project budget feature in the app must model token+ACU+credit volatility and cap spend.

keyFindings.point: Background/cloud and scheduling are where most tools are still weak — the app's opening
keyFindings.detail: Cursor 3 added cloud agents with local<->cloud handoff; Warp shipped 'Oz' cloud agent execution (Feb 2026); Continue split IDE Agents (sync, local) vs Cloud Agents (async, event-driven, for refactors/security patches); Devin added parallel managed sessions (Feb 2026). But genuine per-project cron scheduling + push to phone + WhatsApp/Telegram triggering is absent from all of them. This is the clearest white space for a 'universal agent OS'.

keyFindings.point: Open-source/model-agnostic stack is mature enough to de-risk vendor lock-in
keyFindings.detail: OpenHands (MIT, 71k stars, self-hostable, 68.4% SWE-bench Verified w/ Opus 4.6, ~$0.20–1.05/task on self-hosted H100). Cline (Apache 2.0, 30+ providers, plan/act mode, now also SDK+CLI). Aider (Apache 2.0, architect/editor split = 30–50% cheaper, git-atomic commits). Continue (Apache 2.0, 2.5M+ installs). Zed (Rust, ACP open protocol to plug in Claude Code/Codex/OpenCode as sidebar agents). These give the app fallback engines and an integration protocol (ACP/MCP) so it isn't 100% dependent on Anthropic.

itemsOrTools.name: Cursor (Cursor 3 / Composer 2.5)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: AI IDE. Cursor 3 (Apr 2026) runs up to 8 agents in parallel on isolated git worktrees via /multitask; local execution via Composer 2.5 or cloud isolation VMs with local<->cloud handoff. Plan mode, Background/Cloud Agents, Agents Window, review-and-merge with partial file apply. In-house model Composer 2.5 (May 18 2026, built on Kimi K2.5): 79.8% SWE-bench Multilingual (~Opus 4.7 parity), Composer-tier $0.50/$2.50 per MTok, ~6.7 min/task Fast.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The strongest reference for the desktop multi-agent UX. Composer 2.5's 10-60x cheaper agentic inference is a cost benchmark the app's scheduler must compete with. Cursor's worktree command set (/worktree, /apply-worktree, /best-of-n) is a UX template to match or beat.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Hobby $0; Pro $20/mo; Pro+ $60/mo; Ultra $200/mo (20% off annual). Closed-source IDE; no open SDK to embed.

itemsOrTools.name: Conductor (conductor.build, Melty Labs)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Mac app orchestrating parallel Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor agents, each in an isolated git worktree+branch with per-workspace setup script (~10s spin-up). Plan Mode, Code Review + historical diffs, one-click PR, respond-to-review-comments, multi-repo/fork workspaces, MCP + message queues, Bedrock/Vertex/custom providers, ticket-to-workspace. Tracks frontier models within days.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The single closest existing product to the app's core engine. Validates the architecture (BYO-subscription, free app, worktree isolation, Claude-primary). The app should differentiate on: cross-platform (it is Mac-only), mobile, P2P, native per-project scheduler, WhatsApp/Telegram, and image/video studio — all of which Conductor lacks.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: App is FREE; bring your own Claude/Codex/Cursor subscription. Mac-only, no public SDK.

itemsOrTools.name: Cline
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Open-source autonomous coding agent: VS Code/JetBrains/Cursor/Windsurf/Zed/Neovim extension + preview CLI + SDK. Plan/Act mode (cheap Plan reads+reasons, Act executes with per-step approval). 30+ model providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, Bedrock, OpenRouter, Cerebras, DeepSeek, Qwen, Grok, local via Ollama/LM Studio).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best open-source reference for a model-agnostic engine and a clean plan/act gate the app can emulate. Apache 2.0 means components/patterns are reusable. BYOK economics (~$0.50-2.00/feature with Sonnet) are the cost floor to benchmark against.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free, Apache 2.0, BYO API key (pay provider directly). Teams: free first 10 seats, then $20/user/mo.

itemsOrTools.name: Windsurf (now 'Devin Desktop', Cognition)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Agentic IDE with Cascade agent (multi-file edits, terminal, codebase understanding), Codemaps, in-house SWE-1.5/1.6 models. Acquired by Cognition (~$250M, Dec 2025; $82M ARR) and being merged with Devin into one end-to-end platform; rebranded Devin Desktop.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Shows consolidation risk: an independent tool can be acquired and rebranded fast. Reinforces why the app should not hard-depend on any single third-party IDE. Cascade's quota model is a pricing reference.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free $0; Pro $20/mo (raised from $15 Mar 2026); Max $200/mo; Teams $40/user/mo; Enterprise custom. Mar 19 2026 moved from credits to daily+weekly quotas. Closed-source.

itemsOrTools.name: Aider
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Terminal AI pair programmer. Architect/editor mode: strong reasoning model plans, fast/cheap editor model emits diffs (30-50% cheaper). Atomic git commits per change, repo map, multi-language, lint/test loop. ~44k stars, ~6.8M installs, ~15B tokens/week.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Gold-standard reference for the architect+editor cost-split pattern and git-atomic-commit discipline — directly applicable to the app's Claude-builds/GPT-reviews pipeline and per-project budget control. Fully scriptable for headless/scheduled runs.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free, Apache 2.0, CLI. Pay only the connected LLM (or $0 local). No subscription.

itemsOrTools.name: Devin (Cognition)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Fully autonomous cloud software engineer. Feb 2026 added parallel managed sessions, improved context retention, playbooks, knowledge base; can orchestrate multiple managed Devins in parallel. Bills in ACUs (~15 min active work each).
itemsOrTools.relevance: The high-autonomy benchmark. Its ACU billing model is a cautionary template for the app's scheduler/budget: opaque per-task cost is a top user complaint. The app's transparent per-project budget+cap is a differentiator vs Devin.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free $0; Pro $20/mo (up to 10 concurrent); Max $200/mo (unlimited concurrent); Teams $80/mo base + $40/seat; Enterprise custom (VPC, SAML/OIDC). Overage at API rates; ACUs ~$2.00-2.25.

itemsOrTools.name: Zed
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Rust-native fast editor with Agent Panel: threads with independent context windows running simultaneously (one debugs a test while another documents an API). Terminal Threads (May 20 2026) run Claude Code or Amp as sidebar agents. Open Agent Client Protocol (ACP) plugs in Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode; open-weight Zeta2 edit model.
itemsOrTools.relevance: ACP is a concrete open integration protocol the app could adopt to embed external CLI agents uniformly — reduces lock-in and lets the app host Claude Code, Codex, etc. behind one interface. Multi-thread context isolation is a UX reference.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Personal free (2,000 edit predictions); Pro $10/mo (unlimited predictions, $5 hosted-model tokens, usage at API+10%); Business $30/seat/mo. Editor is open-source.

itemsOrTools.name: Continue (continue.dev)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Open-source 'Continuous AI'. Two agent classes: IDE Agents (sync, local, copilot-style) and Cloud Agents (async, event-driven, for refactors/docs/security patches in background/CI). Shareable custom agents (prompts+tools+rules), source-controlled AI checks enforceable in CI, open-source CLI.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best reference for the app's background-agent + scheduler vision: event-driven async agents that run standards/security checks without a human in the loop. Its custom-agent packaging mirrors the app's skills concept.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free + open-source (Apache 2.0, 2.5M+ installs); paid plans from $10/mo; Team/Company add shared agents, RBAC, SSO, BYOK. CLI is OSS.

itemsOrTools.name: OpenHands
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Open-source (MIT, 71k+ stars) self-hostable autonomous agent platform: plan/write/apply across a codebase. Web GUI, TUI, CLI, Git integrations, Cloud Agent SDK, MCP. 68.4% SWE-bench Verified with Claude Opus 4.6. Model-agnostic, dozens of providers.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The leading self-hostable fallback engine — de-risks Anthropic dependency and gives an OSS path if ToS/pricing change. Self-host cost ~$0.20-1.05/resolved task on H100 is a useful floor. Cloud Agent SDK + mobile access are direct prior art for the app's mobile story.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS free (1 user, unlimited local); Cloud Individual free (10 convos/day, mobile, MCP, BYOK at cost); Cloud Growth $500/mo (unlimited users, RBAC); Enterprise custom (VPC, SSO).

itemsOrTools.name: Replit Agent 3
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Cloud build-and-deploy agent (Sept 10 2025), ~200 min continuous autonomy, self-healing browser testing, writes+runs tests unprompted, can build/deploy other agents. 'Effort-based' checkpoint pricing.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Cautionary tale for the app's budget/scheduler: effort-based checkpoints + autonomous subagent spawning produced documented $1,000/week bill shocks (up from $180-200/mo). The app must give hard per-project spend caps and pre-run cost estimates to avoid this failure mode.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Checkpoint pricing: simple edit ~$0.10-0.25, complex feature $5+. Subscription tiers on top. Cloud-hosted, closed.

itemsOrTools.name: Warp (Warp 2.0 ADE)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Agentic Development Environment born from the terminal. Runs multiple agents in parallel (its SOTA agent + Claude Code + Codex + Gemini CLI) as vertical tabs with git branch/worktree/PR metadata. Warp Code editor (Sept 2025), Oz cloud agent execution (Feb 2026), Warp Drive team knowledge, MCP Gallery, BYOK.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best reference for a terminal-first 'agentic workbench' hosting many heterogeneous agents under one UI — close to the app's 'run any engine' ambition. Its single-plan pricing + Reload Credits is a simple model worth emulating. Oz shows the remote-execution pattern.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free 150 credits/mo (75 after 2 mo); Build $20/mo (1,500 credits, BYOK); Business $50/user/mo (SSO, ZDR, shared credits, up to 50). Open-source ADE.

itemsOrTools.name: Factory (Droids)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: 'Agent-native development': parallel self-directed Droids handle multi-step tasks across desktop, CLI, TUI, web, IDE, and Slack with synced sessions/computers/skills. Factory Bridge runs commands in your local dev env. $150M Series C at $1.5B (Apr 16 2026).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Strongest reference for cross-surface continuity (desktop+CLI+web+Slack+IDE with synced state) — exactly the multi-surface continuity the app wants across desktop+mobile+messaging. Factory Bridge is a model for safely running agent actions in a user's local environment.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: No free tier: Pro $20/mo; Plus $100/mo; Max $200/mo; Teams/Enterprise contact sales. Closed; has CLI+SDK.

itemsOrTools.name: GPT-5.3-Codex (OpenAI) — review model
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: OpenAI's most advanced agentic coding model (Feb 24 2026); powers Codex's 'Code review' feature. Combines GPT-5.2-Codex SWE performance with GPT-5.2 reasoning. API ~$1.75 input / $14.00 output per MTok. GPT-5.2-Codex (Jan 14 2026) is the cheaper sibling at the same rate card.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The recommended GPT review/second-opinion model for the app. Cross-vendor critique (Claude builds, GPT reviews) catches single-vendor blind spots. Codex moved to API-token billing (Apr 2 2026), so review-pass cost is predictable per-token — easy to budget per project.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: API via OpenAI; also on OpenRouter. ~$1.75/$14 per MTok. Codex CLI/cloud subscriptions ~$100-200/dev/mo for heavier use.

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Opus 4.8 + Agent SDK (Anthropic) — primary engine
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: claude-opus-4-8: top coding/agentic model, $5/$25 per MTok, 1M context (200k on MS Foundry), 128k output, effort defaults to 'high', Jan 2026 cutoff. Claude Agent SDK = the Claude Code harness as an embeddable library (files, shell, web, MCP, subagents, hooks). Open SKILL.md skill standard + official/community plugin marketplaces.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The app's primary engine. Agent SDK lets the app embed Claude Code's exact harness rather than reimplement it; SKILL.md + marketplaces are a ready foundation for the 'dynamic skills marketplace'. Cheaper drivers: claude-sonnet-4-6 ($3/$15, 1M) and claude-haiku-4-5 ($1/$5) for subagents. Batch API -50%; prompt caching for cost control.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: API pay-per-token (IDs above). Claude Code via Pro $20/mo ($20 Agent SDK usage), Max 5x $100, Max 20x $200 ($200 SDK usage). SDK is official, supported.

technicalOptions.option: Embed Claude Code via the Claude Agent SDK as the primary engine
technicalOptions.pros: Same harness that powers Claude Code (files, shell, MCP, subagents, hooks, skills); official + supported; SKILL.md/plugin marketplace ready-made for the skills store; Opus 4.8/Sonnet 4.6 at 1M context; effort param + Batch API + caching for cost control.
technicalOptions.cons: Vendor concentration on Anthropic; subject to Anthropic usage limits/ToS; per-token API cost can spike on long agentic runs; consumer Claude Code subscriptions are not designed to be resold/proxied (ban risk if abused).

technicalOptions.option: BYO-subscription model (Conductor-style): app is free, user brings Claude/Codex login
technicalOptions.pros: Zero LLM cost to the app operator; users keep their own limits; proven by Conductor; sidesteps reselling-tokens risk.
technicalOptions.cons: Automating/headless-driving a consumer subscription may violate ToS and risk user account bans; reliability tied to the user's plan limits; harder to guarantee scheduled/background runs when the user's quota is exhausted.

technicalOptions.option: GPT-5.3-Codex as the cross-vendor review model (Claude builds, GPT reviews)
technicalOptions.pros: Purpose-built for code review (powers OpenAI's own review feature); cross-vendor critique catches single-vendor blind spots; predictable per-token cost ($1.75/$14); decouples review from the build engine.
technicalOptions.cons: Adds a second vendor dependency/billing relationship; output token cost ($14/MTok) makes large-diff reviews non-trivial; two SDKs to maintain.

technicalOptions.option: Adopt an open agent protocol (ACP from Zed and/or MCP) to host multiple engines uniformly
technicalOptions.pros: Lets the app run Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, OpenHands behind one interface; reduces lock-in; future-proofs against acquisitions/repricing (cf. Windsurf->Cognition); MCP gives a standard tool/skill interface already used by Conductor, Warp, Continue.
technicalOptions.cons: Protocols are young and moving; per-engine quirks leak through; more integration surface to test.

technicalOptions.option: Self-host OpenHands (MIT) as a fallback/offline engine
technicalOptions.pros: De-risks Anthropic dependency; ~$0.20-1.05/resolved task self-hosted; model-agnostic; MIT license; mobile + Cloud Agent SDK prior art.
technicalOptions.cons: Lower ceiling than frontier closed models (68.4% SWE-bench w/ Opus 4.6 vs higher for hosted); ops burden of GPUs; slower to track newest models.

recommendations: Make Claude Opus 4.8 (claude-opus-4-8) the default builder and Sonnet 4.6 (claude-sonnet-4-6) the default cheap driver, with Haiku 4.5 (claude-haiku-4-5) for subagents; use GPT-5.3-Codex as the review/second-opinion pass. This Claude-builds/GPT-reviews split is exactly what OpenAI's own Codex review feature and the architect/editor pattern (Aider) validate.

Embed the Claude Agent SDK rather than reimplementing the harness — inherit subagents, hooks, skills, and MCP for free, and adopt the open SKILL.md standard so the app's skills marketplace is interoperable with Anthropic's and community marketplaces (Agensi, SkillsMP) from day one.

Treat worktree-per-agent isolation + plan-mode gate + review/merge-to-PR as table-stakes you must match (copy Cursor's /worktree, /apply-worktree, /best-of-n UX and Conductor's per-workspace setup script). Do NOT pitch this as your differentiator — it is commoditized.

Differentiate on what no competitor ships together: a true per-project cron scheduler, background/remote agents that push results to mobile, and WhatsApp/Telegram triggering+notifications. This is the clearest white space (Continue/Warp Oz/Devin have pieces; none have the full messaging+scheduler+mobile loop).

Ship hard per-project spend caps, pre-run cost estimates, and live token/ACU meters — directly countering the #1 market complaint (Replit's $1k/week bill shocks, Devin's opaque ACUs). Use Batch API (-50%) and prompt caching for scheduled/background runs to keep costs predictable.

Adopt an open protocol layer (MCP everywhere; consider Zed's ACP) so the app can host Claude Code, Codex, OpenHands, and OpenCode behind one interface — insurance against repricing and acquisitions (Windsurf->Cognition shows how fast a dependency can vanish).

For the engine business model, prefer first-party API keys (or an enterprise/commercial Anthropic agreement) over driving users' consumer Claude/Codex subscriptions headlessly; the BYO-consumer-subscription path (Conductor-style) is convenient but carries real account-ban/ToS risk when automated and scheduled.
Keep OpenHands (MIT) wired as a self-hostable fallback engine for offline/air-gapped, cost-sensitive, or Anthropic-outage scenarios, even if Claude is the default.
risks: ToS / account-ban risk: headlessly automating or scheduling a user's consumer Claude Code or Codex subscription (BYO-subscription model) likely violates provider terms and can get user accounts banned; safest path is first-party API keys or a commercial agreement.

Vendor lock-in / concentration: making Claude the sole engine exposes the app to Anthropic pricing changes, usage-limit tightening, and model deprecations (e.g., Opus 4.1 retires Aug 5 2026; Sonnet 4 / Opus 4 retire June 15 2026 — pinned model IDs must be migrated proactively).

Cost volatility and bill shock: token/ACU/credit pricing shifted repeatedly in H1 2026 (Cursor credits, Windsurf credits->quotas Mar 19, Codex per-message->per-token Apr 2, Replit effort checkpoints causing $1k/week). Without hard caps and estimates, autonomous/background agents can run up large bills fast.

Commoditization of the core loop: parallel worktree agents + plan mode + PR review are now shipped by Cursor, Conductor, Warp, Factory, Zed and others — the app cannot differentiate on the engine loop and must win on cross-surface (mobile/P2P), scheduling, and messaging or risk being a 'me-too' wrapper.

Acquisition/consolidation risk in dependencies: Windsurf was acquired and rebranded (Cognition, Dec 2025) and Factory raised at $1.5B (Apr 2026); any third-party tool the app hard-depends on can be acquired, repriced, or shut down. Mitigate with open protocols (MCP/ACP) and an OSS fallback.

Reliability of background/scheduled runs: long-horizon autonomous agents (Replit 200-min, Devin parallel sessions) are prone to drift, runaway subagent spawning, and silent failure; scheduled jobs that fail unattended (especially when a user's quota is exhausted) erode trust — needs robust monitoring, retries, and failure notifications to mobile/messaging.

Fast-moving / uncertain data: model IDs, benchmark scores, and prices in this report are a mid-2026 snapshot (e.g., Claude Fable 5 just went GA June 9 2026 at $10/$50; Composer 2.5 May 18 2026). Re-verify pricing and model availability before any pricing or budgeting feature ships.

Security/data exposure: running agents with shell access in users' local environments (Factory Bridge-style) and across mobile/P2P widens the attack surface; un-sandboxed background agents with repo + secret access are a material supply-chain and exfiltration risk.

summary: As of mid-2026, the Claude Agent SDK (renamed from "Claude Code SDK" on Sept 29, 2025) is Anthropic's official library for embedding the exact agent harness that powers Claude Code into your own apps, in TypeScript (@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk, v0.3.170, Node 18+, bundles a native Claude Code binary per platform) and Python (claude-agent-sdk, Python 3.10+). It gives you the full agent loop out of the box: built-in tools (Read/Write/Edit/Bash/Glob/Grep/WebSearch/WebFetch/Monitor/AskUserQuestion), programmatic subagents, lifecycle hooks, MCP client support (stdio/SSE/HTTP/in-process), filesystem-loaded Agent Skills, sessions (resume/fork), six permission modes including plan mode, and a streaming-input mode. The flagship model is Claude Opus 4.8 (claude-opus-4-8, released May 28 2026, $5/$25 per MTok, 1M context); Sonnet 4.6 ($3/$15) and Haiku 4.5 ($1/$5) round out the lineup. Two structural facts dominate any "Claude Code as primary engine" decision: (1) Anthropic explicitly forbids third-party products from using claude.ai/Pro/Max OAuth login or rate limits — the SDK requires API-key auth — and has actively banned tools (OpenClaw, OpenCode, Roo Code, Goose) that did so since Jan 2026; and (2) starting June 15, 2026 even legitimate subscription-based Agent SDK/`claude -p` usage draws from a separate, non-rollover monthly "Agent SDK credit" ($20 Pro / $100 Max5x / $200 Max20x), not interactive limits. For a commercial multi-tenant platform, the only compliant path is per-user/BYO API keys (or Bedrock/Vertex/Azure/Claude-Platform-on-AWS), governed by Anthropic's Commercial Terms.

keyFindings.point: The SDK was renamed Claude Code SDK -> Claude Agent SDK on Sept 29, 2025; package names and system-prompt defaults changed
keyFindings.detail: TypeScript @anthropic-ai/claude-code -> @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk; Python claude-code-sdk -> claude-agent-sdk. In v0.1.0 the default system prompt changed (no longer ships the Claude Code system prompt by default — you opt in via systemPrompt/setting sources). A migration guide exists. Same harness now used internally by Anthropic for research, data synthesis, image/video generation, not just coding.

keyFindings.point: CRITICAL ToS: third-party products may NOT use claude.ai/Pro/Max OAuth login or subscription rate limits; SDK requires API-key auth
keyFindings.detail: Official docs state verbatim: 'Unless previously approved, Anthropic does not allow third party developers to offer claude.ai login or rate limits for their products, including agents built on the Claude Agent SDK. Please use the API key authentication methods.' Using OAuth tokens (sk-ant-oat01- prefix) from Free/Pro/Max in any other product 'constitutes a violation of the Consumer Terms of Service.' Enforced technically since ~Jan 9, 2026.

keyFindings.point: Anthropic actively banned third-party harnesses that smuggled subscription OAuth tokens in 2026
keyFindings.detail: Tools including OpenClaw (formerly Clawdbot), OpenCode, Roo Code, and Goose had consumer OAuth tokens blocked. Anthropic reserves the right to suspend accounts without prior notice. This is a direct ban-risk precedent for any platform routing Claude Code through consumer subscriptions.

keyFindings.point: Billing change June 15, 2026: subscription Agent SDK + `claude -p` usage moves to a separate monthly Agent SDK credit pool (non-rollover)
keyFindings.detail: Credits: Pro $20, Max 5x $100, Max 20x $200, Team standard seat $20 / premium seat $100, Enterprise usage-based $20 / premium-seat $200. Covers your own Python/TS Agent SDK projects, `claude -p`, and third-party apps that authenticate with the user's subscription. When exhausted, usage flows to usage-credits at standard API rates only if enabled. Unused credits do NOT roll over. Interactive Claude Code (terminal/IDE) and Claude.ai chat are unaffected. API-key accounts get no credit (pay-as-you-go as before).

keyFindings.point: Current model lineup + pricing (mid-2026): Opus 4.8 is flagship at $5/$25 per MTok with 1M context
keyFindings.detail: claude-opus-4-8 (released May 28 2026) $5 in/$25 out; claude-opus-4-7 and claude-opus-4-6 same $5/$25; claude-sonnet-4-6 $3/$15; claude-haiku-4-5 $1/$5. Opus 4.7+ use a new tokenizer that can use up to 35% more tokens for the same text. Opus 4.8/4.7/4.6 + Sonnet 4.6 include the full 1M-token context at standard pricing. Prompt caching: 5m write 1.25x, 1h write 2x, cache-read hit 0.1x base input. Batch API: -50%. Fable 5 / Mythos 5 are higher-tier ($10/$50). Opus 4.1/4 deprecated; Haiku 3.5 retired except Bedrock/Vertex.

keyFindings.point: Six permission modes including plan mode; modes settable at query time AND dynamically mid-session
keyFindings.detail: Modes: default (unmatched tools -> canUseTool callback), dontAsk (deny instead of prompting — best for locked-down headless agents), acceptEdits (auto-approve file edits + fs ops mkdir/rm/mv/cp/sed inside cwd), bypassPermissions (approve everything except deny/ask-rule/hook blocks — dangerous, subagents inherit it), plan (read-only exploration; file edits never auto-approved, always route to canUseTool; can use AskUserQuestion), and auto (TypeScript-only, model-classifier approves/denies each call). Change live via setPermissionMode()/set_permission_mode(). Eval order: hooks -> deny rules -> ask rules -> mode -> allow rules -> canUseTool.

keyFindings.point: Subagents are defined programmatically (AgentDefinition) and invoked via the Agent tool; isolate context and run in parallel
keyFindings.detail: Pass agents={name: {description, prompt, tools, model?}}; Claude auto-delegates based on the description. Include 'Agent' in allowedTools to auto-approve invocations. Messages carry parent_tool_use_id so you can attribute streamed output to a specific subagent. WARNING: if parent uses bypassPermissions/acceptEdits/auto, all subagents inherit it and it cannot be overridden per subagent.

keyFindings.point: Hooks run your code at lifecycle events for deterministic control (validate/log/block/transform)
keyFindings.detail: Events: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, SessionStart, SessionEnd, UserPromptSubmit, and more. SDK hooks are in-process callbacks (Python/TS functions) registered via options.hooks with HookMatcher (e.g. matcher='Edit|Write'). Shell-command hooks from settings.json only load if you enable settingSources. A PreToolUse hook can deny outright; hooks run BEFORE deny/ask rules and the permission mode.

keyFindings.point: MCP integration supports four transports including fast in-process SDK tools
keyFindings.detail: mcpServers config accepts stdio (subprocess, most common local), SSE and HTTP (remote/cloud servers), and in-process SDK MCP servers (createSdkMcpServer / tool() in TS; @tool in Python) that run as direct function calls with a single control-protocol round trip — faster than subprocess MCP. Also reads .mcp.json. Hundreds of community servers (modelcontextprotocol/servers). Deny rule mcp__* blocks all MCP tools; allow rules need a literal mcp__<server>__ prefix (server segment cannot be a glob).

keyFindings.point: Agent Skills are filesystem-loaded (SKILL.md), model-invoked, NOT programmatically registrable
keyFindings.detail: Skills live in .claude/skills/*/SKILL.md (project), ~/.claude/skills/ (user), or via plugins. SKILL.md = YAML frontmatter (name, description) + Markdown body + optional bundled scripts/resources; progressive disclosure (metadata at startup, full body on trigger). Loaded only when settingSources includes 'user'/'project' or via the plugins option. Control with skills option: 'all', a name list, or [] (the SDK auto-adds the 'Skill' tool to allowedTools). NOTE: SKILL.md allowed-tools frontmatter is IGNORED by the SDK (CLI-only) — gate tools via allowedTools instead. Anthropic open-sourced ~17 skills at github.com/anthropics/skills with a marketplace.json plugin manifest.

keyFindings.point: Sessions: capture session_id from the init SystemMessage, then resume or fork for branching
keyFindings.detail: Session state is JSONL on your filesystem (vs Anthropic-hosted for Managed Agents). resume=session_id continues with full context; forking lets you branch to explore alternatives (fork is V1-only, not yet in V2 preview). Enables per-project/per-conversation persistence — directly maps to a 'per-project' platform model.

keyFindings.point: Background/event-driven work via the Monitor tool (shipped Apr 9, 2026) and run_in_background
keyFindings.detail: run_in_background is fire-and-forget (one completion notification). Monitor launches a shell command whose stdout becomes an event stream — each matching line wakes the main session as an interrupt (process completion, specific output, errors, timeouts), costing nothing while silent. Enables event-driven agents (build watchers, schedulers, long-running tasks). Streaming input mode via includePartialMessages/include_partial_messages for incremental token/tool-call updates.

keyFindings.point: Managed Agents = hosted alternative: Anthropic runs the agent + sandbox; billed $0.08/session-hour + standard tokens
keyFindings.detail: REST API (public beta). Per-session managed sandbox, Anthropic-hosted event log. Runtime metered to the millisecond, only while status='running' (idle/rescheduling/terminated are free). Batch discount, Fast mode premium, and data-residency multiplier do NOT apply. Good for long-running/async production agents without you operating sandboxes. Agent SDK is the local/in-your-process counterpart; common path = prototype on SDK, productionize on Managed Agents.

keyFindings.point: Output styles deprecated; TypeScript V2 session API is an unstable preview and partly already deprecated
keyFindings.detail: Standalone /output-style command deprecated v2.1.73, removed v2.1.91 — migrate to plugins/subagents/SessionStart hooks or set outputStyle in settings. The V2 TS interface (unstable_v2_createSession/resumeSession + send()/stream()) removes async-generator juggling for multi-turn, but docs warn V2 session functions are deprecated and will be removed; Anthropic currently recommends the V1 query() API. Build on V1 query() for stability.

keyFindings.point: Multi-provider auth: API key, Bedrock, Vertex AI, Azure Foundry, Claude Platform on AWS
keyFindings.detail: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (default); CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK=1; CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX=1; CLAUDE_CODE_USE_FOUNDRY=1; CLAUDE_CODE_USE_ANTHROPIC_AWS=1 + ANTHROPIC_AWS_WORKSPACE_ID. Claude Platform on AWS bills in Claude Consumption Units (100 CCU = $1). If both API key and subscription exist, API key takes precedence (unset ANTHROPIC_API_KEY to fall back). These cloud routes are the enterprise-compliant way to scale without consumer-subscription ban risk.

keyFindings.point: Branding restrictions for partners embedding the SDK
keyFindings.detail: Allowed: 'Claude Agent' (preferred for menus), 'Claude' (inside an Agents menu), '{YourAgentName} Powered by Claude'. NOT permitted: 'Claude Code'/'Claude Code Agent', or Claude Code ASCII art / visual elements that mimic Claude Code. Your product must keep its own branding and not appear to be an Anthropic product.

itemsOrTools.name: @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk (TypeScript)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Official TS library wrapping the Claude Code harness: query() async-generator API, streaming, built-in tools, subagents, hooks, MCP, permissions, sessions. v0.3.170 (Jun 2026), Node 18+. Bundles a native Claude Code binary per platform as an optional dependency — no separate Claude Code install needed.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Primary engine for an Electron/Node desktop app. The bundled binary + in-process model make it the natural fit for a TS-based desktop shell; query() runs the full agent loop inside your process so you control files, tools, and UI streaming.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: npm install @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk. OSS (MIT-style repo). Usage billed via your chosen auth (API key / cloud). github.com/anthropics/claude-agent-sdk-typescript

itemsOrTools.name: claude-agent-sdk (Python)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Official Python library: query() one-shot generator and ClaudeSDKClient for multi-turn + custom in-process tools (@tool) and hooks. Python 3.10+. Same capability surface as TS.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Use for the backend/server tier, schedulers, and a Python worker fleet (per-project scheduler, WhatsApp/Telegram bridges, image/video pipelines orchestrated as tools/subagents). ClaudeSDKClient enables long-lived sessions + custom tools.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: pip install claude-agent-sdk. OSS. github.com/anthropics/claude-agent-sdk-python

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Opus 4.8 (claude-opus-4-8)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Flagship coding/agent model, released May 28 2026. 1M-token context at standard pricing. New tokenizer (from 4.7) for better task performance. Fast-mode option ($10/$50) in research preview.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The 'primary coding/agent engine' model. Use for hard coding, planning, and multi-step agent runs. 1M context suits large-repo and long-session work.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: $5 / MTok input, $25 / MTok output. Cache read 0.5/MTok. Batch $2.50/$12.50. Via Claude API / Bedrock / Vertex / Azure / Claude Platform on AWS.

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Sonnet 4.6 (claude-sonnet-4-6)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Mid-tier model, 1M context at standard pricing. Strong general agent workhorse at lower cost than Opus.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Default for most production agent turns to control cost; reserve Opus 4.8 for the hardest steps. Good per-project default model in the scheduler.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: $3 / MTok input, $15 / MTok output. Batch $1.50/$7.50.

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Haiku 4.5 (claude-haiku-4-5)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Fast, cheap small model.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Use for cheap subagents, classification, routing, hook-side checks, log summarization, and high-volume comms (WhatsApp/Telegram auto-replies). ~$37 per 10k support-style conversations.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: $1 / MTok input, $5 / MTok output. Batch $0.50/$2.50.

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Managed Agents (REST API)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Hosted agent runtime — Anthropic runs the agent loop AND a per-session sandbox, you POST events and stream results. Anthropic-hosted event log; supports long-running/async sessions.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Alternative to running the SDK in your own infra for scale/reliability — offloads sandbox + session ops. Consider for the mobile/cloud tier where you don't want to operate sandboxes per user. Note: custom tools work by Claude triggering and you executing/returning (not arbitrary in-process funcs).
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Public beta. $0.08 per session-hour (running status only) + standard token rates. No batch/fast-mode/data-residency modifiers. No dedicated trial (standard free credits).

itemsOrTools.name: Agent Skills + anthropics/skills marketplace
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Folders of instructions/scripts (SKILL.md + YAML frontmatter) Claude loads on demand. Anthropic open-sourced ~17 skills with a .claude-plugin/marketplace.json plugin manifest; third-party marketplaces exist (skillsmp.com, agent-skills.cc, lobehub).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Direct foundation for your 'dynamic skills marketplace'. Skills are just versioned folders — distribute via your own plugin marketplace, install into .claude/skills or via the plugins option, enable per-session with the skills option. Caveat: SDK loads skills from filesystem only (no programmatic registration) and ignores SKILL.md allowed-tools.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS. github.com/anthropics/skills. Distribute via Claude Code plugin marketplaces (marketplace.json).

itemsOrTools.name: MCP (Model Context Protocol) in the SDK
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Connect external systems via stdio/SSE/HTTP or in-process SDK MCP servers. Hundreds of community servers; in-process tools avoid subprocess overhead.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Backbone for integrations: WhatsApp/Telegram bridges, browser automation (Playwright MCP), image/video studio backends, databases, and your own product APIs all plug in as MCP servers or in-process tools. GPT review-model can be wrapped as a custom in-process tool/subagent.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS protocol. Servers configured via mcpServers/.mcp.json. github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers

itemsOrTools.name: Monitor tool / run_in_background
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Monitor (shipped Apr 9 2026) turns a background shell command's stdout into an event stream that interrupts the agent on matching lines; run_in_background is fire-and-forget with a single completion notice.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Enables the per-project scheduler and long-running/background tasks (builds, deploys, video renders) to wake the agent event-driven instead of polling — lower cost and latency.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Built into the SDK/CLI; billed as normal token usage.

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Platform on AWS / Bedrock / Vertex / Azure Foundry
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Enterprise inference routes for the SDK via env flags (CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK/VERTEX/FOUNDRY/ANTHROPIC_AWS). Claude Platform on AWS bills in CCUs (100 CCU = $1) through AWS Marketplace.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Compliant, scalable, ban-risk-free way to power the platform at volume without consumer subscriptions — and gives data-residency / enterprise-procurement options for B2B customers.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Standard per-model token rates (CCU conversion on AWS). Regional/multi-region endpoints +10%; first-party inference_geo='us' +1.1x on 4.6+ models.

itemsOrTools.name: claude-agent-sdk-starter (Electron) + CodePilot
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Community references: vanzan01/claude-agent-sdk-starter is a clean Electron + official-SDK starter; op7418/CodePilot is a multi-model Electron+Next.js agent desktop client with MCP, skills, and phone control.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Concrete blueprints for embedding the SDK in an Electron desktop app with mobile control — close to your target architecture. Study their process model (main-process SDK + IPC streaming to renderer). Note the starter's 'use your Pro/Max subscription, no API key' path is for PERSONAL dev only and is non-compliant for a shipped product.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS on GitHub. github.com/vanzan01/claude-agent-sdk-starter , github.com/op7418/CodePilot

technicalOptions.option: Run Agent SDK in-process (TS in Electron main / Python worker) with per-user API keys
technicalOptions.pros: Full control of files, tools, UI streaming; lowest latency; in-process MCP tools; sessions as local JSONL; works offline-ish; cheapest at the token layer. Compliant under Commercial Terms when each user supplies/owns billing.
technicalOptions.cons: You operate sandboxing, isolation, secrets, and scaling. Per-user API key onboarding friction. You bear abuse/runaway-cost risk; need tight permission modes + hooks. Bundled binary adds platform-specific packaging work.

technicalOptions.option: Use Claude Managed Agents (hosted) for the cloud/mobile tier
technicalOptions.pros: Anthropic runs the agent loop + per-session sandbox + event log; great for long-running/async; offloads infra and isolation; predictable $0.08/session-hour + tokens.
technicalOptions.cons: Public beta (maturity/limits risk); custom tools are execute-and-return only (no arbitrary in-process funcs); no batch/fast-mode/residency discounts; less control over the filesystem; another bill dimension (session-hours).

technicalOptions.option: Route inference via Bedrock/Vertex/Azure/Claude Platform on AWS instead of first-party API
technicalOptions.pros: Enterprise procurement, data residency, existing cloud commits/credits; avoids consumer-subscription ban risk entirely; CCU/marketplace billing simplifies B2B.
technicalOptions.cons: Regional endpoints +10%; CCU indirection on AWS; slightly more setup; model availability/version lag can occur on partner clouds; postpaid-only on Claude Platform on AWS.

technicalOptions.option: Let end-users authenticate with their own Claude Pro/Max subscription via the new Agent SDK credit
technicalOptions.pros: Zero per-token cost to you; users already pay Anthropic; June-15 credits ($20/$100/$200) cover third-party apps that authenticate with the user's subscription.
technicalOptions.cons: HIGH RISK: doing this via extracted OAuth tokens is an explicit ToS violation and has triggered bans (OpenClaw/OpenCode/Roo/Goose). Only viable if Anthropic provides/approves an official subscription-auth flow for your app ('unless previously approved'). Credits are tiny, non-rollover, and shared with the user's own `claude -p` usage; unreliable as a platform foundation.

technicalOptions.option: Build on V1 query() now vs adopt the V2 TypeScript session preview
technicalOptions.pros: V2 send()/stream() simplifies multi-turn (no async-generator juggling) and inter-turn logic.
technicalOptions.cons: V2 session API is an unstable preview AND already flagged deprecated/for-removal; lacks features like forking; Anthropic recommends V1 query() for stability. Build on V1 query() and isolate the call site behind your own adapter.

recommendations: Make Claude Opus 4.8 (claude-opus-4-8) the primary engine for hard coding/planning, Sonnet 4.6 the default for routine agent turns, and Haiku 4.5 for cheap subagents/classification/comms. Drive model choice per-step and per-project in your scheduler to control cost; reserve Opus for the steps that need it.

For a SHIPPED commercial product, do NOT route through claude.ai/Pro/Max OAuth. Standardize on API-key auth (BYO key per user, or your own org keys), and offer Bedrock/Vertex/Azure/Claude Platform on AWS for enterprise customers. This is the only ToS-compliant, ban-safe foundation. Treat any 'use your subscription' starter code as personal-dev-only.

Architect the desktop app as: Agent SDK (TS) running in the Electron MAIN process, streaming messages over IPC to the renderer; per-project working dirs each with their own .claude/ (skills, CLAUDE.md, settings.json) and JSONL session files. Use sessions resume/fork to give every project persistent, branchable context.

Lock down autonomy with layered controls: start projects in plan mode for review, use dontAsk + a tight allowedTools list for unattended/scheduled runs, acceptEdits only inside an isolated project dir, and NEVER ship bypassPermissions to end users. Add PreToolUse hooks to block dangerous Bash (rm/curl/network) and PostToolUse hooks for audit logging. Remember subagents inherit bypassPermissions/acceptEdits/auto.

Wrap the GPT review-model as an in-process MCP tool (or a 'reviewer' subagent backed by a custom tool that calls OpenAI), so Claude can delegate 'get a second opinion' steps. Wrap WhatsApp/Telegram, the image/video studio, and browser automation (Playwright MCP) as MCP servers / in-process tools too — this keeps the agent loop uniform.

Build the skills marketplace on the Agent Skills + plugin format (SKILL.md + .claude-plugin/marketplace.json). Distribute skills as versioned folders, install into per-project .claude/skills or via the plugins option, and enable per-session with the skills option. Account for the SDK's constraints: skills are filesystem-only (no programmatic registration) and SKILL.md allowed-tools is ignored — enforce tool scoping via allowedTools + permission mode instead.

Use the Monitor tool (event-driven) rather than polling for the per-project scheduler, builds, and long renders; reserve run_in_background for simple fire-and-forget jobs. Turn on includePartialMessages for smooth token-by-token UI streaming.

Pin SDK versions and build on the stable V1 query() API behind your own thin adapter; avoid the V2 session preview (unstable + already deprecated). Watch the TS/Python CHANGELOGs (135+ TS releases; fast cadence). Migrate output-style customizations to plugins/subagents/SessionStart hooks since /output-style was removed in v2.1.91.

Model the June-15-2026 cost reality explicitly: API-key accounts pay pay-as-you-go (no credit), subscription Agent SDK credits are small/non-rollover and unsuitable as a platform base. Add prompt caching everywhere (cache-read = 0.1x input; CLAUDE.md/system prompts/tool defs are ideal cache targets) and Batch API (-50%) for non-interactive jobs to cut cost. Build per-user cost metering + spend caps to prevent runaway agent loops.

Respect Anthropic's partner branding rules: present the engine as 'Powered by Claude' / 'Claude Agent', keep your own product branding, and avoid 'Claude Code' naming or Claude-Code-mimicking visuals. Govern everything under Anthropic's Commercial Terms of Service.
risks: ToS / ban risk (HIGH): routing your product through claude.ai/Pro/Max OAuth or subscription rate limits is explicitly prohibited and has caused real bans (OpenClaw, OpenCode, Roo Code, Goose) since Jan 2026. Anthropic enforces technically and can suspend accounts without notice. Compliant path = API-key/cloud auth only, or explicit prior approval from Anthropic.

Billing volatility (HIGH/changing fast): the June 15, 2026 Agent SDK credit change reprices subscription-based agent usage; credits are tiny, non-rollover, and shared with the user's own `claude -p`. Any cost model that assumed 'flat-rate subscription powers our agent' breaks. Pricing/policy is moving quickly — re-verify before launch.

Runaway cost (HIGH for autonomous agents): unattended Opus 4.8 loops at $5/$25 per MTok plus session/runtime can spike spend. Opus 4.7+ tokenizer can use up to 35% more tokens for the same text. Mandatory: spend caps, model downgrades, prompt caching, batching, and hook-based loop guards.

Vendor lock-in (MEDIUM-HIGH): deep reliance on the Claude Code harness, .claude/ config, skills format, and Anthropic models. Mitigate by abstracting the agent interface (so you can swap to Managed Agents, cloud routes, or even a different harness), keeping the GPT review-model integration provider-neutral, and storing sessions/skills in portable formats.

API instability (MEDIUM): TS SDK at v0.3.x with 135+ releases and a fast cadence; V2 session preview is unstable and already deprecated; output-style removed; defaults changed at the rename. Pin versions, build on V1 query() behind an adapter, and track CHANGELOGs.

Security / autonomy (HIGH): bypassPermissions grants full system access and subagents inherit it; acceptEdits can touch the filesystem; Bash can exfiltrate via network. For a multi-user desktop+mobile product this is a serious attack surface — require sandboxed per-project dirs, deny-rules for network/destructive Bash, PreToolUse hook gating, and never expose bypass mode to users.

Maturity risk (MEDIUM): Managed Agents is public beta; Monitor tool is recent (Apr 2026); Agent Skills SDK loading has rough edges (filesystem-only, ignored allowed-tools). Validate each before depending on it in production.

Mobile/P2P gap (MEDIUM): the SDK is Node/Python and ships a desktop binary — it does not run natively on iOS/Android. The mobile tier must call a server-side SDK/Managed-Agents backend (or thin client + cloud), which adds infra, latency, and per-session cost; plan the P2P/mobile layer around a hosted agent backend rather than on-device execution.

summary: As of mid-2026, OpenAI ships three distinct-but-related agent stacks: (1) Codex CLI + Codex SDK (a coding agent that runs in your terminal, with first-class headless `codex exec --json` JSONL automation and TypeScript `@openai/codex-sdk` / Python `openai-codex` wrappers); (2) the OpenAI Agents SDK (`openai-agents` for Python, `@openai/agents` for TS/JS) for multi-agent orchestration with handoffs, guardrails, sessions, tracing, voice/Realtime agents, and a now-GA Temporal durable-execution integration; and (3) the GPT-5.x model line, where GPT-5.5 (released Apr 23, 2026; API Apr 24) is the current flagship and the default model inside Codex. GPT-5.5 is explicitly tuned for agentic coding, computer use, and code review, supports reasoning_effort of none/low/medium(default)/high/xhigh, has a ~1.05M-token context window, and is priced at $5/$30 per 1M input/output tokens ($0.50 cached). Codex authentication splits sharply: ChatGPT OAuth sign-in (subscription-metered, full workspace/cloud features) vs API key (usage-based billing, recommended for CI/CD and programmatic/headless use, but some cloud/workspace features unavailable). For a universal agent platform with Claude Code as the primary engine, GPT-5.5 (or GPT-5.4 for cost) accessed via the regular OpenAI Responses/Chat Completions API with an API key is the clean, ToS-safe choice for a code-REVIEW model running alongside Claude; do NOT automate ChatGPT OAuth subscription auth (documented ban risk + ToS tension). This space is moving extremely fast — model IDs (GPT-5.6 already teased), Codex defaults, and deprecations change roughly monthly, so pin versions and treat model routing as config.

keyFindings.point: GPT-5.5 is the current OpenAI flagship and the default Codex model
keyFindings.detail: Released Apr 23, 2026 (ChatGPT) / Apr 24 (API). Model ID `gpt-5.5`; also `gpt-5.5-pro` for research-grade reasoning. ~1,050,000-token context, 128,000 max output, knowledge cutoff Dec 1, 2025. Tuned for agentic coding, computer use, long-horizon tool use, error recovery. It is the recommended default in Codex CLI/SDK; there is NO separate 'gpt-5.5-codex' variant (GPT-5.5 itself serves that role). GPT-5.6 is already teased for mid-2026.

keyFindings.point: reasoning_effort is the key quality/cost/latency dial and varies by model
keyFindings.detail: GPT-5.5 supports none, low, medium (default), high, xhigh. GPT-5.2/5.2-Pro added xhigh; GPT-5.1 introduced 'none'; original GPT-5 had minimal/low/medium/high. Guidance: low=efficient, medium=balanced default, high=hard agentic reasoning where latency matters less, xhigh=hardest async tasks/evals. For a code-review model, medium or high is the sweet spot; xhigh for deep audits.

keyFindings.point: Codex has true headless automation via `codex exec --json` (JSONL event stream)
keyFindings.detail: `codex exec "task"` runs non-interactively to completion. `--json` emits JSONL events: thread.started, turn.started/completed/failed, item.started/item.completed, error; item types include agent_message, reasoning, command exec, file changes, MCP tool calls, web searches, plan updates. `--output-schema schema.json` + `-o out.json` for structured output. `--output-last-message`/`-o` saves final message. Resume via `codex exec resume --last` or `resume <SESSION_ID>`. Sandbox flags: `--sandbox read-only|workspace-write|danger-full-access`; `--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox` for isolated runners only. `--skip-git-repo-check`, `--ephemeral`. stdin piping supported (e.g. `npm test 2>&1 | codex exec "summarize failures"`).

keyFindings.point: Codex SDK wraps the CLI as a child process; TS and Python both available
keyFindings.detail: TypeScript `@openai/codex-sdk` (Node 18+, latest ~0.137.0 mid-2026) and Python `openai-codex` (3.10+). TS: `new Codex({env, baseUrl, config})` -> `codex.startThread({workingDirectory, skipGitRepoCheck})` -> `thread.run(input, {outputSchema})` or `thread.runStreamed()` returning async `events`. `codex.resumeThread(threadId)` (sessions persist in ~/.codex/sessions). Input can be text or `{type:'local_image', path}`. SDK injects CODEX_API_KEY into the spawned CLI env. Python adds explicit sandbox presets: read_only / workspace_write / full_access. The SDK spawns `codex exec --json` and parses JSONL into ThreadEvent objects.

keyFindings.point: Auth split: ChatGPT OAuth (subscription) vs API key (usage-based) — they behave very differently
keyFindings.detail: Codex supports browser ChatGPT OAuth, device-code (`codex login --device-auth`) for headless, and API key (OpenAI dashboard). OpenAI explicitly recommends API key auth for programmatic/CI-CD workflows. API key = standard API pricing + API rate limits, but some ChatGPT-workspace/cloud features (Codex Cloud) are unavailable. ChatGPT sign-in = metered against Plus/Pro/Business/Enterprise plan quotas, full workspace RBAC/retention/cloud. `CODEX_API_KEY` env var is ONLY honored by `codex exec`. Enterprise admins can mint dedicated Codex access tokens for non-interactive workflows (distinct from platform API keys).

keyFindings.point: Automating ChatGPT-subscription OAuth carries real ToS and ban risk
keyFindings.detail: OpenAI ToS restricts automated/programmatic extraction; using ChatGPT-plan OAuth for headless automation sits in legal gray area and OpenAI has NOT explicitly blessed third-party automation under a ChatGPT plan. Documented cases in 2026 of Pro accounts banned without warning, plus inconsistent billing (API credits drained despite Plus/Pro login). For a product, this is a hard avoid — use platform API keys with usage-based billing instead.

keyFindings.point: GPT-5.5 is a strong code-review model and benchmarks competitively but Claude still leads on some review axes
keyFindings.detail: On a curated review benchmark GPT-5.5 hit 79.2% expected-issues-found (vs 58.3% baseline) and precision 40.6% (vs 27.9%). CodeRabbit reports shorter, more selective reviews biased toward small workable changes. OpenAI claims 82.7% Terminal-Bench 2.0, beating Claude Opus 4.7 and Gemini 3.1 Pro on those benches. However Claude Opus 4.7 still leads SWE-bench Pro by ~6 pts with lower hallucination; consensus: Opus safer for pure review/refactor, GPT-5.5 wins agentic tool-loops. This validates a dual-model design: Claude primary, GPT-5.5 as independent reviewer.

keyFindings.point: OpenAI Agents SDK is the orchestration layer with cross-provider support via LiteLLM/Any-LLM (beta)
keyFindings.detail: `openai-agents` (Python, ~v0.6+ mid-2026) and `@openai/agents` (TS, needs Zod v4). Primitives: Agents (instructions+tools+guardrails+handoffs), handoffs (full conversation transfer) vs agent-as-tool (parent keeps control), input/output guardrails (run in parallel, fail fast, can use a cheap model), Sessions (auto history), Tracing, Realtime/voice agents (RealtimeAgent/RealtimeSession via `@openai/agents-realtime`). Non-OpenAI models (incl. Anthropic Claude) via `openai-agents[litellm]` adapter — best-effort/beta; some providers don't populate usage metrics. Temporal durable-execution integration GA since Mar 23, 2026 for crash-resilient long-running workflows.

keyFindings.point: GPT-5.5 pricing and the broader Codex model lineup
keyFindings.detail: gpt-5.5: $5 input / $0.50 cached / $30 output per 1M; Batch API flat 50% off. gpt-5.5-pro: $30/$180 per 1M. Codex model picker (mid-2026): gpt-5.5 (default), gpt-5.4 (flagship, cheaper), gpt-5.4-mini (fast/cheap, good for subagents), gpt-5.3-codex-spark (text-only research preview, ChatGPT Pro only). gpt-5.2 and gpt-5.3-codex are DEPRECATED for ChatGPT sign-in (may persist via API key). Monthly deprecation cadence — pin model IDs.

itemsOrTools.name: Codex CLI (codex exec)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Terminal coding agent; `codex exec` runs headless to completion with `--json` JSONL event streaming, structured output via --output-schema, sandbox levels (read-only/workspace-write/danger-full-access), session resume, and stdin piping. Default model gpt-5.5.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Ideal as a scriptable secondary 'doer'/reviewer engine you can shell out to from the platform backend or per-project scheduler. JSONL stream maps cleanly onto a job/event model; structured-output schema lets you get machine-parseable review verdicts.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS (Apache-2.0) at github.com/openai/codex; install via npm/brew. Auth: API key (usage-based, recommended for automation) or ChatGPT OAuth (subscription). https://developers.openai.com/codex/cli

itemsOrTools.name: Codex SDK (@openai/codex-sdk / openai-codex)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Programmatic wrapper around the Codex CLI. startThread/run/runStreamed, resumeThread, image inputs, outputSchema, sandbox presets. Spawns `codex exec --json` and parses events into typed ThreadEvent objects.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Cleaner than shelling out raw: embed a GPT-driven code-review or codegen agent inside your Node/Python backend with typed events for progress UI (mobile + desktop). Sessions persistence aligns with per-project context.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS. npm i @openai/codex-sdk (Node 18+) or pip install openai-codex (3.10+). Same API-key/OAuth auth as CLI; CODEX_API_KEY injected into child. https://developers.openai.com/codex/sdk

itemsOrTools.name: GPT-5.5 (model gpt-5.5)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: OpenAI flagship agentic-coding model. ~1.05M context, 128K output, cutoff Dec 2025. reasoning_effort none/low/medium/high/xhigh. Text+image in, text out. Responses API + Chat Completions + tool calling + streaming.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Primary candidate for the GPT REVIEW model alongside Claude Code. Strong, independent second opinion on diffs; large context fits whole-PR review. Use medium/high effort for reviews, xhigh for deep audits.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: API key via api.openai.com. $5/$30 per 1M (in/out), $0.50 cached, Batch 50% off. Available all paid API tiers + ChatGPT Plus/Pro/Business/Enterprise. https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.5

itemsOrTools.name: GPT-5.4 / gpt-5.4-mini
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Prior-gen flagship (gpt-5.4) and a fast cheap mini (gpt-5.4-mini) for responsive tasks and subagents. Both in Codex picker and as API models.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Cost-control tier: use gpt-5.4-mini for cheap pre-screens, lint-style reviews, or high-volume subagent fan-out in the scheduler; reserve gpt-5.5 for the final reviewer pass. Reduces per-review cost materially.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: API key. Cheaper than gpt-5.5 (mini lowest). ChatGPT sign-in or API key. https://developers.openai.com/codex/models

itemsOrTools.name: OpenAI Agents SDK (Python: openai-agents)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Multi-agent orchestration: Agents with tools/guardrails/handoffs, Sessions (auto history), Tracing, input/output guardrails (parallel, fail-fast, cheap-model checks), Realtime voice agents. LiteLLM/Any-LLM adapters for non-OpenAI models (beta).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Reference architecture for your orchestration layer if you stay in Python; guardrails pattern (cheap model gates expensive one) is directly reusable. LiteLLM adapter lets one codebase route Claude (primary) + GPT (review) — though Claude Agent SDK may remain your primary engine.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS (MIT). pip install openai-agents; [litellm] extra for cross-provider. https://openai.github.io/openai-agents-python/

itemsOrTools.name: OpenAI Agents SDK (TS/JS: @openai/agents)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: TypeScript port with same primitives plus @openai/agents-realtime (RealtimeAgent/RealtimeSession) for browser/server voice agents with interruption detection, guardrails, handoffs. Requires Zod v4.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Directly fits a Node/Electron desktop + React Native/mobile platform. Realtime agents enable voice control of agents on mobile; handoffs model your Claude->GPT-review delegation in-process.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS. npm i @openai/agents (+ @openai/agents-realtime). https://openai.github.io/openai-agents-js/

itemsOrTools.name: Codex GitHub Action (openai/codex-action)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Wraps Codex CLI for CI: inputs for model, reasoning effort, sandbox (read-only/workspace-write/danger-full-access), prompt; outputs final-message and output-file (diff/artifact). Limits OPENAI_API_KEY exposure vs raw CLI.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Blueprint for your per-project scheduler's CI integration and for an automated GPT review-on-PR bot that complements Claude. Two-job pattern (read-only review -> apply patch with write) is a safe sandboxing model to copy.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS. github.com/openai/codex-action; needs OPENAI_API_KEY secret (usage-based). https://developers.openai.com/codex/github-action

itemsOrTools.name: Temporal + Agents SDK durable execution
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: GA (Mar 23, 2026) integration running each agent invocation as a Temporal Activity inside a Workflow: automatic retries, rate-limit handling, crash resume, human-in-the-loop for long-running agents.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Strong fit for your per-project scheduler and long-horizon agent jobs that must survive process/device restarts (esp. mobile/P2P unreliability). Gives durability without hand-rolling a job queue.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS SDK + Temporal (self-host OSS or Temporal Cloud, usage-based). https://temporal.io/blog/announcing-openai-agents-sdk-integration

itemsOrTools.name: LiteLLM (cross-provider gateway)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Translates between provider formats; lets Agents SDK / Claude Agent SDK route to OpenAI, Anthropic, Bedrock, Azure, Vertex, etc., behind one API. Supports Anthropic Messages format for Claude Code.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Key to your cross-provider pattern: a single gateway to run Claude as primary and GPT-5.5 as reviewer with unified auth/billing/observability, and to fail over between providers. Caveat: usage metrics not always populated; adapter is beta in Agents SDK.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS (self-host) + optional managed. https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/tutorials/claude_non_anthropic_models

technicalOptions.option: GPT review via raw OpenAI API (Responses/Chat Completions, gpt-5.5, API key)
technicalOptions.pros: Cleanest ToS posture; usage-based billing with predictable per-token cost; full control over reasoning_effort and prompts; 1.05M context fits whole PRs; no CLI/child-process overhead; stable, documented, supports streaming + structured outputs + tool calling.
technicalOptions.cons: You build the review harness (diff extraction, prompt, parsing) yourself; no built-in agentic file/tool loop like Codex; another SDK to maintain alongside Claude.

technicalOptions.option: GPT review via Codex SDK / codex exec (agentic, repo-aware)
technicalOptions.pros: Agent can actually read the repo, run commands, and produce richer context-aware reviews; JSONL/structured-output ready; sessions persist; sandbox controls; minimal prompt engineering.
technicalOptions.cons: Heavier (spawns CLI child process); ChatGPT-OAuth path is ToS-risky for automation (use API key only); Codex defaults/deprecations shift monthly; less precise control than raw API; overkill if you only need a read-only verdict on a diff.

technicalOptions.option: Unify Claude (primary) + GPT (review) behind LiteLLM / Agents SDK
technicalOptions.pros: One gateway, one auth/billing/observability surface; easy provider failover; reuse guardrail pattern (cheap model gates expensive); future-proofs adding Gemini etc.
technicalOptions.cons: LiteLLM/Any-LLM adapters are best-effort/beta in Agents SDK; usage metrics sometimes missing; extra latency + failure layer; provider-specific features (e.g. Claude caching, GPT reasoning_effort) can be flattened/lost through the adapter.

technicalOptions.option: ChatGPT-subscription OAuth auth for cost savings
technicalOptions.pros: Flat subscription cost can be cheaper than per-token for heavy interactive use; full Codex Cloud + workspace features.
technicalOptions.cons: Documented ban risk + ToS tension for programmatic/automated use; inconsistent billing (API credits drained despite plan); not an approved automation path; unacceptable for a shipped multi-user product. Avoid.

recommendations: Use GPT-5.5 (`gpt-5.5`) as the code-REVIEW model alongside Claude Code as primary engine. Access it via the regular OpenAI API with an API key (Responses or Chat Completions), NOT via ChatGPT-OAuth subscription auth. Set reasoning_effort=medium for routine PR reviews, high for security/architecture passes, xhigh for deep audits.

For the review harness, prefer the raw OpenAI API when you only need a verdict on a diff (cheapest, most controllable, 1.05M context fits whole PRs). Reach for the Codex SDK/`codex exec --json` only when the reviewer genuinely needs to read the repo / run commands — and then run it read-only sandbox with an API key.

Treat all model IDs and Codex defaults as runtime config, not hardcoded constants. The line moves ~monthly (GPT-5.2/5.3 already deprecated for sign-in; GPT-5.6 teased). Pin a default (gpt-5.5), expose an override, and keep a fallback (gpt-5.4 / gpt-5.4-mini) for cost and outages.

Implement a two-tier review to control cost: pre-screen diffs with gpt-5.4-mini (or skip trivial diffs), escalate only substantive changes to gpt-5.5. Use the Batch API (flat 50% off) for non-interactive bulk reviews in the scheduler.

Route both providers through a single gateway (LiteLLM or your own thin abstraction) so Claude-primary + GPT-review share auth, billing, retries, and observability, and you can fail over. But test that provider-specific features you care about (Claude prompt caching, GPT reasoning_effort) survive the adapter — they often don't.

For long-running / scheduled agent jobs (per-project scheduler, mobile/P2P where processes die), adopt a durable-execution layer (Temporal integration is GA) rather than hand-rolling retries. This is the reliability backbone for your scheduler.

For mobile + voice control of agents, evaluate @openai/agents + @openai/agents-realtime for the voice layer even if Claude remains the coding engine — it's the most mature TS Realtime stack. Keep it as an optional input modality, not the core.

Sandbox every agent that touches the filesystem: default to read-only for review, workspace-write only for apply-fix steps, and reserve danger-full-access / --dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox for ephemeral isolated runners only. Copy the codex-action two-job pattern (review read-only -> apply patch with write).

Never embed user-provided OpenAI API keys with subscription OAuth; for a multi-tenant marketplace, use per-tenant platform API keys (or enterprise Codex access tokens) and meter usage yourself to avoid surprise API-credit drains and to keep billing attributable.
risks: ToS + ban risk: Automating ChatGPT-subscription OAuth (Codex with Plus/Pro login) is in a gray zone OpenAI has not blessed for third-party automation; 2026 has documented unexplained Pro account bans and inconsistent billing. A shipped product must use platform API keys, not subscription OAuth.

Fast-moving / deprecation risk: Model IDs and Codex defaults change roughly monthly (gpt-5.2 and gpt-5.3-codex already deprecated for sign-in; GPT-5.6 teased). Hardcoding model IDs or relying on a specific Codex default will break; pin + config + fallbacks required.

Cost risk: gpt-5.5 at $5/$30 per 1M (and $30/$180 for Pro) plus high/xhigh reasoning_effort can make whole-repo or per-PR reviews expensive at scale. High reasoning effort silently multiplies output/reasoning tokens. Budget caps, mini pre-screens, caching, and Batch API are needed.

Vendor lock-in: Codex SDK is tightly coupled to OpenAI's CLI and Responses API; deep adoption couples your platform to OpenAI's cadence. Mitigate by keeping the review model behind a provider-neutral interface so GPT-5.5 can be swapped for Gemini/Claude.

Reliability of cross-provider adapters: LiteLLM/Any-LLM integration in the Agents SDK is best-effort/beta; provider feature parity varies, usage metrics may be absent, and an extra translation layer adds latency and a failure point. Don't put it on the critical path without fallbacks.

Auth feature-gap risk: API-key auth (required for safe automation) loses some ChatGPT-workspace/Codex Cloud features; if you design around Codex Cloud you'll hit walls. Confirm every feature you depend on works under API-key auth.

Sandbox/security risk: `--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox` and danger-full-access give agents unrestricted FS/command access; in a marketplace running untrusted skills this is a remote-code-execution surface. Strong sandboxing/isolation is mandatory.

Benchmark/marketing caution: OpenAI's GPT-5.5 superiority claims over Claude Opus 4.7 are contested (Claude still leads SWE-bench Pro with lower hallucination per independent tests). Validate review quality on YOUR codebases before trusting GPT-5.5 verdicts over Claude's.

summary: As of mid-2026, every major LLM provider exposes a coarse "reasoning effort / thinking" dial as a first-class API parameter, and the dials are NOT interchangeable. Anthropic uses output_config.effort with levels low/medium/high(default)/xhigh/max plus thinking:{type:"adaptive"}; on Opus 4.7/4.8 and Fable/Mythos 5 the old budget_tokens fixed-budget API is removed (400 error) and adaptive thinking is the only mode. OpenAI uses reasoning.effort with values none/minimal/low/medium/high/xhigh (model-dependent subset): GPT-5.1 defaults to none, GPT-5.5 defaults to medium, and a separate text.verbosity (low/medium/high) controls answer length independently of thinking depth. Google Gemini 3.x uses thinking_level (minimal/low/medium/high, default high/dynamic), deprecating thinking_budget. The dominant 2026 finding is an "effort paradox": for most models on most real workloads, higher effort costs 4-17x more and 5-60x more latency for little or negative quality gain, so low/medium is the right default and high/xhigh/max should be reserved for verified-hard tasks. For a universal agent platform the durable UX pattern is mode-aware effort: plan mode = high/xhigh + review checkpoint, execution mode = low/medium for cheap fast inference, with per-job override. This space changes every 4-8 weeks (model IDs, defaults, level names), so the platform must treat effort as a normalized abstraction layer, not hardcode vendor values.

keyFindings.point: Anthropic effort parameter: 5 levels, no beta header, controls ALL tokens not just thinking
keyFindings.detail: output_config.effort accepts low/medium/high(default)/xhigh/max. high == omitting the param. xhigh only on Opus 4.7, Opus 4.8, Fable 5, Mythos 5; max on those plus Opus 4.6/Sonnet 4.6. Effort affects text, tool calls AND thinking (lower effort = fewer tool calls, terser output), so it works even without thinking enabled. 'Behavioral signal, not a strict token budget.' Anthropic recommends xhigh as the START for coding/agentic on Opus 4.7/4.8, high for other intelligence-sensitive work, medium/low only after evals confirm quality holds.

keyFindings.point: Anthropic killed fixed thinking budgets on newest models; adaptive thinking is mandatory
keyFindings.detail: thinking:{type:"adaptive"} lets Claude decide whether/how much to think per request. On Opus 4.7 and Opus 4.8 it is the ONLY supported mode; manual thinking:{type:"enabled",budget_tokens:N} returns HTTP 400. On Fable 5/Mythos 5 thinking is always on and cannot be disabled. budget_tokens is deprecated-but-functional on Opus 4.6/Sonnet 4.6. On Opus 4.8 thinking is OFF unless you explicitly pass thinking:{type:"adaptive"}. Default display is now 'omitted' (empty thinking field, faster TTFT) on Opus 4.7/4.8/Fable/Mythos — set display:"summarized" to see reasoning. You are billed for full internal thinking tokens regardless of display; read usage.output_tokens_details.thinking_tokens.

keyFindings.point: Claude Opus 4.7 introduces task budgets and stricter low/medium effort scoping
keyFindings.detail: Task budgets let you tell Claude how many tokens it has for a FULL agentic loop (thinking + tool calls + tool results + final output), useful for the per-project scheduler. Opus 4.7/4.8 respect low/medium more strictly than 4.6 — at low effort the model scopes to exactly what was asked. Steering is promptable: append 'Please think hard before responding.' or 'Answer directly without deliberating.' per user turn. At xhigh/max set max_tokens high (start 64k) so the model doesn't hit stop_reason:max_tokens mid-loop.

keyFindings.point: OpenAI reasoning_effort now spans none→xhigh; verbosity is a SEPARATE orthogonal dial
keyFindings.detail: reasoning.effort values: none, minimal, low, medium, high, xhigh (each model supports a subset). GPT-5.1 defaults to 'none' (behaves like a non-reasoning model, fast tool-calling) and only supports none/low/medium/high. GPT-5.5 defaults to 'medium' and supports none/low/medium/high/xhigh. 'minimal' (very few/no reasoning tokens) was introduced with GPT-5; 'none' was added with GPT-5.1. text.verbosity (low/medium/high, default medium) controls final answer LENGTH independent of thinking depth — low verbosity = terse code with no comments, high = production-ready with arg-parsing/docs. Reasoning tokens billed as output tokens; visible in usage.output_tokens_details.reasoning_tokens. Reasoning models perform better via the Responses API than Chat Completions.

keyFindings.point: Google Gemini 3.x switched from thinking_budget to thinking_level
keyFindings.detail: Gemini 3 introduces thinking_level (minimal/low/medium[3.0 Flash only]/high). Default is high/dynamic thinking. thinking_budget is accepted for backward compat but Google warns it 'may result in unexpected performance' on Gemini 3 Pro — do not mix the two (conflict error). Gemini uses encrypted 'thought signatures' to carry reasoning context across calls, analogous to Anthropic's signature field. Gemini bills internal thinking tokens as output tokens.

keyFindings.point: The 'effort paradox': higher effort often equal/worse quality at much higher cost
keyFindings.detail: FutureSearch (900 tasks, 5 frontier models, 3 tiers): GPT-5 low 49.6% → high 48.1% (accuracy DROPS, cost +56%). Gemini 3 Flash low 49.9% → high 47.9% (−2 pts, 3x cost). Claude 4.5 Opus 54.9% vs 54.8% (flat, +47% cost). Only Claude 4.6 Opus improved with effort: +1.9 pts but +128% cost and 3x latency (159s→461s). Separate benchmark: across the full effort curve quality lifts 8-22 pts but fees inflate 4-17x and latency 5-60x; the clean lift is low→medium (12-15 pts), medium→high adds only 3-7 pts. Recommendation: default LOW/MEDIUM, escalate to high only on verified-hard, binary-answer tasks (hard math/contest), never blanket-apply high to PR-scale review.

keyFindings.point: Plan mode and thinking/effort are ORTHOGONAL controls — the core UX insight for our app
keyFindings.detail: Plan mode = WORKFLOW control: Claude writes a structured plan (files it will touch, risks) then STOPS for human approval, modifies nothing. Thinking/effort = REASONING control: Claude thinks harder internally but does not pause. They compose: plan mode benefits from high/xhigh effort (architecture, trade-offs); execution benefits from low/medium (fast, cheap). This is exactly the dual-engine split for a Claude-Code-primary platform.

keyFindings.point: Mode-aware effort switching is a known gap in Claude Code itself
keyFindings.detail: GitHub issue #50323 requests automatic model+thinking switching between plan and execution modes. Today MAX_THINKING_TOKENS and model are GLOBAL settings.json values requiring session restart; users manually swap profiles.
