Proposed: per-mode config block {plan:{model:opus,maxThinkingTokens:32000}, acceptEdits:{model:sonnet,maxThinkingTokens:4000}} or a ModeChange hook. Closed as duplicate (consolidated, not yet shipped as of 2026). Implication: our platform should build this orchestration layer ourselves rather than wait for the CLI.

keyFindings.point: Claude Code 'ultracode' / 'ultra' is xhigh effort + multi-agent permission, NOT a new API level
keyFindings.detail: Anthropic docs explicitly note ultracode in Claude Code's effort menu is NOT an additional API effort level — it pairs xhigh effort with standing permission to launch multi-agent workflows via mid-conversation system messages. The complete API effort set is the 5 documented values. Build similar behavior via the orchestration-mode pattern.

keyFindings.point: Open-weight models now expose reasoning as a parameter, useful for cost-floor / self-host
keyFindings.detail: DeepSeek V4 (MIT-licensed weights: V4-Pro, V4-Flash, V3.2, R1) exposes reasoning_effort high/max and returns reasoning_content alongside content. Qwen 3.5/3.6 use an enable_thinking boolean toggle; licensing is mixed (Qwen3.6-35B-A3B Apache 2.0, but Qwen3.6-Plus is proprietary cloud-only). These give a vendor-lock-in escape hatch and a cheap tier for low-effort subagent work.

itemsOrTools.name: Anthropic Claude Opus 4.8 (claude-opus-4-8)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Flagship coding/agentic model, released 2026-05-28, 1M-token context at standard pricing. Adaptive thinking only (thinking:{type:"adaptive"}); effort levels low/medium/high/xhigh/max via output_config.effort. Default effort high, default thinking display 'omitted'.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Primary engine for Claude Code as the platform's main agent. Use xhigh for plan/architecture and complex builds, medium/low for execution and subagents. effort + max_tokens + task budgets give precise per-job cost control for the per-project scheduler.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: $5/M input, $25/M output (Fast mode $10/$25... per token sources vary; standard $5/$25). Cached input heavily discounted. Anthropic API, Bedrock, Vertex. SDKs: Python, TS, Go, Java, C#, PHP, Ruby.

itemsOrTools.name: Claude adaptive thinking (thinking:{type:"adaptive"})
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Model decides per-request whether/how much to think; auto-enables interleaved thinking (think between tool calls). Replaces budget_tokens. Steerable per-message ('think hard' / 'answer directly').
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best default for agentic loops — avoids wasting reasoning on easy steps and avoids stopping early on hard ones. Interleaved thinking is key for tool-heavy flows (WhatsApp/Telegram handlers, scheduler tasks, image/video pipeline orchestration).
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: No beta header. Billed for full thinking tokens (read usage.output_tokens_details.thinking_tokens). display:'summarized' to surface reasoning to users.

itemsOrTools.name: OpenAI GPT-5.5 (reasoning.effort + text.verbosity)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Agentic model, 1.05M context. reasoning.effort none/low/medium(default)/high/xhigh; text.verbosity low/medium/high(default medium) controls answer length separately. Reasoning tokens billed as output.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Strong choice as the GPT REVIEW model. Run reviews at medium/high effort with LOW verbosity to get rigorous, terse critiques. 'none' effort gives fast non-reasoning tool-calling for cheap classification/routing in the platform.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: $5/M input, $30/M output standard short-context; cached input ~$0.50/M (90% off); Batch/Flex $2.50/$15; Priority $12.50/$75. GPT-5.5 Pro $30/$180. Responses API recommended over Chat Completions.

itemsOrTools.name: OpenAI GPT-5.1 (reasoning_effort default 'none')
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Defaults to none (non-reasoning, low latency, performant tool-calling); supports none/low/medium/high. Upgrading from older reasoning models requires explicitly passing effort to get reasoning back.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Cheapest fast tier for latency-sensitive review/routing where reasoning isn't needed. Watch the default-changed footgun: code that assumed reasoning-on must now pass effort explicitly.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OpenAI API; no deprecation planned for GPT-5.1/5/4.1. Responses API preferred.

itemsOrTools.name: Google Gemini 3.x (thinking_level)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: thinking_level minimal/low/medium(3.0 Flash)/high, default high/dynamic. thinking_budget deprecated/conflicting on Gemini 3. Thought signatures carry reasoning across calls.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Viable third reviewer / cheap multimodal tier (image+video studio captioning, vision tasks). Gemini 3 Flash is a low-cost high-volume option for the image/video pipeline.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Gemini 3 Pro $2/M in (≤200k), $12/M out; >200k $4/$18. Gemini 3 Flash $0.50/$3. Gemini 3.5 Flash (2026-05-19) $1.50/$9. Gemini Developer API + Vertex.

itemsOrTools.name: DeepSeek V4 (reasoning_effort, MIT open weights)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: reasoning_effort high/max flips thinking mode; returns reasoning_content alongside content. Weights MIT-licensed (V4-Pro, V4-Flash, V3.2, R1).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Vendor-lock-in escape hatch and self-host option for cheap low-effort subagent/classification work; P2P/mobile-friendly if quantized. Reduces dependency on Anthropic+OpenAI for the cost floor.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: DeepSeek API (cheap) or self-host MIT weights. Open-weight, no per-token vendor cost when self-hosted.

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Code plan mode + task budgets
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Plan mode = write plan, stop for approval, modify nothing (workflow gate). Task budgets cap tokens for a full agentic loop. Orthogonal to effort.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Directly maps to our plan-vs-execution UX: plan mode (high/xhigh, human checkpoint) then execution (low/medium). Task budgets enforce per-project scheduler spend caps.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Built into Claude Code; replicate via API orchestration mode (mid-conversation system messages) for our own desktop/mobile client.

recommendations: Build a provider-agnostic EFFORT ABSTRACTION layer in the platform: a normalized scale (e.g. FAST / BALANCED / DEEP / MAX) that maps per-vendor to {effort, thinking mode, verbosity, max_tokens}. Anthropic FAST→effort:low; BALANCED→medium; DEEP→high or xhigh + thinking:adaptive; MAX→max. OpenAI FAST→reasoning.effort:none/minimal + verbosity:low; BALANCED→medium; DEEP→high; MAX→xhigh. Gemini FAST→thinking_level:low; DEEP→high. Never hardcode raw vendor values in product code — level names and defaults change every release.

Adopt mode-aware effort as the default UX (the Claude Code #50323 pattern): Plan mode auto-selects DEEP/MAX with a human approval checkpoint; Execution mode auto-drops to BALANCED/FAST. Expose a per-job override slider so a user can bump a single hard task to MAX without changing global config. Persist last-used effort per project in the scheduler.

Use the dual-engine split deliberately: Claude Opus 4.8 at xhigh in plan mode as the primary author; GPT-5.5 at medium/high effort with text.verbosity:low as the review model (terse, rigorous critiques). Add Gemini 3 Flash or DeepSeek V4 as a cheap third opinion / cost-floor tier for routing, classification, and high-volume subagent work.

Default to LOW/MEDIUM effort everywhere and ESCALATE only on evidence. The effort paradox shows high effort is flat-or-worse for most models on most tasks at 4-17x cost and 5-60x latency. Reserve DEEP/MAX for verified-hard, binary-answer work (architecture decisions, hard debugging, math). Surface the cost/latency multiplier in the UI when a user picks a higher level.

Instrument real cost: log usage.output_tokens_details.thinking_tokens (Anthropic) / reasoning_tokens (OpenAI) per job and show users actual thinking spend, since billed tokens never match visible tokens. Use this telemetry to auto-tune default effort per task type via A/B evals on YOUR workloads.

Set max_tokens generously (start 64k) for xhigh/max agentic loops to avoid stop_reason:max_tokens mid-task; use Anthropic task budgets to enforce per-project scheduler spend caps for the full loop (thinking+tools+output), not just final output.

Prefer adaptive thinking + interleaved thinking for all tool-heavy flows (WhatsApp/Telegram handlers, scheduler, image/video orchestration) — it reasons between tool calls and is more token-efficient than fixed budgets. Add a short system-prompt steer ('think only when it improves quality') to suppress over-thinking on simple chat replies.

Cache aggressively and keep thinking mode constant within a conversation: switching between adaptive and enabled/disabled breaks Anthropic message-level prompt cache breakpoints (system prompts/tools stay cached). On OpenAI lean on 90%-off cached input. This materially cuts the per-project scheduler bill.

For mobile + P2P, route default traffic to FAST tiers (GPT-5.1 none, Gemini Flash, or self-hosted DeepSeek V4) to control latency and cost; only reach for desktop-grade DEEP/MAX Opus calls on explicit user request or plan-mode escalation.
risks: FAST-MOVING / UNCERTAIN: model IDs, default effort levels, and level names change every 4-8 weeks (Opus 4.5→4.6→4.7→4.8→Fable/Mythos 5 in ~6 months; GPT-5→5.1→5.2→5.4→5.5; Gemini 3→3.1→3.5). Defaults also flip silently (GPT-5.1 default 'none', Opus 4.8 thinking display default 'omitted', Opus 4.8 thinking OFF unless adaptive explicitly set). Hardcoding any of these WILL break. Treat every number/name in this report as a snapshot, re-verify at integration time, and pin model IDs.

BREAKING API CHANGES: Anthropic removed fixed budget_tokens on Opus 4.7/4.8/Fable/Mythos (returns HTTP 400). Any code path assuming thinking:{type:"enabled",budget_tokens:N} will hard-fail on new models. Gemini thinking_budget conflicts with thinking_level on Gemini 3. Migration is forced, not optional.

COST BLOWOUT: thinking/reasoning tokens are billed as output tokens and are invisible by default (display:'omitted'); a 'short' answer can hide a huge billed thinking spend. xhigh/max on Opus, high/xhigh on GPT-5.5, and dynamic high on Gemini can 4-17x cost vs low for negative quality gain (effort paradox). Without per-job telemetry the scheduler can silently run up large bills.

EFFORT PARADOX = QUALITY RISK: blindly defaulting to high/max can REDUCE accuracy on some models/tasks (GPT-5, Gemini 3 Flash, Claude 4.5 Opus all flat-or-worse at high effort in FutureSearch). Auto-escalation logic must be eval-gated per task type, not a global 'more is better' assumption.

VENDOR LOCK-IN: each provider's effort/thinking/verbosity API is incompatible (output_config.effort vs reasoning.effort vs thinking_level; adaptive vs none vs dynamic; thought signatures vs signature fields are non-portable across vendors). Building product logic directly on one vendor's shape creates migration debt. The abstraction layer is the mitigation.

TOS / BAN RISK for the comms layer: WhatsApp/Telegram automation via unofficial bridges (the wa_* tools) risks number bans and violates WhatsApp ToS for non-Cloud-API automation; this is independent of the LLM effort topic but is the highest operational ban-risk surface in the platform. Use official WhatsApp Cloud API / Telegram Bot API where possible and isolate per-tenant numbers.

RELIABILITY: at xhigh/max with generous max_tokens, long agentic loops (Anthropic cites 30+ min, multi-million-token rollouts) increase timeout, partial-failure, and stop_reason:max_tokens risk. Need robust checkpointing/resume in the scheduler and graceful degradation to lower effort on retry.

RAW CHAIN-OF-THOUGHT UNAVAILABLE: Fable 5/Mythos 5 never return raw CoT and can refuse 'reasoning_extraction' attempts; summarized thinking is processed by a different model and is subject to change. Do not build product features that depend on parsing full internal reasoning — only the summarized/omitted blocks and signatures are stable.

technicalOptions.option: Normalized effort abstraction layer (FAST/BALANCED/DEEP/MAX → per-vendor params)
technicalOptions.pros: Insulates product from rapid vendor churn (IDs, defaults, level names); enables one-line provider swap; lets us A/B effort per task type; future-proofs against breaking changes like budget_tokens removal.
technicalOptions.cons: Upfront engineering; must maintain a mapping table and re-verify each vendor release; lossy where a vendor has a unique level (e.g. OpenAI 'none', Gemini dynamic) with no clean cross-vendor equivalent.

technicalOptions.option: Mode-aware auto-effort (plan=DEEP+checkpoint, execution=BALANCED/FAST) with per-job override
technicalOptions.pros: Matches the proven Claude Code plan-vs-execution UX; aligns spend with cognitive demand; review checkpoint catches wrong plans before expensive execution; one slider gives users control without global config edits.
technicalOptions.cons: Claude Code itself hasn't shipped native per-mode model/effort switching (#50323 closed as duplicate) so we build/maintain the orchestration ourselves; risk of users leaving DEEP on and overspending — needs visible cost hints.

technicalOptions.option: Static high/max effort everywhere (max quality first)
technicalOptions.pros: Simplest to implement; best-case quality on genuinely hard tasks; no per-task tuning needed.
technicalOptions.cons: Effort paradox — flat or WORSE accuracy on most models/tasks at 4-17x cost and 5-60x latency; terrible for mobile/P2P latency and for the scheduler bill; not defensible.

technicalOptions.option: Multi-provider dual/triple-engine (Opus author + GPT review + Gemini/DeepSeek cheap tier)
technicalOptions.pros: Diverse-model review catches more errors; cost floor via cheap/open-weight tier; reduces single-vendor lock-in and outage risk; lets each model run at its sweet-spot effort.
technicalOptions.cons: 3x integration + observability surface; cross-vendor non-portability of thinking signatures and effort semantics; harder cost accounting; prompt-cache benefits don't span providers.

technicalOptions.option: Self-host open-weight reasoning (DeepSeek V4 MIT / Qwen Apache) for FAST tier
technicalOptions.pros: Eliminates per-token vendor cost for high-volume low-effort work; data stays in-house (P2P/mobile privacy); reasoning_effort/enable_thinking still available; no ToS effort limits.
technicalOptions.cons: Infra/ops burden and GPU cost; quality below Opus/GPT-5.5 on hard tasks; Qwen licensing mixed (some proprietary cloud-only); quantization for mobile/P2P degrades reasoning.

summary: As of mid-2026, multi-agent orchestration has bifurcated into two layers: (1) agent-authoring frameworks (LangGraph, CrewAI, Microsoft Agent Framework/AutoGen, OpenAI Agents SDK, Claude Agent SDK, Inngest AgentKit) that express patterns like planner-executor, orchestrator-worker (supervisor), swarm/handoff, and map-reduce fan-out; and (2) durable execution engines (Temporal, Inngest, Restate, Trigger.dev, plus new entrants AWS Durable Functions, Cloudflare Workflows GA, Vercel Workflow DevKit) that journal every step so long-running agents survive crashes. The dominant production topology is orchestrator-worker (~70% of deployments) with scoped token budgets and human-in-the-loop (HITL) approval gates implemented via pause/resume interrupts. LLM-as-judge is maturing into Agent-as-a-Judge and panels-of-judges (PoLL) to counter single-model bias and adversarial manipulation. For a Claude-Code-primary platform, the natural spine is the Claude Agent SDK (subagents + worktree isolation + hooks + MCP) for execution, GPT-5.1 as an independent review/judge model, and a durable engine (Trigger.dev or Inngest for TS-native ergonomics, Temporal for heavy multi-week workflows) underneath the per-project scheduler. The fastest-moving and most uncertain areas are durable-engine pricing for LLM payloads, the AutoGen-to-MAF transition, and unofficial WhatsApp automation ban risk (15-30% over 12 months for proactive messaging), which is a serious ToS/account-risk landmine for our comms layer.

keyFindings.point: Orchestrator-worker (supervisor) is the dominant production pattern (~70% of multi-agent deployments in 2026)
keyFindings.detail: A single orchestrator classifies, decomposes, dispatches to specialized workers (researcher/coder/tester/reviewer), and merges results. Strength: oversight, scoped per-branch token budgets, ability to cancel wandering sub-trees, clean audit trail. Cost: coordination latency (a 3-level hierarchy with 2s LLM calls adds >=6s before any worker starts) and up to ~15x token cost vs single agent. Anthropic's stance: 'multi-agent systems work mainly because they help spend enough tokens'; only justified when the task is provably breadth-first. Used in internal builds at Stripe, Mercury, and Anthropic/OpenAI reference designs.

keyFindings.point: Planner-executor splits strategy from execution and is more token-efficient than ReAct loops for structured tasks
keyFindings.detail: Planner creates a complete plan upfront (~1 planning call), then an executor runs each step. More efficient than ReAct's iterative loop for well-structured work. Maps cleanly onto our per-project scheduler: plan once, persist the plan as durable state, execute steps as independently-retryable durable activities.

keyFindings.point: Map-reduce / fan-out via LangGraph Send API gives dynamic parallelism when item count is unknown at design time
keyFindings.detail: Send() is a special edge that dynamically creates parallel branches at runtime (return a list of Send objects instead of a routing string). Reported ~60% latency reduction; 50 documents summarized side-by-side in ~12s vs ~10min serially. Best for document summaries, batch labeling, parallel tool calls, multi-source research. For 3-10 parallel tasks, fan-out inside a supervisor is the recommended default.

keyFindings.point: Swarm/handoff: OpenAI Swarm is now a reference design; OpenAI Agents SDK is the supported production successor
keyFindings.detail: Released March 2025 as Swarm's production evolution. Core abstraction is the handoff (a tool call that returns another Agent; runner switches active_agent and keeps shared conversation history). Three built-in primitives: Handoffs, Guardrails (input/output validation), Tracing. Python and TypeScript. Swarm itself (github.com/openai/swarm) is educational-only now.

keyFindings.point: Worktree-isolated parallel agents are a first-class Claude Code feature and directly match our engine
keyFindings.detail: Subagents can run in isolated git worktrees (set isolation: worktree in subagent frontmatter, or ask Claude to 'use worktrees'); each gets its own branch + working dir, removed automatically on finish. Three orchestration primitives: SendMessage (typed direct/broadcast/shutdown/plan-approval messaging), TaskCreate (shared work queue with dependency tracking), worktree isolation for safe parallel edits. Anthropic ran this at scale: 16 agents, 2,000 sessions, 100,000 lines of compiler code. Teams reliably run 4-8 concurrent worktrees per developer; above that the bottleneck is review, not the model.

keyFindings.point: LLM-as-judge is migrating to Agent-as-a-Judge and panels (PoLL); adversarial robustness is a real concern
keyFindings.detail: Single-pass LLM judges are vulnerable: a crafted 'null'/nonsense persuasive response can trick a GPT-4 evaluator into high rankings; universal adversarial attacks exist. Panels of LLM Evaluators (PoLL) using smaller, disjoint model families outperform a single large judge by reducing intra-model bias at lower cost. CollabEval uses 3-phase collaborative eval (initial -> multi-round discussion -> final judgment); CourtEval uses an adversarial courtroom metaphor. Agent-as-a-Judge adds planning, tool-augmented verification, and persistent memory for multi-step evaluands. Implication for us: use GPT-5.1 as a cross-family judge against Claude outputs to cut shared-model bias.

keyFindings.point: Human-in-the-loop gates are now native via durable interrupts that pause and resume without restarting
keyFindings.detail: LangGraph offers static breakpoints (compile-time) and dynamic interrupt() raised inside a node from runtime state; graph state is persisted so execution pauses safely and resumes later. Decision verbs: approve / edit (modify before running) / reject (with feedback) / respond (ask-user tools). Conditional predicates gate interrupts on tool-call arguments (e.g., only pause for file writes or SQL). Trigger.dev and Inngest AgentKit provide equivalent pausable HITL without you managing infra. This is the exact mechanism our approval gates for sensitive actions (sending WhatsApp, publishing video) should use.

keyFindings.point: Durable execution crossed into the early majority in late 2025; the engine is now table stakes for production agents
keyFindings.detail: AWS released Durable Functions, Cloudflare shipped Workflows GA, Vercel launched its Workflow DevKit. Durable engines journal every step (activity scheduled/started/completed) and replay deterministically, skipping completed steps. Temporal raised $300M at a $5B valuation (Feb 17, 2026) with 9.1T lifetime action executions (1.86T from AI-native companies). Key LLM caveat: Temporal suffers workflow-history saturation from large LLM payloads, forcing teams to add payload codecs that offload data to external storage.

keyFindings.point: Claude Agent SDK gets a separate Agent SDK credit pool starting June 15, 2026 - a billing change to plan around
keyFindings.detail: Agent SDK / claude -p usage on subscription plans draws from a NEW monthly Agent SDK credit separate from interactive limits (effective June 15, 2026). SDK is Python + TypeScript, same harness as Claude Code; subagents each run in a fresh conversation (only final message returns to parent), supporting parallel style-checker/security-scanner/test-coverage runs. Hooks: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, SessionStart, SessionEnd, UserPromptSubmit, plus subagent-completion. Session state is JSONL on the local filesystem (resumable/forkable). Deepest native MCP support of any framework as of early 2026.

keyFindings.point: Cross-family review model economics: Claude Opus 4.8 execution + GPT-5.1 review is affordable with caching
keyFindings.detail: Claude Opus 4.8 (released May 28, 2026): $5 in / $25 out per M tokens (Fast Mode $10/$50), full 1M context at standard price; cache reads ~10% of input (~$0.50/M), batch 50% off, stackable to ~95% savings. Claude Sonnet 4.6: $3/$15. GPT-5.1 (launched Nov 13, 2025): GPT-5.1 Codex Max from $1.25 in / $10 out per M, 400K context, cached input $0.125/M; base GPT-5 $0.625 in / $5 out. Cached input is 10% and automatic on both providers. Using GPT-5.1 only for review/judge passes keeps the second-opinion layer cheap.

keyFindings.point: AutoGen is in maintenance mode; Microsoft Agent Framework (MAF) 1.0 GA is the successor (April 2, 2026)
keyFindings.detail: MAF unifies AutoGen + Semantic Kernel into one supported open-source SDK/runtime with identical concepts/APIs across .NET and Python. AutoGen still usable for existing projects but MAF recommended for new ones. CrewAI executes 30-60% faster than AutoGen on simple orchestration; AG2/AutoGen wins complex multi-turn negotiation. Don't build new work on AutoGen.

keyFindings.point: Unofficial WhatsApp automation carries serious, quantified ban risk - a direct threat to our comms layer
keyFindings.detail: With the official WhatsApp Business API ban risk is near-zero if you follow Meta policy (opt-in consent, approved templates, good quality rating). Unofficial libs (WAHA, whatsapp-web.js, Baileys): respond-only bots have <2% ban rate over 12 months, but proactive messaging to new contacts sees 15-30% bans. 2026 detection added unanswered-message counting plus device fingerprinting at registration, send-velocity/reply-rate behavioral analysis, and user-report signals. Meta also began blocking third-party AI chatbots on WhatsApp in 2026. Telegram is safer: official Bot API is fully sanctioned; userbots are allowed but must obey ToS (no spam/mass messaging).

keyFindings.point: OpenTelemetry GenAI is becoming the cross-framework observability standard, reducing vendor lock-in
keyFindings.detail: Six platforms anchor 2026: LangSmith (deepest LangChain integration, near-zero overhead), Langfuse (open-source leader, self-hostable, OTEL-based), Arize Phoenix, Helicone (drop-in proxy), Datadog LLM Observability, Honeycomb. Pydantic AI, smolagents, and Strands emit OTEL traces natively. Hardest multi-agent problem: correlating traces across parallel branches and propagating trace context through every message boundary so a sub-agent poisoning shared state is debuggable. W&B Weave preserves parent-child agent relationships per trace.

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Agent SDK
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Anthropic's official Python/TS library running the same harness as Claude Code: autonomous agent loop with file/shell/web/code tools, subagents (each in a fresh context; only final message returns to parent), worktree isolation, lifecycle hooks (PreToolUse/PostToolUse/Stop/SessionStart/SessionEnd/UserPromptSubmit/subagent-completion), granular per-tool permissions, JSONL resumable/forkable sessions, and the deepest native MCP support of any framework.
itemsOrTools.relevance: This IS our primary engine. Subagents + worktree isolation directly deliver parallel isolated agents; hooks enforce HITL gates and policy; MCP wires in our WhatsApp/Telegram, image/video studio, and skills marketplace tools. Per-project scheduler can spawn scoped SDK sessions.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS library (Python + TS), Apache-style. From June 15, 2026 subscription usage draws from a separate monthly Agent SDK credit pool. API token costs apply (Opus 4.8 $5/$25 per M).

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Code worktrees + Task tool
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: First-class parallel-agent isolation: subagents run in temporary git worktrees (isolation: worktree frontmatter), each with own branch/working dir, auto-removed on finish. Orchestration primitives: SendMessage (typed direct/broadcast/shutdown/plan-approval), TaskCreate (shared dependency-tracked queue), worktree isolation.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Directly implements worktree-isolated parallel agents and the orchestrator-worker pattern for our code/content workers. Proven at Anthropic scale (16 agents, 2,000 sessions, 100k LOC). Plan for 4-8 concurrent worktrees/dev; review is the real bottleneck.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Bundled with Claude Code / Agent SDK; OSS tooling on top (e.g., spillwavesolutions/parallel-worktrees).

itemsOrTools.name: OpenAI Agents SDK (ex-Swarm)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Production successor to Swarm for swarm/handoff orchestration. Agents defined with instructions, model, tools, and a handoff list; a handoff is a tool call returning another Agent while preserving shared history. Built-ins: Handoffs, Guardrails (I/O validation), Tracing. Python + TS.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Reference design for our swarm/handoff flows and a clean host for GPT-5.1 as a guardrail/review agent. Tracing primitive aids multi-agent debugging.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS SDK; pay per OpenAI token (GPT-5.1 Codex Max $1.25/$10 per M, GPT-5 $0.625/$5). Swarm itself is educational-only.

itemsOrTools.name: LangGraph + LangGraph Platform
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Graph-based orchestration (nodes=agents/steps, edges=control flow) supporting single/multi-agent/hierarchical/supervisor/swarm/map-reduce patterns; durable execution with checkpointing (auto-save after each step, pause/resume), structured memory, and native HITL via static breakpoints + dynamic interrupt(). MIT OSS; Platform adds hosted deploy + LangSmith observability.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best-documented home for explicit pattern graphs (map-reduce via Send API, supervisor fan-out, HITL gates). Useful as a higher-level orchestration spec layer above Claude Agent SDK workers, or for content pipelines. Used by Klarna, Lyft, Uber, LinkedIn, Coinbase, Nvidia, Harvey.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS (MIT) free. Platform Developer: free first 100k node execs/mo, then $0.001/node. Plus: $0.001/node + standby ($0.0007/min dev, $0.0036/min prod) + $39/user/mo LangSmith Plus.

itemsOrTools.name: Temporal (Cloud + OSS)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Durable execution engine: journals event history per workflow, replays deterministically skipping completed steps; SDKs in Go/Java/TS/Python/.NET; inspect/replay/rewind every step. $300M raised at $5B valuation (Feb 2026); 9.1T lifetime actions.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The heavy-duty option for multi-week, mission-critical project workflows and our per-project scheduler durability. Caveat for us: large LLM payloads saturate workflow history - must use payload codecs to offload to external storage.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS self-host free. Cloud (May 2026): Essentials from $100/mo (1M actions, 99.9% SLA), Business from $500/mo (2.5M actions), Enterprise/Mission-Critical custom; overage ~$50/M actions.

itemsOrTools.name: Inngest + AgentKit + step.ai
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Event-driven durable execution with TS-native step.run()/step.ai; handles >100M daily executions, automatic retries, scheduling, observability. AgentKit builds multi-agent networks (Agents + Router + shared Network State, deterministic state-based routing) over OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini; useAgent React hook streams durable workflow updates to the browser; native HITL.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Strong fit for our TS desktop/mobile stack: durable per-project scheduler + agent networks with native HITL and real-time browser streaming. Maps cleanly onto event-driven agent patterns. Watch step-based billing cost explosion under heavy LLM retries.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS AgentKit (MIT). Inngest: 50k step-runs/mo free; Hobby from $20/mo; metered by steps/events/seats.

itemsOrTools.name: Trigger.dev v4
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Fully-managed durable task platform: tasks are inherently durable via checkpointing, no timeouts, survive crashes, runs can last minutes/hours; warm starts 100-300ms; concurrency control (single/parallel/per-tenant via concurrency keys; limits only apply while executing); built-in HITL waits that snapshot+restore the process so you don't pay for wait time.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Excellent durable backbone for our per-project scheduler and long-running content/video jobs - pausable HITL and no-pay-while-waiting fit image+video studio rendering and approval gates. TS-native, low ceremony vs Temporal.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free $0/mo (20 concurrent runs, 10 schedules, 1-day logs); Hobby $10/mo (50 concurrent runs, 100 schedules, 7-day logs); OSS core.

itemsOrTools.name: Restate
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Durable execution using the same journal/replay mechanism as Temporal but with a lighter footprint; journal prevents duplicate execution on resume.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Lighter-weight durable alternative if Temporal's operational ceremony is too heavy and we want self-hosted control with smaller overhead.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS; self-host. Cloud offering exists - confirm current tier pricing before committing (changing fast).

itemsOrTools.name: CrewAI (OSS + AMP)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Role-based crew orchestration with process types (sequential/hierarchical); lowest learning curve (~20 lines to start), 30-60% faster than AutoGen on simple tasks. AMP cloud handles deploy/updates/visual UI; AMP Factory deploys to on-prem/private VPC (AWS/Azure/GCP) with SSO (Entra/Okta), RBAC, dedicated VPC.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Fast path for role-based content crews (writer/editor/SEO/reviewer) in our marketplace. Enterprise deployment options matter if we self-host for customers. Token bills pass through to us/provider.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Core MIT OSS free. Free 50 execs/mo; Professional $25/mo (100 execs/mo); Enterprise custom (~$60k-$120k/yr est.).

itemsOrTools.name: Microsoft Agent Framework (MAF) 1.0
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Enterprise successor to AutoGen, unifying AutoGen + Semantic Kernel into one open-source SDK/runtime with identical concepts/APIs across .NET and Python; GA April 2, 2026; multi-agent workflows, hosted agents, CodeAct, agent harness.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Only relevant if we need .NET/Azure-native enterprise deployment. AutoGen is maintenance-mode - avoid for new work. Lower priority than Claude/OpenAI SDKs for our stack.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS SDK (.NET + Python); runs on Azure App Service / Foundry; pay underlying model + Azure compute.

itemsOrTools.name: Langfuse (observability)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Open-source, self-hostable LLM/agent observability built on OpenTelemetry; hierarchical traces capture every LLM call, tool invocation, retrieval step; preserves parent-child agent relationships.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Our default cross-framework tracing layer (avoids lock-in via OTEL) to debug parallel sub-agents and propagate trace context across message boundaries - essential for diagnosing shared-state poisoning in worktree swarms.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS self-host free; managed cloud tiers available. OTEL-native, integrates with Pydantic AI/smolagents/Strands.

itemsOrTools.name: LangSmith (observability/eval)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: LangChain-native observability + evaluation: full reasoning traces (prompts, retrieved context, tool selection, I/O, errors), near-zero overhead, supports LLM-as-judge evaluators and datasets.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best if we adopt LangGraph; also a managed home for judge-panel evals comparing Claude vs GPT-5.1 outputs. Bundled into LangGraph Platform Plus.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free tier; Plus $39/user/mo; usage-based traces. Vendor-tied to LangChain ecosystem.

itemsOrTools.name: Telegram Bot API / userbots
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Official, fully-sanctioned Bot API for in-app bots; userbots (e.g., via TDLib/Telethon) automate as a normal user account for actions bots can't do (group management, human-style reactions), but must obey ToS (no spam/mass messaging).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Lower-risk comms channel than WhatsApp for our messaging layer. Prefer official Bot API; treat userbots as restricted/opt-in with rate limits.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free official API; bot ToS at telegram.org/tos/bots; userbots governed by core.telegram.org/api/terms.

recommendations: Adopt a two-layer architecture: Claude Agent SDK as the execution engine (subagents + worktree isolation + hooks + MCP) wrapped by a durable orchestration engine for crash-safety, scheduling, and HITL. Do not hand-roll durability - the journaling/replay problem is solved.

For the per-project scheduler and long-running content/video jobs, default to Trigger.dev v4 (TS-native, pausable HITL, no-pay-while-waiting, free 20 concurrent runs) or Inngest AgentKit (event-driven, useAgent browser streaming) given our TS desktop+mobile stack. Reserve Temporal for genuinely multi-week mission-critical workflows, and if used, immediately implement payload codecs to keep LLM outputs out of workflow history.

Implement the orchestrator-worker pattern as the primary topology with explicit per-branch token budgets and the ability to cancel wandering sub-trees; use map-reduce fan-out (LangGraph Send-style) only when item count is unknown at design time. Keep most flows single-agent-with-tools unless the task is provably breadth-first, per Anthropic's guidance, to avoid the ~15x token blow-up.

Use GPT-5.1 (Codex Max or base) strictly as an independent cross-family REVIEW/JUDGE model against Claude Opus 4.8 execution output. Cross-family judging reduces shared-model bias; run a small panel (PoLL) or 2-pass adversarial verify-then-judge for high-stakes outputs (publishing, code merges). Cache aggressively - both providers give 10% cached-input automatically.

Gate every sensitive/irreversible action (send WhatsApp/Telegram message, publish video, merge code, spend money) behind a durable HITL interrupt with approve/edit/reject/respond semantics and conditional predicates that auto-approve safe args. Use Claude Agent SDK PreToolUse hooks plus the durable engine's pause/resume so approvals survive process restarts.

Make WhatsApp official-API-first: route proactive/outbound messaging through the official WhatsApp Business API (opt-in consent, approved templates, quality rating) and confine any unofficial-library use to respond-only flows (<2% ban rate). Default the broader comms layer to Telegram's official Bot API, which is lower-risk. Treat unofficial WhatsApp proactive messaging as a feature we do NOT ship by default.

Standardize observability on OpenTelemetry GenAI semantics with Langfuse (self-hostable, no lock-in) as the trace store; ensure trace context propagates across every inter-agent message boundary so parallel worktree sub-agents and shared-state poisoning are debuggable. Layer LangSmith only if we adopt LangGraph.

Keep model and framework choices swappable: route all LLM calls through an abstraction (AgentKit/AgentGateway-style) so Claude Opus 4.8 -> Sonnet 4.6 -> GPT-5.1 substitution is config, not code. Build new work on Claude Agent SDK / OpenAI Agents SDK / MAF - never on maintenance-mode AutoGen.

For the dynamic skills marketplace, model skills as MCP servers + scoped subagent definitions (frontmatter with tool permissions and isolation: worktree) so third-party skills run sandboxed with least privilege and per-tool approval; enforce permission boundaries via SDK hooks.
risks: Account-ban / ToS risk (HIGH): unofficial WhatsApp automation for proactive messaging carries a 15-30% ban rate over 12 months; Meta added unanswered-message counting, device fingerprinting, and behavioral velocity analysis in 2026 and began blocking third-party AI chatbots on WhatsApp. Shipping outbound unofficial WhatsApp could get our users mass-banned and expose us to platform-policy liability.

Billing/credit change (Claude Agent SDK): from June 15, 2026 SDK and claude -p usage draws from a separate monthly Agent SDK credit pool on subscription plans - capacity planning and cost models must account for this split, or scheduled/background agents may exhaust credits unexpectedly.

Token-cost explosion: orchestrator-worker multi-agent can cost ~15x a single agent; durable engines with step-based pricing (Inngest, partly Trigger.dev) can scale unpredictably when LLM retries (rate limits) generate many billable steps. Without per-branch token budgets and caching, the image/video + multi-agent flows could be ruinously expensive.

LLM-as-judge unreliability: single-pass judges are adversarially manipulable (a crafted nonsense response can score high) and carry single-model bias. Relying on one judge (even GPT-5.1) for auto-merge/auto-publish is unsafe; needs panels or adversarial verify steps, which add cost and latency.

Vendor lock-in: LangSmith ties to LangChain; Temporal's proxied runtime and CrewAI AMP create switching costs. Durable-engine and observability choices are stickiest - mitigate with OTEL and an LLM routing abstraction. Pricing across Temporal/Inngest/Trigger.dev/LangGraph Platform is changing fast (multiple 2026 revisions) and should be re-verified before contracts.

Reliability of large LLM payloads in durable engines: Temporal workflow-history saturation requires payload codecs; getting this wrong causes silent workflow failures or storage blowups. Restate cloud pricing and maturity are less proven - validate before depending on it.

Coordination latency: deep orchestrator hierarchies add >=6s before workers start (2s/level) - bad for interactive mobile/WhatsApp UX. Keep hierarchies shallow and prefer fan-out over deep nesting for latency-sensitive paths.

AutoGen deprecation: AutoGen is maintenance-mode; any dependency on it is technical debt. MAF is the successor but is .NET/Azure-leaning and may not fit our TS/desktop+mobile+P2P architecture cleanly.

technicalOptions.option: Durable engine: Trigger.dev v4
technicalOptions.pros: TS-native (matches our stack), checkpointed durable tasks with no timeouts, pausable HITL that snapshots/restores so you don't pay during waits, warm starts 100-300ms, per-tenant concurrency keys, generous free tier (20 concurrent runs). Lowest ceremony.
technicalOptions.cons: Younger/smaller than Temporal; fully-managed cloud dependence (self-host less battle-tested); step/run-based billing can climb with heavy parallel LLM workloads.

technicalOptions.option: Durable engine: Inngest + AgentKit
technicalOptions.pros: Event-driven primitives map cleanly to agent patterns; native async/await step.run(); AgentKit gives multi-agent networks with deterministic state routing over Claude/OpenAI/Gemini; useAgent React hook streams durable runs to browser/mobile; native HITL; 50k step-runs/mo free.
technicalOptions.cons: Step-based pricing can scale unpredictably under LLM retries; orchestration is event-centric which can feel indirect for sequential planner-executor flows.

technicalOptions.option: Durable engine: Temporal
technicalOptions.pros: Most battle-tested (9.1T actions, $5B valuation), deterministic replay, full inspect/replay/rewind, multi-language SDKs, best for multi-week mission-critical workflows; self-host free.
technicalOptions.cons: Heavy ceremony; proxied runtime complicates debugging; LLM payloads saturate workflow history (needs codecs); Cloud from $100/mo and overage ~$50/M actions; overkill for short interactive agent turns.

technicalOptions.option: Orchestration framework: Claude Agent SDK as orchestrator
technicalOptions.pros: Native to our primary engine; worktree isolation, subagents, hooks, deepest MCP support, local JSONL sessions (resumable/forkable), per-tool permissions. Minimal glue for a Claude-Code-primary platform.
technicalOptions.cons: Less explicit graph/pattern modeling than LangGraph; orchestration logic is code, not a declarative graph; new separate Agent SDK credit pool (June 15, 2026) to budget; primarily Anthropic-model-centric.

technicalOptions.option: Orchestration framework: LangGraph (as pattern spec layer)
technicalOptions.pros: Explicit graphs for supervisor/swarm/hierarchical/map-reduce; durable checkpointing; native static + dynamic HITL interrupts; large enterprise adoption; strong docs; MIT OSS.
technicalOptions.cons: Steepest learning curve; LangSmith/LangChain ecosystem lock-in; Platform standby-time + per-node + per-user billing adds up; another runtime to operate alongside Claude SDK.

technicalOptions.option: Review/judge model: GPT-5.1 (cross-family) vs second Claude pass (same-family)
technicalOptions.pros: GPT-5.1 as judge gives genuine cross-family bias reduction, independent failure modes, cheap (Codex Max $1.25/$10, base $0.625/$5, cached 10% auto, batch 50% off). Strong for catching Claude blind spots.
technicalOptions.cons: Adds a second vendor dependency and ToS surface; single GPT judge is still adversarially manipulable (mitigate with panel/PoLL); extra latency on the critical path; need prompt/format normalization between providers.

technicalOptions.option: Comms: WhatsApp official Business API vs unofficial libraries (Baileys/WAHA/whatsapp-web.js)
technicalOptions.pros: Official API: near-zero ban risk with policy compliance, supported, templates, quality rating. Unofficial: free, no Meta approval, full feature access, fine for respond-only (<2% bans).
technicalOptions.cons: Official: opt-in/template constraints, per-message/conversation fees, approval friction. Unofficial: 15-30% ban rate for proactive messaging, 2026 detection (fingerprinting/velocity/unanswered-count), Meta blocking third-party AI chatbots - real account-loss and platform-policy risk.

summary: As of mid-2026, Anthropic Agent Skills (SKILL.md + YAML frontmatter, three-tier progressive disclosure) are the de-facto cross-platform standard for packaging agent know-how: open-sourced Dec 18 2025, governed under the Agentic AI Foundation (146+ members by Feb 2026), adopted by Claude Code, OpenAI Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot, Cursor and 20+ tools. Distribution runs on two first-party rails: Claude Code plugin marketplaces (a plugin bundles skills/agents/hooks/MCP/LSP; install via /plugin install name@marketplace) and the Anthropic /v1/skills REST API (create/version/list/delete plus a /content endpoint that downloads any skill version as a zip, the cleanest primitive for on-demand fetch). True dynamic discovery (search a live catalog, pick, fetch, hot-load mid-session) is NOT yet first-class: the Agent SDK only discovers skills from the filesystem at session start with no programmatic register API, so the emerging pattern is an MCP-based skills server (skills-mcp, mcp-skillset, FastMCP Skills Provider) plus the official Skills-Over-MCP Working Group (SEP-2640) serving skills as MCP Resources. Third-party registries are exploding but mostly download-then-load with uneven quality, and the security model treats every skill as arbitrary code execution. For our platform the highest-leverage architecture is a curated registry exposed via an MCP skill-broker for search plus on-demand download into a per-session .claude/skills/ dir, gated behind a GPT review/scan step, while avoiding unofficial WhatsApp automation given Meta Jan 15 2026 ban on third-party AI chatbots over WhatsApp and 2-8 week account-ban risk.

keyFindings.point: SKILL.md format is minimal and strictly validated: only name and description are required frontmatter fields
keyFindings.detail: name: max 64 chars, lowercase letters/numbers/hyphens only, no XML tags, cannot contain reserved words anthropic/claude. description: non-empty, max 1024 chars, no XML tags, must state BOTH what the skill does AND when to use it. Body should stay under ~500 lines / ~5k tokens. A skill is a directory with SKILL.md; bundled files (REFERENCE.md, FORMS.md, scripts/) optional. This single-file-with-YAML-header format is the open standard adopted across 20+ tools.

keyFindings.point: Progressive disclosure is a 3-tier loading model with concrete token costs, enabling install of hundreds of skills
keyFindings.detail: Level 1 Metadata (name+description) always loaded at startup into system prompt, ~100 tokens/skill. Level 2 (SKILL.md body) loads only when triggered, under ~5k tokens, read via bash. Level 3+ (bundled resources/scripts) load only when referenced, effectively unlimited; scripts run via bash so CODE never enters context, only stdout. Implication: per-turn cost of a large catalog is dominated by Level-1 metadata (~10k tokens for 100 skills), so gate discovery behind a search tool instead of pre-loading all metadata.

keyFindings.point: The Anthropic Skills API (/v1/skills, beta header skills-2025-10-02) is the cleanest on-demand fetch primitive and includes a zip-download endpoint
keyFindings.detail: Endpoints: POST/GET/DELETE /v1/skills, POST/GET/DELETE /v1/skills/{id}/versions, and GET /v1/skills/{skill_id}/versions/{version}/content which downloads a skill version as a zip. Versions are Unix-epoch-timestamp strings (e.g. 1759178010641129); name/description/directory auto-extracted from the uploaded SKILL.md. Skills referenced at inference via skill_id in the Messages container parameter + code-exec tool. Requires three beta headers together: code-execution-2025-08-25, skills-2025-10-02, files-api-2025-04-14. API custom skills are workspace-wide; NOT ZDR-eligible.

keyFindings.point: Claude Code plugin marketplaces are the primary first-party distribution layer; a plugin bundles skills + agents + hooks + MCP + LSP + monitors
keyFindings.detail: Install non-interactively: claude plugin install name@marketplace --scope user|project|local. Marketplaces added via /plugin marketplace add owner/repo (GitHub), git URL, local path, or remote marketplace.json URL. Official claude-plugins-official auto-available; claude-plugins-community (commit-SHA pinned, auto safety-screened) added manually. Manifests: .claude-plugin/marketplace.json and .claude-plugin/plugin.json (only name required); CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT/CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA path vars; userConfig for prompted secrets (keychain ~2KB limit); native channels field models Telegram/Slack/Discord injection. Marketplace plugins are COPIED to ~/.claude/plugins/cache (no external file refs). /reload-plugins hot-applies installs, but MCP-bearing plugins invalidate prompt cache and need --force.

keyFindings.point: The Agent SDK does NOT support programmatic runtime skill registration; skills are filesystem-only and discovered at session start
keyFindings.detail: Skills must be created as filesystem artifacts; the SDK does not provide a programmatic API for registering Skills. Set setting_sources=[user,project] (default empty loads nothing) and a skills filter (all | name list | empty); the SDK auto-adds the Skill tool to allowedTools. The skills option is a context filter, not a sandbox. The plugins option can load skills from a specific path. Core gap for download-and-use-immediately: must materialize SKILL.md into the session .claude/skills/ then reload/restart, OR serve skills over MCP.

keyFindings.point: Dynamic, search-driven, hot-loadable skill discovery is being standardized via MCP; the official Skills-Over-MCP Working Group (SEP-2640) serves skills as MCP Resources
keyFindings.detail: modelcontextprotocol/experimental-ext-skills proposes serving how-to skills through MCP Resources so an agent connects once and gets automatic discovery, server-side dynamic updates (no client reinstall), multi-server composition, and RBAC/versioned distribution. Marked exploratory. Working prior art: skills-mcp (vector search using bge-small-en-v1.5 384-dim, paginated skills_find_relevant), mcp-skillset (hybrid vector + knowledge-graph RAG, on-demand loading when needed not at startup), FastMCP Skills Provider (skill://name/SKILL.md + _manifest with SHA256 hashes; client utils list_skills/download_skill/sync_skills).

keyFindings.point: Agent Skills became an open, vendor-neutral standard on Dec 18 2025, making artifacts portable and reducing lock-in
keyFindings.detail: Anthropic released the SKILL.md spec + SDK as an open standard; governed cross-platform (agentskills.io) and housed under the Agentic AI Foundation (146 member orgs by Feb 2026). A skill authored for Claude runs unchanged in OpenAI Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Cline, Windsurf, OpenCode. The artifact is portable; the distribution/runtime (plugin marketplaces, /v1/skills, code-exec container) remains Anthropic-specific.

keyFindings.point: Runtime environment for skills differs sharply by surface; Claude Code has full network access, the API container has NONE
keyFindings.detail: Claude API skill container: NO network access, NO runtime package installation, only pre-installed packages. claude.ai: varying network access per admin settings. Claude Code: full network access (same as any program on the user machine), global installs discouraged. Matters for image/video studio and comms skills: any skill calling an external API (Telegram, render farm, model API) must run in Claude Code / Agent SDK on our host, not in the managed API code-exec container.

keyFindings.point: Security model: every skill equals arbitrary code/instructions; Anthropic says treat like installing software, and only official/community marketplaces do any screening
keyFindings.detail: Risks called out: data exfiltration, unauthorized system access, tool misuse, prompt-injection via skills that fetch external URLs (even trustworthy skills can be compromised if dependencies change). Plugins/marketplaces can execute arbitrary code with user privileges. Community marketplace pins each plugin to a commit SHA + automated validation; GitHub-scraped catalogs (SkillsMP 800k skills, min 2 stars) have no meaningful review. Biggest risk surface for a platform installing third-party skills on users' behalf.

keyFindings.point: Cost levers for an agentic skills platform are concrete and stack on top of token pricing
keyFindings.detail: Code execution tool: 50 free container-hours/day per org, then $0.05/hour per container (billed by wall-clock). Managed Agents: $0.08 per session-hour while running. Web search tool: $10 per 1,000 searches. These compound fast in workflows chaining multiple tools per request. Skills add no separate fee but Level-1 metadata is a recurring per-turn token cost. Budget the scheduler/per-project automation around container-hours.

keyFindings.point: WhatsApp automation carries severe, escalating ban risk and a new Meta policy directly targets AI chatbots
keyFindings.detail: Effective Jan 15 2026 Meta blocks third-party general-purpose AI chatbots (ChatGPT, Copilot, Perplexity) from WhatsApp Business API; only business-workflow automation (support, orders, bookings, notifications) remains permitted. Unofficial QR-pairing libs (Baileys, Evolution API, WA-Automate, OpenClaw) carry high-to-critical ban risk: accounts survive 2-8 weeks, ban waves every 2-3 months. Official path = Meta Cloud API via registered BSP at EUR 0.03-0.15 per conversation. Telegram has a sanctioned, well-documented Bot API, fundamentally safer.

keyFindings.point: Third-party skill/MCP registries are large but fragmented and mostly download-then-load, not live-search
keyFindings.detail: tonsofskills.com: 425 plugins / 2,810 skills / 200 agents, ccpi CLI (pnpm add -g @intentsolutionsio/ccpi; ccpi search/install/list/update). skills.sh: ~2,000 skills via npx skills add. claudeskills.info: 658 free community skills. SkillsMP: indexes 800k+ GitHub skills (min 2 stars). Agensi: ~200 curated/security-scanned skills, curl one-liner, 80/20 creator split, claims MCP live access. MCP registries: Smithery 7,000+ servers (smithery mcp search/add/list + Registry API), PulseMCP 11,840+ (hand-reviewed daily), mcp.so 19,700+ community-submitted.

itemsOrTools.name: Anthropic Skills API (/v1/skills)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: REST API to create, version, list, delete custom skills and download any version as a zip via GET /v1/skills/{skill_id}/versions/{version}/content. Skills referenced at inference via skill_id in the Messages container param + code-exec tool. Beta header skills-2025-10-02.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Cleanest first-party primitive for fetching the right skill on demand. Our skill-broker can mirror our registry into per-workspace skill_ids and pull the zip when a job needs it. Caveat: API container has NO network access, so network-dependent skills must run in Claude Code/SDK instead.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Anthropic API key. Skills add no per-skill fee; code-exec container $0.05/hr (50 free hrs/day/org). Not ZDR-eligible.

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Code Plugins + Marketplaces
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Distribution layer: a plugin bundles skills/agents/hooks/MCP/LSP/monitors/themes/channels. Install via claude plugin install name@marketplace --scope user|project|local or /plugin install. Marketplaces from GitHub/git/local/URL. /reload-plugins hot-applies; auto-update supported. plugin.json/marketplace.json manifests; CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT/CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA path vars; userConfig prompts (secrets to keychain); native channels field for Telegram/Slack/Discord injection.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Native per-project skill/capability delivery with built-in scoping (project scope shares via git to all collaborators) and a channels primitive that maps directly to our WhatsApp/Telegram requirement. Our own marketplace can be a private git repo auto-installed per project via extraKnownMarketplaces.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free/OSS mechanism, bundled with Claude Code. Self-host a marketplace as a git repo or marketplace.json URL.

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Agent SDK (skills loading)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Programmatic engine (TS @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk, Python claude_agent_sdk). Skills discovered from filesystem when setting_sources includes user/project; skills option (all | name list | empty) filters and auto-adds the Skill tool. No programmatic skill-register API, must write SKILL.md to disk. plugins option loads skills from a path. allowed-tools frontmatter ignored in SDK (use allowedTools option).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Our primary engine. Key constraint: to load a downloaded skill we must materialize it into .claude/skills/ before/at session start; mid-session hot-add is not first-class. Drives the architecture toward an MCP skill-broker or a write-to-disk-then-reload loop.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS SDK; pays standard model + tool token/container fees.

itemsOrTools.name: Skills-Over-MCP Working Group (experimental-ext-skills, SEP-2640)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Official MCP-org effort to serve skills as MCP Resources so agents connect once and get automatic discovery, server-side dynamic updates (no reinstall), multi-server composition, and RBAC/versioned distribution. Exploratory, not yet ratified spec.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The standards track that matches our exact requirement (search live catalog, pick, load on demand). Build our skill-broker against this shape now so we are forward-compatible when SEP-2640 lands. Lowest-lock-in path to dynamic discovery.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS / open spec.

itemsOrTools.name: FastMCP Skills Provider
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Exposes a skills directory over MCP: skill://name/SKILL.md, skill://name/_manifest (JSON of files + sizes + SHA256), skill://name/path resources. Client utils list_skills, download_skill, sync_skills, get_skill_manifest. reload=True for live re-scan. Vendor providers pre-target ~/.claude/skills/, ~/.cursor/skills/.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Production-ready building block for our skill-broker MCP server: manifest-driven discovery, integrity hashes (supply-chain safety), and a sync-to-disk path that feeds the Agent SDK filesystem loader. Strong candidate to implement the download-on-demand-then-SDK-loads-it bridge.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS (FastMCP, Python).

itemsOrTools.name: skills-mcp / mcp-skillset
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: skills-mcp: MCP server with semantic/vector search over a skill catalog (bge-small-en-v1.5 384-dim; paginated skills_find_relevant returning ~100-200 token descriptors). mcp-skillset (bobmatnyc): hybrid vector + knowledge-graph RAG, runtime discovery, toolchain-aware auto-recommendation, on-demand loading when needed not at startup.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Direct prior art for the search-and-pick step. Vector-search-over-descriptions keeps Level-1 metadata cost flat as the catalog grows past ~50 skills. Note: at 300+ skills a single find_relevant call with high top_k can itself eat meaningful context, so tune top_k.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS.

itemsOrTools.name: tonsofskills.com + ccpi CLI
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Open-source marketplace: 425 plugins / 2,810 skills / 200 agents (incl. 42 SaaS packs / 1,086 skills). ccpi package manager (pnpm add -g @intentsolutionsio/ccpi) with ccpi search/install/list --installed/update.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Largest open Claude-native skill catalog and a working npm-style package-manager model to study/mirror. Useful as a seed corpus and reference CLI UX for our own registry. Unreviewed content, must scan before exposing to users.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS / free; npm package @intentsolutionsio/ccpi.

itemsOrTools.name: Smithery / PulseMCP / mcp.so
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: MCP server registries. Smithery: 7,000+ servers, hosted remote servers, one-click install, CLI (smithery mcp search/add/list) + Registry API (programmatic search to connection URLs). PulseMCP: 11,840+ hand-reviewed servers. mcp.so: 19,700+ community-submitted.
itemsOrTools.relevance: For the MCP-tool side of our capability marketplace (vs the SKILL.md side). Smithery Registry API models programmatic search-to-launch-config that our host could call to wire up tools on demand. Quality varies; prefer reviewed (PulseMCP) or hosted (Smithery) for production.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free discovery/registry; Smithery offers hosted remote servers (some premium). No published per-skill pricing.

itemsOrTools.name: skills.sh / claudeskills.info / SkillsMP / Agensi
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Download-based skill directories. skills.sh ~2,000 skills, npx skills add (Claude Code/Codex/Cursor/OpenClaw). claudeskills.info 658 free community skills. SkillsMP indexes 800k+ GitHub skills (min 2 stars). Agensi ~200 curated security-scanned skills, curl one-liner, 80/20 creator split, claims MCP live access.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Breadth sources to seed/benchmark our curated registry. Agensi is closest to our curated + security-scanned + creator-payments + live-MCP target model. SkillsMP shows the scale and the noise of the open ecosystem, never expose unscanned.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Mostly free; Agensi one-time purchase or free, 80/20 split.

itemsOrTools.name: Agent Skills open standard (agentskills.io)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Vendor-neutral SKILL.md spec (open-sourced Dec 18 2025), governed under the Agentic AI Foundation (146+ members). Same skill runs in Claude Code, OpenAI Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Cline, Windsurf, OpenCode, 20+ tools.
itemsOrTools.relevance: De-risks lock-in at the artifact layer: curated skills are portable across engines, so our GPT review model (Codex CLI / OpenAI) consumes the same skills as Claude Code. The distribution/runtime layer still differs per engine, abstract behind our skill-broker.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Open spec, free.

itemsOrTools.name: WhatsApp Cloud API (Meta, via BSP) vs Telegram Bot API
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Cloud API: official Meta channel, per-conversation pricing by category. Jan 15 2026 policy blocks third-party general-purpose AI chatbots; business-workflow automation still allowed. Telegram Bot API: official, sanctioned, well-documented bot automation.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Defines the safe boundary for our comms layer. Unofficial QR-pairing (Baileys/Evolution/OpenClaw) = 2-8 week account-ban risk + violates Meta new AI-chatbot ban, do NOT use for the consumer assistant. Lead with Telegram; use WhatsApp only via Cloud API + registered BSP scoped to business workflows.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: WhatsApp Cloud API EUR 0.03-0.15/conversation by category/country, needs BSP. Telegram Bot API free.

technicalOptions.option: A) Plugin-marketplace rail: ship our skills as Claude Code plugins in a curated private marketplace (git repo), auto-installed per project via extraKnownMarketplaces, hot-applied with /reload-plugins
technicalOptions.pros: Native, zero custom infra; built-in scoping (project scope shares to all collaborators), versioning (semver or commit-SHA), auto-update, commit-SHA pinning for safety; native channels field maps to WhatsApp/Telegram; bundles skills+agents+hooks+MCP+LSP in one unit; per-component token-cost preview built in.
technicalOptions.cons: Discovery is install-time, not live-search; plugins are copied to cache (no external file refs); MCP-bearing plugins invalidate prompt cache on reload (need --force); tied to Claude Code/SDK runtime; curating a quality marketplace is our burden.

technicalOptions.option: B) MCP skill-broker rail: our own MCP server exposes search_skills (vector search) + download_skill (writes SKILL.md into the session .claude/skills/) + integrity manifest, modeled on FastMCP Skills Provider / SEP-2640
technicalOptions.pros: True dynamic discovery (search a live catalog, pick, fetch on demand); flat Level-1 token cost (search vs pre-loading all metadata); SHA256 manifests for supply-chain safety; engine-agnostic (works for Claude Code AND GPT/Codex review model); forward-compatible with the official Skills-Over-MCP standard; server-side updates without client reinstall.
technicalOptions.cons: Agent SDK has no mid-session register API, so download still needs write-to-disk + reload/new-session to take effect; we own the search infra (embeddings, ranking, caching); SEP-2640 still experimental and may shift; reload cost on prompt cache.

technicalOptions.option: C) Skills API rail: mirror our registry into Anthropic /v1/skills, fetch versions via the /content zip endpoint, reference by skill_id in the Messages container
technicalOptions.pros: First-party, workspace-wide sharing, clean versioned zip download, no filesystem juggling for pure-inference jobs; managed code-exec sandbox.
technicalOptions.cons: API skill container has NO network access (kills any skill calling Telegram/render/model APIs); not ZDR-eligible; Anthropic-specific (lock-in); skills do not sync across claude.ai/API/Claude Code surfaces; less suited to our network-heavy studio/comms skills.

technicalOptions.option: D) Hybrid (recommended): MCP skill-broker (B) for search + on-demand fetch, materializing into per-project .claude/skills/ consumed by the Agent SDK (A-style), with a GPT/Codex review-model gate scanning every skill before it is exposed, and the open standard keeping artifacts portable
technicalOptions.pros: Combines live discovery (B) with native SDK loading (A); GPT review step mitigates the arbitrary-code-execution risk; portable SKILL.md artifacts reduce lock-in; can fall back to /v1/skills (C) for pure-inference document skills; aligns with where the standard is heading.
technicalOptions.cons: Most engineering to build (broker + embeddings + review pipeline + reload orchestration); must solve mid-session reload UX; ongoing curation + security-scan cost; multiple moving parts to keep in sync.

recommendations: Adopt the hybrid architecture (Option D): build an MCP skill-broker server (modeled on FastMCP Skills Provider + SEP-2640) exposing search_skills (vector search over name+description so per-turn metadata cost stays flat past ~50 skills) and download_skill that writes the SKILL.md bundle into the active project .claude/skills/ for the Agent SDK to load. Track SEP-2640 so we are drop-in compatible when it ratifies.

Author and curate skills as standard portable SKILL.md (name max 64 chars lowercase-hyphen; description max 1024 chars stating what+when; body under 500 lines; heavy reference in Level-3 bundled files/scripts executed via bash, never inlined). This keeps artifacts usable by BOTH Claude Code (primary engine) and the GPT/Codex review model, minimizing lock-in.

Run every third-party or user-submitted skill through a mandatory GPT-based security review + static scan BEFORE it enters our catalog: flag external URL fetches, unexpected network calls, and tool use that does not match the stated purpose. Pin catalog entries to content hashes (FastMCP _manifest SHA256) and to commit SHAs. Treat skill install as installing software with the user privileges.

For comms, lead with Telegram Bot API (official, free, sanctioned) and treat WhatsApp as business-workflow-only via Meta Cloud API + a registered BSP. Do NOT build the consumer assistant on unofficial WhatsApp libraries (Baileys/Evolution/OpenClaw); they violate Meta Jan 15 2026 third-party-AI-chatbot ban and incur 2-8 week account-ban waves. Use the plugin channels field to model both uniformly.

Budget and instrument tool costs in the per-project scheduler: code-exec containers bill $0.05/container-hour by wall-clock (50 free hrs/day/org), managed agents $0.08/session-hour, web search $10/1k. Keep network-dependent skills (studio, comms, scheduler) on Claude Code/Agent SDK hosts (full network access), and reserve the API code-exec container (no network) only for self-contained document/data skills.

For per-project distribution use Claude Code project-scope plugins (extraKnownMarketplaces in .claude/settings.json) so a workspace auto-offers its curated skill set to every collaborator on trust; use commit-SHA versioning during active dev (auto-updates each commit) switching to explicit semver for published/stable skills. Mind that MCP-bearing reloads invalidate the prompt cache.
risks: Security / arbitrary code execution: Anthropic explicitly warns skills and plugins run arbitrary code with the user privileges and that even trusted skills can be compromised when external dependencies change. A platform auto-installing third-party skills on users' behalf is the single largest attack surface (data exfiltration, prompt injection via fetched URLs, tool misuse). Mandatory pre-ingest review + hash pinning is non-negotiable.

WhatsApp ban + policy risk: Meta Jan 15 2026 ban on third-party general-purpose AI chatbots over WhatsApp directly targets our use case; unofficial QR-pairing automation has 2-8 week account survival and recurring ban waves. Misjudging this could brick users' primary phone numbers and expose us to ToS liability.

Dynamic-loading gap / standard immaturity: The Agent SDK has no programmatic mid-session skill-register API, and the MCP Skills standard (SEP-2640) is explicitly experimental. Our download-and-use-immediately UX must engineer around write-to-disk + reload, and the standard may shift under us, forcing rework.

Token-cost creep at scale: Level-1 skill metadata is loaded every turn (~100 tok/skill); a large pre-loaded catalog silently inflates per-turn cost, and even search-based discovery warns a single find_relevant call at 300+ skills with high top_k can eat meaningful context. Combined with $0.05/hr containers and $10/1k searches, agentic chains can balloon cost.

Vendor lock-in at the runtime layer: while SKILL.md artifacts are portable, the distribution/runtime (plugin marketplaces, /v1/skills, code-exec container, beta headers like skills-2025-10-02 that change over time) is Anthropic-specific and beta; breaking changes and surface fragmentation (skills do not sync across claude.ai/API/Claude Code) are likely. Abstract behind our broker.

Registry quality + supply chain: open catalogs are huge but largely unreviewed (SkillsMP 800k GitHub-scraped min 2 stars; tonsofskills/skills.sh community-driven). Pulling skills directly without curation imports malware, prompt-injection, and abandonware risk. Marketplace plugins are copied to cache and cannot reference external files, which also breaks naive cross-repo skill designs.

domain: Model Context Protocol (MCP) ecosystem in 2026: transports, registries/marketplaces, dynamic tool discovery & lazy schema loading, security/sandboxing of third-party tools, and scoped per-project/per-job server attachment
summary: As of mid-2026 MCP is the de facto interop layer for AI agents, adopted by Anthropic (Claude Code/Desktop/API), OpenAI (Apps SDK, Agents SDK, Responses API), Google (Gemini CLI), Microsoft, Docker, and Cloudflare. The current spec revision is 2025-11-25 (prior: 2025-06-18), which standardizes two transports — stdio (local subprocess, newline-delimited JSON-RPC) and Streamable HTTP (single POST/GET endpoint with optional SSE streaming, replacing the deprecated 2024-11-05 HTTP+SSE transport) — and adds OAuth 2.1 hardening (OpenID Connect Discovery, incremental scope consent via WWW-Authenticate, Client ID Metadata Documents), tool/resource icons, experimental durable "tasks", and JSON Schema 2020-12 as the default dialect. The biggest 2026 shift is solving "context bloat": loading hundreds of tool schemas can consume 50-70K+ tokens before the first query, so Anthropic shipped the Tool Search Tool to GA (Feb 2026) with defer_loading, cutting startup tokens ~85% and lifting Opus 4.5 tool-selection accuracy from 79.5% to 88.1%. Discovery has consolidated around the official open-source MCP Registry (registry.modelcontextprotocol.io, launched preview Sept 2025, reverse-DNS namespaces) plus large third-party catalogs (Glama ~21K, PulseMCP ~12K, Smithery ~7K, Docker MCP Catalog 300+ signed images). Security is the dominant risk: scans of thousands of public servers found ~41% of official-registry servers with zero auth and high rates of SSRF/command-injection, driving adoption of MCP gateways (Docker MCP Gateway, MintMCP, Lunar MCPX, IBM ContextForge) for container isolation, RBAC, tool filtering, and per-team/per-agent scoping — the exact primitives a universal multi-tenant agent platform needs. The space is moving fast; treat beta headers, spec revisions, and registry APIs as moving targets.

keyFindings.point: Two standardized transports; Streamable HTTP is the remote default and SSE-only transport is deprecated
keyFindings.detail: MCP 2025-11-25 defines stdio (host spawns server as child process, newline-delimited JSON-RPC over stdin/stdout; stderr allowed for all logging) and Streamable HTTP (single MCP endpoint supporting POST+GET, optional Server-Sent Events to stream multiple messages; servers must return HTTP 403 for invalid Origin headers; GET streams now support polling/resumption per SEP-1699). The legacy HTTP+SSE transport from 2024-11-05 is deprecated. For our platform: use stdio for locally bundled tools (desktop/mobile-host) and Streamable HTTP for remote/hosted/marketplace servers and P2P-shared servers.

keyFindings.point: Current spec is 2025-11-25; major adds are OAuth/authorization hardening, tool icons, experimental durable tasks, and JSON Schema 2020-12
keyFindings.detail: Versus 2025-06-18: OpenID Connect Discovery 1.0 for auth-server discovery (PR#797); incremental scope consent via WWW-Authenticate (SEP-835); OAuth Client ID Metadata Documents as recommended client registration (SEP-991); RFC 9728 alignment making WWW-Authenticate optional with .well-known fallback; icons for tools/resources/prompts (SEP-973); experimental 'tasks' for durable requests with polling + deferred result retrieval (SEP-1686) — directly relevant to a per-project scheduler and long video renders; URL-mode elicitation (SEP-1036); tool-calling in sampling via tools/toolChoice (SEP-1577); JSON Schema 2020-12 default (SEP-1613); input-validation errors returned as Tool Execution Errors (not protocol errors) so the model can self-correct (SEP-1303).

keyFindings.point: Dynamic tool discovery is native to the protocol via notifications/tools/list_changed + listChanged capability
keyFindings.detail: Servers declaring the listChanged capability emit notifications/tools/list_changed when their tool set changes at runtime; clients then re-call tools/list. This lets a server expose different tools by user context/scope without reconnect — the protocol primitive behind per-project/per-job scoped tool sets. Note client support varies: Gemini CLI tracked an open issue (#13850) to honor list_changed, so don't assume every client re-fetches.

keyFindings.point: Context bloat is the defining 2026 scaling problem; Anthropic's Tool Search Tool (GA Feb 2026) is the leading fix
keyFindings.detail: Real-world data: a 4-server setup burns ~51K tokens (~47% of a 200K window) and 7+ servers exceed 67K tokens before any user input; reports cite up to ~72% of context consumed by tool schemas pre-query (Perplexity publicly dropped MCP internally citing this). Anthropic's Tool Search Tool: mark tools defer_loading:true so only a lightweight search interface + critical (defer_loading:false) tools load initially; Claude searches (regex/BM25/custom) and matched tool schemas expand on demand. ~85% startup-token reduction (e.g. 75K to ~8K); Opus 4 tool-selection accuracy 49% to 74%, Opus 4.5 79.5% to 88.1%. MCP toolsets can be deferred via default_config with the mcp-client-2025-11-20 beta header; the feature itself needs advanced-tool-use-2025-11-20. Caveat: still public beta; some report it's not yet reliable for complex multi-tool marketing-style workflows.

keyFindings.point: The official open-source MCP Registry is the canonical machine-readable discovery layer; large third-party catalogs sit on top
keyFindings.detail: registry.modelcontextprotocol.io (preview Sept 8 2025; backed by Anthropic, GitHub, PulseMCP, Microsoft) is an open-source, API-accessible 'source of truth' using reverse-DNS namespaces (io.github.user/server, com.example/server) tied to verified GitHub/domain ownership for anti-spoofing. Distinguish registries (machine-readable, for clients to discover programmatically) from directories (human-browsable). Third-party scale: Glama ~21K servers w/ visual previews + daily updates, PulseMCP ~12K hand-reviewed daily, Smithery ~7K w/ app-store UX + one-click install + hosted remote servers, mcp.so ~5K, Docker MCP Catalog 300+ signed/attested container images.

keyFindings.point: Public MCP servers are alarmingly insecure; tool poisoning, rug-pulls, SSRF, and unauthenticated exposure are mainstream attack classes
keyFindings.detail: Scans of 8,000+ public servers: 36.7% SSRF-vulnerable, 43% unsafe command-execution paths, 41% of official-registry servers with ZERO authentication. Censys found 12,520 internet-exposed MCP services, most unauthenticated, many exposing DB-query/command-exec directly. Named threats: prompt injection, tool poisoning (malicious instructions hidden in tool descriptions/metadata the model reads but the user doesn't), rug-pull (tool description changes maliciously after install), token-passthrough abuse (accepting tokens not issued for the server); 9 CVEs traced to broken OAuth flows. OWASP now publishes an MCP Security Cheat Sheet. Mandatory controls: OAuth 2.1 + PKCE, capability-level scoping, input validation/sanitization of tool metadata, container sandboxing, and human approval for destructive calls.

keyFindings.point: MCP gateways are the standard pattern for sandboxing third-party tools and attaching scoped server/tool sets per project/team/agent
keyFindings.detail: Docker MCP Gateway (open source, docker mcp CLI plugin): runs each server in an isolated container with restricted privileges/network/CPU/memory limits, lifecycle management, call-tracing/logging, and runtime signature verification (docker mcp gateway run --verify-signatures against signed SBOMs/build attestations). MintMCP: OAuth2/SAML/SSO + SCIM-driven RBAC, 'Virtual MCP Bundles' (per-use-case endpoints exposing only minimum required tools, role-based + per-tool permissions) and 'Agent Bundles' (M2M auth, scoped tools with independent rotation/revocation) — a near-exact match for per-project/per-job scoping. Lunar MCPX: tool-level RBAC for teams sharing servers but needing different tool subsets. IBM ContextForge (mcp-context-forge) and Obot are other enterprise gateways. Gateways also enable tool filtering to cut context bloat by exposing only relevant tools.

keyFindings.point: Claude Code supports three config scopes incl. project-shared .mcp.json; this is our blueprint for per-project scoped servers
keyFindings.detail: claude mcp add --scope project --transport stdio my-db -- npx ... writes a committable .mcp.json at project root; scopes are local/user/project with project overriding user. .mcp.json references env vars (e.g. ${VAR}) so secrets aren't hardcoded — each dev/runtime supplies its own. Guidance: commit only servers with shared or no credentials; use --scope user for personal-credential servers. Claude Code also has a plugin system (.claude-plugin/marketplace.json) bundling Skills + MCP server configs + hooks, distributed via git — the model for our dynamic skills marketplace.

keyFindings.point: Anthropic MCP Connector lets the Claude Messages API call remote MCP servers with no separate client, and supports tool allow/deny lists
keyFindings.detail: mcp_servers parameter on /v1/messages connects to URL-addressable remote servers; beta header anthropic-beta: mcp-client-2025-11-20. Supports enable-all / allowlist / denylist tool configuration — built-in scoping. Entered public beta Feb 2026 (free + paid). No separate MCP-connector pricing; you pay normal token costs. Current Claude models/pricing (per 1M tok, in/out): Opus 4.8 claude-opus-4-8 $5/$25 (1M ctx); Opus 4.7 claude-opus-4-7; Sonnet 4.6 claude-sonnet-4-6; Haiku 4.5 claude-haiku-4-5 (200K ctx); Fable 5 claude-fable-5 is the most capable, thinking always on. Use claude-opus-4-8 as the primary engine.
