keyFindings.point: OpenAI fully supports MCP across Apps SDK, Agents SDK, and Responses API — viable for the GPT review-model role
keyFindings.detail: Apps SDK is built on MCP and extends it so developers design both logic and UI of ChatGPT apps. Agents SDK has MCP support built in (no extra client lib). Responses API supports remote MCP servers + code interpreter as built-in tools. OpenAI also shipped a Secure MCP Tunnel for enterprises so ChatGPT web/Codex/Responses API/AgentKit reach private/on-prem MCP servers without public exposure — relevant if GPT-as-reviewer must reach the same scoped tools. This means a single MCP server can serve both Claude (engine) and GPT (reviewer).

keyFindings.point: Cloudflare is a low-cost path to host remote MCP servers with built-in OAuth and hibernation-based billing
keyFindings.detail: Cloudflare Workers + Agents SDK deploy Streamable-HTTP remote MCP servers at 300+ edge locations with built-in OAuth 2.1 (Cloudflare Access or third-party IdP as provider, scoped tokens), Durable Objects for per-session state, and a McpAgent class using WebSockets Hibernation API so you pay compute only while the agent works. Free tier: 100K requests/day Workers + 100K/day Durable Objects (SQLite-backed DO only on free); Paid plan $5/mo minimum. Good fit for hosting our marketplace/job-scoped servers cheaply with per-user OAuth.

itemsOrTools.name: Official MCP Registry
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Open-source, API-accessible canonical registry of public MCP servers using reverse-DNS namespaces tied to verified GitHub/domain ownership; backed by Anthropic, GitHub, PulseMCP, Microsoft. Preview launched Sept 8 2025.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The trustworthy upstream 'source of truth' to seed and continuously sync our dynamic skills/tools marketplace; namespace verification reduces spoofing/typosquatting of marketplace listings.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free, open source (github.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry). REST API at registry.modelcontextprotocol.io; self-hostable.

itemsOrTools.name: Anthropic Tool Search Tool (defer_loading)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Lazy schema loading: mark tools/MCP toolsets defer_loading:true; Claude loads only a search interface + critical tools, then fetches matched schemas on demand (regex/BM25/custom search). ~85% startup-token cut; +accuracy.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Essential for a universal platform with hundreds of marketplace tools across many projects — keeps Claude Code's context lean while exposing a huge tool catalog per project/job.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Public beta via Claude API; headers advanced-tool-use-2025-11-20 (+ mcp-client-2025-11-20 to defer MCP tools). No surcharge; normal token billing. GA'd Feb 2026.

itemsOrTools.name: Anthropic MCP Connector (Messages API)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Connect Claude directly to remote (URL) MCP servers from /v1/messages with no separate MCP client; supports enable-all/allowlist/denylist tool scoping.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Lets our Claude Code engine attach scoped, per-project remote tool sets server-side with built-in allow/deny filtering — minimal client plumbing.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Public beta; header anthropic-beta: mcp-client-2025-11-20. No separate fee; token cost only. Primary model claude-opus-4-8 ($5/$25 per 1M, 1M ctx).

itemsOrTools.name: Docker MCP Catalog + Toolkit + Gateway
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: 300+ verified MCP servers as signed/attested container images; Gateway (open-source CLI plugin) runs each server in an isolated container with privilege/network/CPU/memory limits, lifecycle mgmt, call-tracing, and runtime signature verification.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best off-the-shelf sandboxing for untrusted third-party/marketplace tools on the desktop host; signature verification + per-container isolation directly mitigate rug-pull, command-injection, and resource-exhaustion risks.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Gateway open source (github.com/docker/mcp-gateway); Catalog free. Requires Docker Desktop/Engine. docker mcp gateway run --verify-signatures.

itemsOrTools.name: MintMCP Gateway
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Enterprise MCP gateway with OAuth2/SAML/SSO + SCIM-driven RBAC, 'Virtual MCP Bundles' (per-use-case endpoints exposing minimum tools, per-tool permissions) and 'Agent Bundles' (M2M auth, scoped tools with independent rotation/revocation).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Near-exact reference architecture for our per-project/per-job scoped server attachment and multi-tenant tool permissioning; Agent Bundles map to per-job agent identities with revocable scoped tools.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Commercial SaaS/self-host (pricing on request via mintmcp.com). Closed-source.

itemsOrTools.name: Lunar MCPX / IBM ContextForge / Obot
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Alternative MCP gateways: Lunar MCPX offers tool-level RBAC for teams sharing servers but needing different tool subsets; IBM mcp-context-forge and Obot provide enterprise routing, policy, audit. Obot publishes a '13 best gateways' comparison.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Open-source/self-host options (ContextForge) for tool-level scoping and audit without vendor lock-in; gives a build-vs-buy spectrum for our gateway layer.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: ContextForge open source (github.com/IBM/mcp-context-forge); Lunar/Obot mixed OSS+commercial.

itemsOrTools.name: Glama / PulseMCP / Smithery / mcp.so (directories)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Human-browsable catalogs: Glama ~21K servers + visual previews, PulseMCP ~12K hand-reviewed daily, Smithery ~7K with app-store UX, one-click install, hosted remote servers + Registry API, mcp.so ~5K.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Sources to populate/curate our marketplace UI; Smithery's Registry API + hosted infra (handles OAuth/token-refresh/sessions) is a model for our hosted-server offering and a potential aggregation backend.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free to browse; Smithery has a Registry API + CLI (github.com/smithery-ai/cli) and hosted deploys (pricing on smithery.ai). Quality/security varies — vet before listing.

itemsOrTools.name: Cloudflare Workers + Agents SDK (remote MCP hosting)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Deploy Streamable-HTTP MCP servers at the edge with built-in OAuth 2.1 (scoped tokens), Durable Objects state, and McpAgent WebSockets Hibernation so you pay compute only while active.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Cheap, globally distributed, OAuth-secured hosting for our marketplace/job-scoped servers; hibernation billing suits bursty per-job tool usage; good fit for mobile + P2P reach.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free tier 100K req/day Workers + 100K/day Durable Objects (SQLite DO only); Paid $5/mo minimum. Agents SDK open source.

itemsOrTools.name: OpenAI MCP (Apps SDK / Agents SDK / Responses API / Secure MCP Tunnel)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Full MCP support: Apps SDK (MCP + UI), Agents SDK (built-in MCP client), Responses API (remote MCP as built-in tool), Secure MCP Tunnel (reach private/on-prem servers without public exposure).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Lets the GPT review model consume the SAME scoped MCP servers as Claude — one server definition serves both engine and reviewer; Secure Tunnel matters if the reviewer must reach private project tools.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Included in OpenAI API/ChatGPT; token-billed. Agents SDK open source.

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Code project-scoped .mcp.json + plugin marketplaces
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Three config scopes (local/user/project); project scope writes a committable .mcp.json with env-var-referenced secrets. Plugin system (.claude-plugin/marketplace.json) bundles Skills + MCP configs + hooks, distributed via git.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Direct blueprint for per-project scoped server attachment and our dynamic skills marketplace distribution format; env-var indirection is the secret-handling pattern to copy.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Included with Claude Code. claude mcp add --scope project --transport <stdio|http> ...

recommendations: Adopt a gateway-first architecture: never let Claude Code or the GPT reviewer talk to third-party/marketplace MCP servers directly. Route everything through a gateway (Docker MCP Gateway for local container isolation; a MintMCP/ContextForge-style layer for remote RBAC) that enforces per-project/per-job scoping, tool allow/deny lists, audit logging, and signature verification.

Model per-project/per-job scoping on Claude Code's project scope + MintMCP 'Bundles': each project gets a committable manifest (like .mcp.json) listing its allowed servers/tools with env-var secret indirection; each scheduled job gets a short-lived 'Agent Bundle' identity with M2M auth and independently revocable scoped tokens, so a rogue tool can't escalate beyond that job.

Make Tool Search / defer_loading the default for every project so the marketplace can expose hundreds of tools without blowing Claude's context. Keep only ~5-15 critical tools as defer_loading:false per project; everything else discoverable on demand. Budget for it being beta and add a deterministic fallback (gateway-side tool filtering) for workflows where search mis-selects.

Use the official MCP Registry as the trusted upstream and sync it into our marketplace, preferring reverse-DNS-verified namespaces; layer Glama/PulseMCP/Smithery only as enrichment. Require namespace/domain verification and a security scan gate before any server is listable or installable in our marketplace.

Standardize on Streamable HTTP for all hosted/remote/marketplace/P2P servers (use stdio only for tools bundled into the desktop/mobile host as sandboxed subprocesses). This maximizes client compatibility (Claude, GPT, Gemini) and lets a single server serve both engine and reviewer.

Host our first-party and curated marketplace servers on Cloudflare Workers + Agents SDK for cheap, edge-distributed, OAuth-secured deployment with hibernation billing — ideal for bursty per-job tool usage and mobile/P2P reach; reserve Docker-container sandboxing for running untrusted servers locally on the desktop client.

Pin spec/beta versions explicitly (transport = Streamable HTTP per 2025-11-25; beta headers mcp-client-2025-11-20 and advanced-tool-use-2025-11-20) and wrap them behind a capability-negotiation layer so spec revisions and header churn don't break the platform. Use claude-opus-4-8 as the engine and a GPT model as reviewer, both pointed at the same gateway endpoints.

Enforce the OWASP MCP controls as non-negotiable platform policy: OAuth 2.1 + PKCE, capability-level scoping, sanitize/scan all tool descriptions and outputs for injection/poisoning before they reach either model, human-in-the-loop approval for destructive/irreversible tool calls, and pin tool-description hashes to detect rug-pulls after install.

Leverage the spec's experimental durable 'tasks' (SEP-1686) and the listChanged dynamic-discovery notification for the per-project scheduler: long jobs (video renders, batch image studio runs) use task polling/deferred results; scope changes (project switches) emit tools/list_changed so the active client re-fetches the correct scoped tool set without reconnect.
risks: Security of third-party servers is poor and material: ~41% of official-registry servers had zero auth, ~43% had unsafe command-exec paths, ~37% SSRF; 12,520 internet-exposed MCP services found mostly unauthenticated. Listing/installing unvetted servers exposes users to tool poisoning, rug-pulls, data exfiltration, and RCE — our marketplace becomes a supply-chain attack surface if not gated by scanning + sandboxing.

Tool poisoning / rug-pull specifically defeats one-time review: a server can ship benign tool descriptions, get approved, then mutate descriptions post-install to inject prompts. Requires continuous re-verification (description hashing, re-scan on update), not just install-time checks.

Context-bloat economics: without aggressive lazy loading, multi-project multi-tool setups burn 50-72% of the context window on schemas before any work, inflating cost and latency on every call. Tool Search mitigates but is still beta and can mis-select tools in complex workflows — a reliability risk for automated/scheduled jobs.

Fast-moving, partly-unstable foundations: spec revisions every few months (2025-06-18 to 2025-11-25), beta headers that change (mcp-client-2025-11-20, advanced-tool-use-2025-11-20), experimental 'tasks', and uneven client support (e.g. Gemini CLI lagging on list_changed) mean behavior can shift under us; some major players (Perplexity) have publicly pulled MCP internally over context waste.

Vendor lock-in vs portability tension: Anthropic Tool Search/defer_loading and MCP Connector are Anthropic-API features; relying on them ties context optimization to Claude. Mitigate by keeping tool definitions in portable MCP servers behind a gateway so GPT (Responses/Agents SDK) and others can consume the same servers; prefer open-source gateways (Docker, ContextForge) over closed SaaS (MintMCP) where lock-in matters.

ToS / platform risk for the comms surface (WhatsApp/Telegram): MCP servers wrapping unofficial WhatsApp/Telegram automation can violate platform ToS and incur account bans independent of MCP itself — scope and isolate these per-job with revocable credentials and prefer official Business APIs where possible.

OAuth/credential handling is a top CVE source (9 CVEs from broken OAuth flows; token-passthrough abuse where servers accept tokens not issued for them). Our gateway must enforce audience-bound, capability-scoped tokens and never pass user tokens through to downstream servers verbatim.

Cost and reliability of hosted dependencies: relying on third-party hosted servers (Smithery, vendor remote servers) adds uptime, rate-limit, and pricing-change exposure outside our control; per-job token costs on the engine (Opus 4.8 $5/$25 per 1M) plus tool-output bloat can escalate fast for image/video studio and scheduled fan-out workloads.

technicalOptions.option: stdio transport for tools
technicalOptions.pros: Lowest latency, no network, trivially scoped to a single host/user, easy to sandbox as a child process; ideal for desktop/mobile-bundled tools and secrets that never leave the device.
technicalOptions.cons: Local only — can't be shared remotely or P2P without a bridge; each tool is a subprocess (resource overhead at scale); no built-in multi-client fan-out.

technicalOptions.option: Streamable HTTP transport for tools
technicalOptions.pros: Remote, multi-client, works for marketplace/hosted/P2P servers; optional SSE streaming; single endpoint (POST+GET); the interoperable choice across Claude, GPT, Gemini; supports polling/resumption (SEP-1699).
technicalOptions.cons: Network/auth surface to secure (Origin checks, OAuth 2.1); higher latency; servers must handle sessions/scale; deprecated SSE-only fallback still lingers in older clients.

technicalOptions.option: Anthropic Tool Search / defer_loading (lazy schema loading)
technicalOptions.pros: ~85% startup-token reduction, higher tool-selection accuracy, scales to hundreds of marketplace tools per project without context bloat; integrates with MCP via default_config.
technicalOptions.cons: Beta; Anthropic-specific (lock-in); can mis-select tools in complex multi-tool flows; adds an extra search round-trip; needs a deterministic fallback for automated jobs.

technicalOptions.option: Gateway-mediated scoping (Docker MCP Gateway / MintMCP / ContextForge)
technicalOptions.pros: Central enforcement of per-project/per-job tool allow-lists, RBAC, audit, signature verification, container isolation, and tool filtering — model-agnostic so engine and reviewer share one policy.
technicalOptions.cons: Extra hop/latency and an operational component to run/secure; closed-source options (MintMCP) add cost/lock-in; misconfiguration centralizes blast radius.

technicalOptions.option: Self-host on Cloudflare Workers/Agents SDK vs use vendor-hosted servers
technicalOptions.pros: Self-host: cheap edge deploy, built-in OAuth, hibernation billing, full control of scoping and data residency. Vendor-hosted (Smithery): zero ops, handles OAuth/token-refresh/sessions.
technicalOptions.cons: Self-host: you own uptime, security, scaling. Vendor-hosted: uptime/rate-limit/pricing risk outside your control, potential data exposure to a third party, weaker scoping guarantees.

summary: As of mid-2026, the cross-platform desktop framework decision is genuinely close: Tauri v2 (native OS WebView + Rust core) ships ~3-10 MB installers and idles at ~30-50 MB RAM with a capability-deny-by-default security model and first-class mobile (iOS/Android) from one codebase; Electron (bundled Chromium + Node) ships 80-150 MB and idles at 150-300 MB but gives identical rendering everywhere, native Node in-process (critical for bundling Node SDKs), and the most battle-tested auto-update/packaging path. For an AI-agent IDE whose primary engine is the Claude Agent SDK (a Node/Python package + bundled CLI), Electron removes an entire class of sidecar/pkg pain because Node runs in the main process; Tauri requires shipping the agent as a sidecar binary (externalBin) or stock-Node-plus-resources. Preventing system sleep during long agent jobs is a solved-but-manual problem: Electron has built-in powerSaveBlocker (prevent-display-sleep / prevent-app-suspension); Tauri has no official plugin and relies on community crates (tauri-plugin-keepawake, tauri-plugin-nosleep) or a thin Rust binding to macOS IOPMAssertionCreateWithName/IOKit, Windows SetThreadExecutionState(ES_CONTINUOUS|ES_SYSTEM_REQUIRED|ES_DISPLAY_REQUIRED), and Linux systemd-inhibit/D-Bus. The fastest-moving risks are commercial: Anthropic's June 15 2026 billing split puts Agent-SDK/headless usage on a separate, non-rollover monthly credit pool and forbids reselling claude.ai login to your end users, and unofficial WhatsApp automation (Baileys/Evolution/whatsapp-web.js) carries a real ban-wave risk versus the official (paid, per-message) WhatsApp Cloud API. Telegram's Bot API is free and instant by contrast. The pragmatic answer for a universal agent+media platform under time pressure is Electron now (Node SDK in-process, mature updater, powerSaveBlocker) with a clean modular/IPC boundary so a later Tauri port stays cheap, while treating Claude billing and WhatsApp ToS as the top program risks.

keyFindings.point: Tauri v2 vs Electron concrete deltas (2026 data)
keyFindings.detail: Tauri ships installers <10 MB (real apps ~3-10 MB) and idles ~30-50 MB RAM, cold-starts <0.5s; Electron ships 80-150 MB, idles 150-300 MB, starts 1-2s. Tauri uses per-OS WebViews (WebView2/Win, WKWebView/macOS, WebKitGTK/Linux) so CSS/JS can diverge across platforms and needs more cross-platform QA; Electron's bundled Chromium renders identically everywhere. Tauri security is capability-deny-by-default (opt-in permissions); Electron requires manual renderer hardening (contextIsolation, sandbox, no nodeIntegration). Tauri plugin registry grew 47 (Jan 2025) to 120+ (Apr 2026); Electron ecosystem still larger/more mature.

keyFindings.point: Bundling Node SDKs: Electron wins on the Claude Agent SDK
keyFindings.detail: Electron runs Node in the main process, so @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk (npm: claude-agent-sdk, ships a bundled CLI binary, subagents, sessions, MCP client) and any Node MCP servers run in-process with no extra packaging. Tauri can only bundle self-contained binaries as sidecars (tauri.conf.json bundle.externalBin, named binaries/<name>-<target-triple>, invoked via Command.sidecar from @tauri-apps/plugin-shell with explicit shell capability). Node sidecars require either pkg/SEA single-file compilation (now awkward as pkg is deprecated; Node 20+ SEA is the modern path) or shipping stock node + JS as a Resource. This is the single biggest architectural reason to favor Electron for an agent IDE.

keyFindings.point: Preventing system/display sleep during agent jobs — native APIs
keyFindings.detail: macOS: IOKit IOPMAssertionCreateWithName with kIOPMAssertionTypePreventUserIdleSystemSleep (idle system) and kIOPMAssertionTypePreventUserIdleDisplaySleep (display); `caffeinate -i` (system) / `-d` (display) wrap exactly these. Windows: SetThreadExecutionState(ES_CONTINUOUS | ES_SYSTEM_REQUIRED | ES_DISPLAY_REQUIRED) — ES_CONTINUOUS is mandatory or it only resets the idle timer once; Win11 drops the assertion immediately when cleared (no 2-min grace). Linux: systemd-inhibit / org.freedesktop.login1 D-Bus Inhibit, or the older org.freedesktop.ScreenSaver. None of these can stop a user manually choosing Sleep.

keyFindings.point: Electron has this built-in; Tauri does not (community-only)
keyFindings.detail: Electron: powerSaveBlocker.start('prevent-display-sleep') keeps display+system awake, prevent-app-suspension keeps system awake but lets display sleep; prevent-display-sleep has higher precedence; returns an int id, stop(id) releases. Tauri v2 has NO official plugin (open issue tauri-apps/tauri#3697). Community options: tauri-plugin-keepawake (thewh1teagle, crate v0.1.x, start({display,idle,sleep})), tauri-plugin-nosleep (pevers, npm tauri-plugin-nosleep-api, block(NoSleepType.PreventUserIdleDisplaySleep)/unblock()), tauri-plugin-screen-wake-lock. These are small/low-maintenance; for production reliability you may want to vendor a thin Rust binding yourself.

keyFindings.point: Auto-update + signing: both viable; signing is mandatory on macOS either way
keyFindings.detail: Tauri updater: minisign keypair (tauri signer generate), pubkey in tauri.conf.json, TAURI_SIGNING_PRIVATE_KEY at build; serves static JSON manifest OR dynamic endpoint (204=no update, 200=JSON with version/url/signature; supports {{current_version}}/{{target}}/{{arch}}). Bundles: macOS .app.tar.gz, Windows .exe/.msi, Linux .AppImage, each + .sig. Windows auto-exits during install. Electron: electron-updater (electron-builder) is the de-facto path — GitHub/S3/generic providers, validates code signature on macOS AND Windows, NSIS target (Squirrel.Windows deprecated), DMG/AppImage/deb/rpm, delta updates via NSIS/blockmap. BOTH require: macOS Apple Developer ID + notarization (Apple Developer Program $99/yr, notarization mandatory for Gatekeeper); Windows OV code-signing cert (~$200-300/yr; EV ~$280-625/yr but EV no longer needed for normal apps since MS dropped EV OIDs Aug 2024, only for kernel drivers).

keyFindings.point: Native OAuth: deep-link (custom scheme) + loopback fallback, both supported
keyFindings.detail: Tauri: @tauri-apps/plugin-deep-link registers a custom scheme (e.g. myapp://) in tauri.conf.json; for providers that reject custom schemes (Google requires http://localhost), use FabianLars/tauri-plugin-oauth which spins a temporary localhost server to capture the redirect. Always use Authorization Code + PKCE (never implicit) so only a short-lived code transits the deep link, exchanged in-app. Choochmeque/tauri-plugin-google-auth provides full PKCE + local redirect server across macOS/Win/Linux. Electron does the same with a hidden BrowserWindow or shell.openExternal + a loopback http server + protocol.registerSchemesAsPrivileged / app.setAsDefaultProtocolClient.

keyFindings.point: Modular/plugin architecture for on-the-fly features
keyFindings.detail: VS Code is the reference: multi-process (main + renderers + dedicated extension host running extensions in isolation, async IPC). For your app, mirror this: a sandboxed extension/skill host process loading drop-in modules at runtime, never giving 3rd-party code raw Node/main access. Claude Code's own plugin model maps directly: a plugin = .claude-plugin/plugin.json manifest bundling skills + MCP servers + hooks/commands, auto-loaded from ~/.claude/skills/<name>/ (user) or .claude/skills/<name>/ (project) on next session with no install step. Marketplaces are just GitHub repos; the official MCP Registry holds 2,000+ servers (5,000+ community) mid-2026 — a ready-made distribution model for a 'skills marketplace'.

keyFindings.point: Tauri v2 mobile + P2P for the mobile/P2P pillar
keyFindings.detail: Tauri v2 (stable since Oct 2024) builds Linux/macOS/Windows/iOS/Android from one codebase with native API access (Swift @command-equivalent on iOS, Kotlin @Command on Android), HMR on device, and built-in notifications/dialogs/NFC/biometric/clipboard/deep-link plugins — a genuine edge over Electron for the desktop+mobile requirement (Electron has no first-class mobile). For P2P: Iroh (n0-computer, Rust/QUIC, dial-by-PeerId, ~9/10 connections go direct via QUIC-level NAT-traversal + relay fallback) is the strongest 2026 option and pairs with Automerge/Yjs CRDTs for local-first sync; js-libp2p WebRTC or RxDB WebRTC replication are browser-friendly alternatives.

keyFindings.point: AI engine + review model + media model IDs and pricing (June 2026)
keyFindings.detail: Primary engine: Claude Opus 4.8 (released May 28 2026) and Opus 4.7 both $5/$25 per Mtok in/out; Sonnet 4.6 $3/$15; Haiku 4.5 $1/$5; prompt caching ~90% off cached reads, Batch API 50% off. GPT review model: GPT-5.1 $1.25/$10 per Mtok (cached input $0.125/M), no deprecation planned; GPT-5.1-Codex-Max for code-specific review. Image: GPT Image 1.5 ~$0.04 standard (top LMArena ~1264 Elo), Flux 2 Pro ~$0.055 (Elo ~1265), Nano Banana 2 / Gemini 3 Flash Image ~$0.045 (512px) to ~$0.15 (4K), GPT Image 1 Mini ~$0.005 cheapest. Video: Google Veo 3.1 $0.10-$0.60/sec (Vertex AI); ByteDance Seedance 2.0 ~$0.06-$0.13/sec; OpenAI Sora 2 was shut down globally in 2026 (do NOT design around it).

keyFindings.point: CRITICAL commercial risk: Anthropic June 15 2026 billing + reselling ToS
keyFindings.detail: From June 15 2026, Agent SDK / headless (claude -p, scheduled, CI) usage on Pro/Max/Team/Enterprise draws from a SEPARATE non-rollover monthly Agent-SDK credit (Pro = $20/mo, Max 20x = $200/mo of SDK usage), billed at API rates; interactive Claude Code draws a different pool. Heavy per-project schedulers WILL exhaust this fast and must top up via API credits. Crucially: 'Unless previously approved, Anthropic does not allow third-party developers to offer claude.ai login or rate limits for their products' — so a marketplace product CANNOT resell users' Claude subscriptions; end users must bring their own API key or you bill them under your own Anthropic Commercial ToS account.

keyFindings.point: CRITICAL platform risk: WhatsApp automation ban waves
keyFindings.detail: Only the official WhatsApp Business Cloud API (via a Meta BSP) is ToS-safe: near-zero ban risk with opt-in + approved templates, but PAID per-message since July 2025 (marketing ~$0.02 IN to $0.22 DE; utility/auth often <$0.01) and weeks of Meta verification + template approval. Unofficial libs (Baileys, whatsapp-web.js, Evolution API) reverse-engineer the protocol: accounts typically last 2-8 weeks, ban waves every 2-3 months after WA updates; reactive-only bots <2%/yr ban rate but proactive outreach 15-30%/yr, and 2026 added an 'unanswered message' tracker that restricts accounts. A Dec 2025 supply-chain incident shipped a poisoned WhatsApp npm package stealing sessions. Telegram Bot API is free, instant (@BotFather), no per-message charge (only optional paid broadcast 0.1 Stars/~$0.002 above 30 msg/s) — far lower risk for the comms pillar.

itemsOrTools.name: Electron (v3x, 2026)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Chromium + Node.js desktop runtime; Node runs in the main process. Built-in powerSaveBlocker, autoUpdater, protocol/deep-link, IPC, contextBridge sandboxing.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best fit for bundling the Claude Agent SDK and Node MCP servers in-process (no sidecar/pkg pain), built-in sleep-blocking, and the most mature signed auto-update path. Heavier RAM/disk; no native mobile.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS (MIT). Free.

itemsOrTools.name: Tauri v2
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Rust core + native OS WebView; builds desktop AND iOS/Android from one codebase. Capability-based security, plugin system, updater, deep-link plugins.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Tiny bundles, low RAM, strong default security, and the only option that also ships your mobile pillar from the same code. Cost: Node SDK must be a sidecar binary; sleep-blocking and some plugins are community-maintained; cross-WebView QA overhead.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS (MIT/Apache-2.0). Free.

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Agent SDK (TypeScript/Python)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Drives the exact Claude Code agent loop (tools, file edit, bash, web, subagents, sessions, MCP client) from your program; bundles a CLI binary.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Your primary engine. npm @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk; V2 preview drops async-generator coordination for send()/stream() per turn. Note new separate headless credit pool + reselling ToS.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS SDK; usage billed via Anthropic API (Opus 4.8 $5/$25 Mtok) or subscription Agent-SDK credit (Pro $20/mo, Max 20x $200/mo) from Jun 15 2026.

itemsOrTools.name: powerSaveBlocker (Electron)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: start('prevent-display-sleep' | 'prevent-app-suspension') returns an id; stop(id) releases. prevent-display-sleep outranks prevent-app-suspension.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Zero-dependency way to keep the machine awake while an agent job runs; wrap start at job begin / stop at completion. Use prevent-app-suspension for headless jobs that don't need the screen on.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Built into Electron. Free.

itemsOrTools.name: tauri-plugin-keepawake / tauri-plugin-nosleep
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Community Tauri v2 plugins that prevent display/idle/system sleep via OS APIs (IOPMAssertion / SetThreadExecutionState).
itemsOrTools.relevance: The Tauri answer to powerSaveBlocker since there's no official plugin. keepawake: start({display,idle,sleep}); nosleep: block(NoSleepType.PreventUserIdleDisplaySleep)/unblock(). Low maintenance — consider vendoring a thin binding for production.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS. Free. (crate tauri-plugin-keepawake; npm tauri-plugin-nosleep-api)

itemsOrTools.name: electron-updater (electron-builder)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Auto-update with GitHub/S3/generic providers, code-signature validation on macOS+Windows, NSIS/DMG/AppImage targets, delta/blockmap updates.
itemsOrTools.relevance: De-facto Electron update stack; far better-documented than Tauri's updater. Requires macOS app to be signed for updates to work.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS. Free (plus cert/notarization costs).

itemsOrTools.name: Tauri updater plugin
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Minisign-signed updates from a static JSON manifest or dynamic endpoint; per-OS bundles + .sig. Windows auto-exits on install.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Standardized, ~6 lines of config, but less documented; if migrating from another updater you need a bridge release. Good once on Tauri.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS. Free.

itemsOrTools.name: Deep-link + loopback OAuth plugins
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Custom-scheme deep link (myapp://) + temporary localhost server for providers (e.g. Google) that reject custom schemes; PKCE code exchange.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Native OAuth for both frameworks. Tauri: plugin-deep-link + FabianLars/tauri-plugin-oauth + Choochmeque/tauri-plugin-google-auth. Electron: setAsDefaultProtocolClient + loopback server.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS. Free.

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Code plugin/skill system + MCP Registry
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Plugins = .claude-plugin/plugin.json bundling skills + MCP servers + hooks, auto-loaded from ~/.claude/skills or .claude/skills; marketplaces are GitHub repos; official MCP Registry has 2,000+ servers.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Direct blueprint and distribution rails for your 'dynamic skills marketplace' — reuse the manifest format and registry pattern instead of inventing one.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS / free; MCP servers vary.

itemsOrTools.name: WhatsApp Business Cloud API (Meta)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Official, ToS-safe WhatsApp messaging via a BSP; opt-in + approved templates; per-message billing since Jul 2025.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The only sanctioned path for the WhatsApp pillar; near-zero ban risk but paid (~$0.005-$0.22/msg by category/country) and requires Meta verification + template approval (weeks).
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Paid per-message; access via Meta Cloud API / BSP.

itemsOrTools.name: Baileys / Evolution API / whatsapp-web.js (unofficial)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Reverse-engineered WhatsApp Web clients enabling free programmatic messaging without Meta approval.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Cheap and fast to integrate but HIGH ban risk: accounts last 2-8 weeks, ban waves every 2-3 months; proactive outreach 15-30%/yr ban rate; recent poisoned-npm-package incident. Use only for opt-in, reactive, low-volume — never as the core of a paid product.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS/free; severe ToS + ban risk.

itemsOrTools.name: Telegram Bot API
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Free bot platform; instant bot creation via @BotFather; no per-message charge; webhooks/long-poll.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Lowest-risk, lowest-cost comms channel — ship this first for the messaging pillar; only optional cost is paid broadcast (0.1 Stars/~$0.002) above 30 msg/s.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free.

itemsOrTools.name: GPT-5.1 / GPT-5.1-Codex-Max (OpenAI)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: General + code-specialized reasoning models for the second-opinion 'review' role.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Cheap, stable review model: $1.25/$10 per Mtok (cached input $0.125/M); no deprecation planned. Use Codex-Max variant for code review diffs.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Pay-as-you-go API.

itemsOrTools.name: Image/Video gen APIs (GPT Image 1.5, Flux 2 Pro, Nano Banana 2/Gemini 3 Flash Image, Veo 3.1, Seedance 2.0)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Image and video generation for the studio pillar.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Image: GPT Image 1.5 ~$0.04, Flux 2 Pro ~$0.055, Nano Banana 2 ~$0.045-$0.15, GPT Image 1 Mini ~$0.005. Video: Veo 3.1 $0.10-$0.60/sec, Seedance 2.0 ~$0.06-$0.13/sec. Route through a provider abstraction; Sora 2 is shut down — don't depend on it.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Pay-per-image/second APIs; fal.ai/Replicate/Vertex aggregate access.

itemsOrTools.name: Iroh (P2P) + Automerge/Yjs (CRDT)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Iroh: Rust/QUIC dial-by-PeerId with ~90% direct connections behind any NAT + relay fallback. Automerge/Yjs: conflict-free local-first sync.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Strongest 2026 stack for the P2P + offline-first mobile/desktop sync pillar; libp2p-iroh bridges to libp2p, RxDB-WebRTC is a browser-side alternative.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS (Iroh has optional hosted relays); free self-host.

recommendations: Build the first shippable version on Electron, not Tauri. The decisive factor is that your primary engine (Claude Agent SDK) and most MCP servers are Node — Electron runs them in-process with zero sidecar/SEA packaging, gives you built-in powerSaveBlocker and the mature electron-updater signed-update path, and consistent Chromium rendering for the IDE/studio UI. Accept the larger bundle/RAM as the cost of velocity.

Architect a hard process/IPC boundary now so a future Tauri (or Tauri-mobile) port is a re-shell, not a rewrite: keep the agent engine, scheduler, comms, and media adapters as a UI-agnostic Node service talking over a typed IPC/RPC layer; keep all OS-native concerns (sleep-blocker, deep-link, OAuth loopback, auto-update) behind a thin platform interface with one Electron impl today and a Tauri impl later.

Implement sleep prevention as a reference-counted job-scoped wrapper: on agent job start call powerSaveBlocker.start('prevent-app-suspension') for headless runs (lets the display sleep, saves battery) and 'prevent-display-sleep' only when the user is watching; release on completion/failure/cancel. For the eventual Tauri path, wrap macOS IOPMAssertionCreateWithName, Windows SetThreadExecutionState(ES_CONTINUOUS|ES_SYSTEM_REQUIRED[|ES_DISPLAY_REQUIRED]), and Linux systemd-inhibit behind the same interface — vendor a thin Rust binding rather than depending on a single-maintainer community plugin.

Use OAuth Authorization Code + PKCE with a loopback (http://127.0.0.1:<random-port>) redirect as the default, and custom-scheme deep links (myapp://) as the secondary handler. Loopback is the most provider-compatible (Google requires it) and keeps tokens out of the deep link. Store refresh tokens in the OS keychain (Keychain/DPAPI/libsecret), never plaintext.

Adopt Claude Code's plugin/skill conventions verbatim for your skills marketplace: a manifest (.claude-plugin/plugin.json-style) bundling skills + MCP servers, distributed as Git repos and indexed like the MCP Registry, loaded into a sandboxed extension-host process (VS Code model) so third-party skills never touch your main process. This gives you on-the-fly feature add without app updates and a ready ecosystem of 2,000+ MCP servers.

For the model layer, build a provider-abstraction router: Claude Opus 4.8/Sonnet 4.6 as the primary engine, GPT-5.1 / Codex-Max as the independent review pass, and a pluggable image/video backend (GPT Image 1.5 / Flux 2 Pro / Nano Banana 2 for images; Veo 3.1 / Seedance 2.0 for video). Hard-require prompt caching and the Batch API on Claude to control cost; do not hard-code any single model ID — they rev every few months.

Make end users bring their own API keys (Anthropic, OpenAI, image/video) by default. This sidesteps Anthropic's reselling ToS (you may not offer claude.ai login/rate limits to your customers), avoids the new separate non-rollover Agent-SDK credit pool exhausting under your per-project scheduler, and removes you from the cost-of-goods risk on long agent runs.

For comms, ship Telegram first (free, instant, no ban risk) and gate WhatsApp behind the official Business Cloud API via a Meta BSP. If you must support unofficial WhatsApp for a niche, restrict it to opt-in, reactive-only, low-volume usage, isolate it in its own process, pin/audit dependencies (the Dec 2025 poisoned-npm incident), and put a loud ToS/ban-risk warning in front of the user — never make it the backbone of a paid product.

For the mobile + P2P pillars, prototype Tauri v2 mobile (one codebase to iOS/Android) and Iroh + Automerge/Yjs for local-first P2P sync in parallel as a spike, since Electron cannot serve mobile. This validates the cross-framework boundary from the recommendation above and informs whether the eventual unification target is Tauri-everywhere.
risks: Anthropic commercial/billing risk (fast-moving): the June 15 2026 split puts headless Agent-SDK usage on a separate non-rollover monthly credit pool billed at API rates, and reselling claude.ai login/limits to your end users is prohibited without prior approval. A per-project scheduler running long agent jobs can blow the Pro $20/Max $200 SDK credit quickly; mis-modeling this is a direct margin and ToS risk. Anthropic has already changed this policy multiple times in 2026 — treat it as unstable and design BYO-key.

WhatsApp ban risk: unofficial libs (Baileys/Evolution/whatsapp-web.js) get accounts banned in 2-8 weeks with ban waves every 2-3 months; proactive messaging hits 15-30%/yr ban rates and 2026's unanswered-message tracker adds new restrictions. Building a product feature on these risks mass user account loss and reputational damage. The compliant path (Cloud API) is paid per-message and gated behind weeks of Meta verification/template approval.

Tauri sleep-prevention maturity risk: no official plugin exists (open since 2022, tauri#3697); community crates are single-maintainer and low-activity. If you go Tauri, you may need to own/vendor the native bindings, and Windows 11's immediate assertion-drop behavior means the blocker must be held continuously for the full job, not pulsed.

Code-signing/notarization is non-optional and recurring: macOS requires Apple Developer Program ($99/yr) + mandatory notarization for both frameworks' auto-update to work; Windows needs an OV cert (~$200-300/yr). Unsigned builds break electron-updater on macOS and trigger Gatekeeper/SmartScreen warnings. Budget time for the notarization pipeline and cert procurement (EV/OV identity validation can take days).

Tauri cross-WebView fragmentation: using WebView2/WKWebView/WebKitGTK means CSS/JS behavior diverges across OSes (and Linux WebKitGTK lags), multiplying QA for a UI-heavy IDE + media studio. Electron avoids this but at 80-150 MB and 150-300 MB RAM, which hurts on low-end machines and battery.

Vendor lock-in and model churn: Claude/GPT/image/video model IDs and prices rev every few months (Opus 4.5->4.7->4.8 in ~2 months; Sora 2 was shut down globally in 2026). Hard-coding model IDs or a single provider creates brittle dependencies; the studio pillar specifically must not depend on Sora.

Third-party Node supply-chain risk: an agent IDE that bundles many MCP servers and a dynamic skills marketplace has a large, fast-changing dependency surface (the Dec 2025 poisoned WhatsApp npm package stole sessions). Sandboxing the extension host, pinning/locking dependencies, and vetting marketplace skills are essential, not optional.

Reliability of long-running agent jobs vs OS power events: even with a power assertion held, OS forced-sleep (lid close on some configs, low-battery hibernate, admin power policy) can still interrupt jobs; the scheduler needs durable checkpointing/resume rather than assuming the wake-lock guarantees completion.

summary: As of mid-2026, the dominant, proven pattern for controlling/monitoring a desktop AI agent from a phone is NOT pure browser-to-browser WebRTC but a relay-brokered, end-to-end-encrypted WebSocket fabric where a CLI daemon on the desktop and a mobile app each hold an outbound connection to a dumb relay that forwards encrypted blobs. Anthropic's own Claude Code "Remote Control" (GA-preview Feb 25, 2026, Claude v2.1.52, Max tier first) and the open-source Happy Coder (github.com/slopus/happy, MIT, ~22k stars, Expo/React Native + self-hostable relay, X25519/ECDH/AES-256-GCM) both use this relay+E2EE model precisely because it traverses CGNAT/firewalls with zero inbound ports and survives laptop sleep. True P2P (WebRTC data channels, libp2p, iroh) still needs a relay fallback for the 15-25% of clients behind symmetric NAT/CGNAT, so a relay+P2P hybrid is mandatory regardless. For network reachability of self-hosted servers, Tailscale/WireGuard (mesh, admin-only) and Cloudflare Tunnel (public reverse proxy) are complementary, not competing. For state sync, Yjs is the production default (~920k weekly npm downloads, deepest ecosystem) while Automerge 3.0 (Aug 2025, 10x memory cut, Rust-via-JSI on React Native) is the better local-first/versioned choice. React Native (Expo) is the pragmatic mobile client given the JS/TypeScript reuse with a Node CLI engine, though Flutter wins raw rendering performance. The space is moving fast: Remote Control is a research preview, Tailscale just removed its 100-device free cap, and iroh is pre-1.0.

keyFindings.point: The winning architecture for phone-to-desktop-agent control is relay-brokered E2EE WebSocket, not pure P2P
keyFindings.detail: Both Anthropic Remote Control and Happy Coder converge on: a CLI daemon on the desktop opens an OUTBOUND HTTPS/WSS connection to a relay; the phone opens its own outbound connection; the relay forwards opaque encrypted blobs and never holds plaintext. This makes outbound-only requests with no inbound ports, traverses CGNAT/corporate firewalls, and lets sessions survive laptop sleep and reconnect. This is the model to copy for our platform's mobile control plane.

keyFindings.point: Anthropic Claude Code Remote Control (our primary engine already ships mobile control)
keyFindings.detail: Announced Feb 25, 2026 by PM Noah Zweben. Requires Claude Code v2.1.52; run `claude remote-control` or `/rc` to get a QR/URL; pair via Claude mobile app, browser URL, or claude.ai/code session list. Outbound HTTPS only, no inbound ports, TLS, multiple short-lived single-purpose credentials that expire independently. Research Preview on Claude Max tier, with Claude Pro ($20/mo) promised later. Local context (filesystem, env vars, MCP servers) stays on the host. Implication: we may not need to build mobile control for Claude-Code sessions from scratch — but it is Anthropic-hosted relay (vendor lock-in, no self-host) and preview-stage.

keyFindings.point: Happy Coder is the reference open-source implementation to fork/study
keyFindings.detail: github.com/slopus/happy, MIT, ~22k stars, ~95% TypeScript. Four modules: Happy CLI (wraps Claude Code/Codex), Happy Agent (remote session control), Happy App (Expo web+mobile client, iOS App Store + Google Play), Happy Server (encrypted sync relay, self-hostable). E2EE: X25519 keypairs, ECDH key exchange, AES-256-GCM message encryption, ephemeral session keys for forward secrecy. Features: push notifications for approval gates, one-keypress device handoff, realtime voice. Hosted at app.happy.engineering. This is the closest existing thing to our mobile+P2P requirement and is directly reusable.

keyFindings.point: Pure WebRTC/P2P always needs a TURN relay fallback — budget for it
keyFindings.detail: 15-25% of clients sit behind symmetric NAT / CGNAT / restrictive firewalls where hole-punching fails and a relay is the ONLY path. This 15-25% TURN rate has been stable for years. STUN-only direct success is ~70-90% on residential, far lower on corporate. Self-hosted coturn (de-facto OSS TURN, GitHub coturn/coturn, supports UDP/TCP/TLS/DTLS) costs roughly a t3-xlarge (~$149/mo) + bandwidth on AWS and must be DDoS-protected and multi-region. Conclusion: a relay+P2P hybrid is non-negotiable; the relay-first E2EE-WebSocket model sidesteps most of this complexity.

keyFindings.point: Tailscale removed the free 100-device cap and is the easiest mesh for self-hosted server reachability
keyFindings.detail: 2026 Personal/free plan: up to 6 users, UNLIMITED user devices (100-cap removed), up to 50 tagged resources, 1,000 ephemeral resource-minutes/mo. Built on WireGuard. Native apps on iOS/Android/macOS/Windows/Linux/Synology/Unraid/etc. Paid: Standard $8/user/mo, Premium $18/user/mo. Best for admin-only private device-to-device access (phone reaching your self-hosted server). Funnel (beta) can expose a tailnet host publicly at *.ts.net but is limited (max 3 funnels, no custom domain, no WAF).

keyFindings.point: Cloudflare Tunnel is complementary to Tailscale, not a competitor — use both
keyFindings.detail: Cloudflare Tunnel runs `cloudflared` agent making outbound connections; assigns a public URL; bypasses CGNAT, no inbound ports, no static IP, automatic DNS, WAF/Access available. Best for public-shareable web apps over a normal browser URL and production. Tailscale Funnel is fine for quick shares but CF Tunnel wins production. Rule of thumb: admin-only private access -> Tailscale; public-shareable -> Cloudflare Tunnel.

keyFindings.point: Tailscale embedding has ToS/lock-in risk; Headscale or raw WireGuard de-risks it
keyFindings.detail: Tailscale is a commercial product wrapping WireGuard + a SaaS coordination/control plane. Embedding a custom VPN into our commercial product favors raw WireGuard. Headscale (github.com/juanfont/headscale, v0.24 in early 2026) is the OSS reimplementation of Tailscale's control server — runs on a ~5 EUR/mo Contabo VPS, full data sovereignty, ~90% of Tailscale's function, but designed for a SINGLE tailnet / small org, NOT multi-tenant enterprise. Risk: relying on Tailscale SaaS control plane for a commercial app = vendor lock-in + per-user cost at scale.

keyFindings.point: Yjs is the production CRDT default; Automerge 3.0 is the local-first/versioned alternative
keyFindings.detail: Yjs: ~920k weekly npm downloads, ~17k stars, deepest ecosystem (Tiptap, Hocuspocus, BlockSuite), no WASM (smaller bundle), 26k-156k ops/sec. Providers: y-websocket (client-server), y-webrtc (P2P), y-indexeddb / op-sqlite / MMKV for offline persistence on React Native — combine network + persistence providers. Automerge 3.0 (Aug 2025): Rust core, 10x memory cut (Moby-Dick-sized doc 700MB->1.3MB; 17h load -> 9s), native React Native bindings via JSI (replaces WASM), Git-like history/branching, Peritext rich text. Loro is the fast-rising third option. Pick Yjs for ergonomics+ecosystem; Automerge for rich JSON + explicit local-first versioning.

keyFindings.point: Push notifications: FCM is free/unlimited but Expo simplifies it; ntfy is the self-hostable wildcard
keyFindings.detail: FCM: free, no paid tier, ~5,000 msg/sec upstream and 1,000 msg/sec downstream burst per project, 1,000 topic subs/device, APNs 4KB payload cap (MessageTooBig if exceeded). Expo Push is free, wraps FCM+APNs; expo-notifications getDevicePushTokenAsync exposes the native token if you want to talk to FCM/APNs directly. Self-host options: ntfy (HTTP pub-sub, OSS, iOS+Android apps, UnifiedPush distributor, only uses Firebase for ntfy.sh Play flavor — F-Droid/self-host flavor avoids Google entirely), Novu (OSS), Knock (self-hostable). For approval-gate pings from a per-project scheduler, FCM/APNs via Expo is the default; ntfy is the no-Google escape hatch.

keyFindings.point: Secure pairing: QR-out-of-band + Noise/SPAKE2+ is the established pattern
keyFindings.detail: Standard flow: two devices exchange a short-lived QR (e.g. invalid after ~2 min) carrying a static public key out-of-band, then run a Noise handshake authenticating long-term device-ID static keys to defeat MITM; or SPAKE2+ over NIST P-256 to derive a secure channel from a short secret (used in Apple Car Key, Matter). Happy Coder's X25519+ECDH and Anthropic's QR-scan pairing both instantiate this. For our platform: QR pairing carrying the desktop's X25519 public key + a one-time pairing token, then E2EE channel through the relay.

keyFindings.point: React Native (Expo) vs Flutter: RN wins for our JS/Node-engine reuse, Flutter wins raw rendering
keyFindings.detail: Flutter ~46% vs RN ~35% market share (2026); Flutter Impeller gives consistent 120fps and better memory; RN New Architecture (Fabric/JSI/TurboModules) is production-ready and near-native for standard UIs. RN's decisive edge: the npm/JS ecosystem (react-native-webrtc at WebRTC M124 with data-channel + filesystem access; Yjs/Automerge JS; Anthropic/OpenAI JS SDKs; op-sqlite/MMKV) — huge code reuse with a Node-based CLI engine. Flutter advantage: 6-platform single codebase incl. desktop, Dart FFI to TFLite/Gemma. For a Claude-Code/GPT TypeScript platform, RN+Expo minimizes context-switching and maximizes shared code.

keyFindings.point: libp2p and iroh are the credible decentralized-P2P stacks if you avoid a central relay
keyFindings.detail: js-libp2p: browser transports are WebSocket, WebTransport (Chrome/FF/Edge/Opera, NOT Safari), and WebRTC-direct (all WebRTC browsers); browser-to-browser uses WebRTC private-to-private with Circuit Relay V2 for signaling (a decentralized, resource-constrained alternative to TURN — relay only signals, then drops out). iroh (n0-computer, Rust, pre-1.0): peer-to-peer QUIC addressed by public key (EndpointId), hole-punches with public-relay fallback, relay drops out once direct connection is up; iroh-relay is self-hostable. These matter if we want serverless/decentralized agent-to-agent links, but add complexity vs a managed E2EE relay.

keyFindings.point: DIY mobile-control stacks (SSH+tmux+mosh+ntfy) are robust but lack approval-gate UX
keyFindings.detail: The hacker baseline: mosh (survives pocketing the phone) + tmux (session survives mosh death) + ntfy (push instead of screen-watching) + Tailscale (reachability), driven from Termius. Free and robust but NO structured approval-gate push prompts or rich diff review — exactly the gap a purpose-built mobile app (Happy/Remote Control style) fills. Confirms our app's value-add is the structured control plane (approve/reject, diff review, voice, scheduler triggers), not raw terminal access.

itemsOrTools.name: Anthropic Claude Code Remote Control
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Built-in mobile/web control of a running Claude Code terminal session via `claude remote-control` / `/rc`; QR or URL pairing to the Claude app; outbound-HTTPS-only relay through Anthropic API with TLS and short-lived scoped credentials; survives sleep/network drops.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Our primary engine is Claude Code, so this natively delivers part of the mobile-control requirement — but it is Anthropic-hosted (no self-host), preview-stage, and Max-tier-gated, so we cannot depend on it as the sole control plane for a self-hosted universal platform.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Research Preview, Claude Max tier; Claude Pro ($20/mo) promised later. Requires Claude Code v2.1.52+. No public SDK for the relay.

itemsOrTools.name: Happy Coder (slopus/happy)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Open-source mobile+web client for Claude Code and Codex: CLI daemon + self-hostable encrypted relay + Expo app. E2EE via X25519/ECDH/AES-256-GCM with ephemeral keys; push notifications, one-keypress device handoff, realtime voice.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Closest existing implementation of our mobile+P2P+secure-pairing requirement; MIT-licensed and self-hostable, so directly forkable for our control plane and a blueprint for the relay+E2EE pattern. Confirms Expo/React Native is a viable client choice.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free, MIT, github.com/slopus/happy; hosted relay at app.happy.engineering or self-host Happy Server; apps on iOS App Store + Google Play.

itemsOrTools.name: Tailscale (WireGuard mesh)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Zero-config mesh VPN over WireGuard with a SaaS control plane; native apps on all platforms; Funnel (beta) for limited public exposure.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Easiest way for the mobile app to reach a self-hosted desktop/server privately across NAT without opening ports; good for admin-only access to per-project agents.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free Personal: 6 users, unlimited devices (100-cap removed 2026), 50 tagged resources, 1,000 ephemeral resource-min/mo. Standard $8/user/mo, Premium $18/user/mo.

itemsOrTools.name: Headscale
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: OSS self-hosted reimplementation of Tailscale's coordination/control server for a single tailnet; works with stock Tailscale clients.
itemsOrTools.relevance: De-risks Tailscale SaaS lock-in for our commercial product — run our own control plane cheaply with full data sovereignty; but single-tailnet/small-org focus limits multi-tenant SaaS use.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free, OSS (github.com/juanfont/headscale, ~v0.24 early 2026); runs on ~5 EUR/mo VPS.

itemsOrTools.name: Cloudflare Tunnel (cloudflared)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Outbound-only reverse proxy exposing a local service at a public Cloudflare URL with automatic DNS, WAF, and Access; bypasses CGNAT, no inbound ports.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best path to publicly expose a self-hosted dashboard/server (e.g. image+video studio, web UI) over a normal browser URL in production; complements Tailscale (private) for the public surface.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free tier covers tunnels; Zero Trust paid tiers for advanced Access policies. Requires a Cloudflare-managed domain.

itemsOrTools.name: coturn
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: De-facto OSS STUN/TURN server (UDP/TCP/TLS/DTLS) for WebRTC NAT traversal/relay fallback.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Required fallback if we do real WebRTC data channels for the 15-25% of clients behind symmetric NAT/CGNAT; needed for any P2P media (video studio live preview) path.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free, OSS (github.com/coturn/coturn). Self-host cost ~$149/mo t3-xlarge + bandwidth on AWS; needs DDoS protection + multi-region.

itemsOrTools.name: react-native-webrtc
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: WebRTC module for React Native (Android/iOS, partial web/tvOS, Expo plugin); P2P audio/video, data channels, screen capture; tracks WebRTC core M124.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The library for direct P2P data channels in the RN client if we add true peer-to-peer (e.g. local-network low-latency control, large file/media transfer bypassing relay).
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free, OSS (github.com/react-native-webrtc/react-native-webrtc, ~5k stars); Expo via config plugin.

itemsOrTools.name: Yjs (+ y-websocket / y-webrtc / op-sqlite)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: High-performance CRDT for shared data types; providers for client-server (y-websocket), P2P (y-webrtc), and offline persistence (y-indexeddb, op-sqlite, MMKV on RN).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Default engine for syncing agent/session/scheduler state between desktop and phone with offline-first behavior and conflict-free merges; deepest ecosystem and smallest footprint.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free, OSS (github.com/yjs/yjs, ~920k weekly npm downloads). Hocuspocus is a ready y-websocket backend.

itemsOrTools.name: Automerge 3.0
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Rust-core CRDT with JS/WASM and native React Native (JSI) bindings; Git-like history/branching, Peritext rich text; 3.0 cut memory ~10x.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Alternative to Yjs when we need rich JSON state, explicit local-first sync, and version history/branching of agent state on resource-constrained phones.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free, OSS (automerge.org, github.com/automerge/automerge + react-native-automerge).

itemsOrTools.name: iroh (n0-computer)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Rust modular networking stack: peer-to-peer QUIC addressed by public key, hole-punching with public-relay fallback that drops out after direct connection; self-hostable iroh-relay.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Candidate for a modern, public-key-addressed P2P transport between our desktop agents and mobile if we want decentralized, IP-independent dialing instead of a central E2EE relay.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free, OSS (github.com/n0-computer/iroh), pre-1.0; Rust with FFI bindings.

itemsOrTools.name: js-libp2p (WebRTC + Circuit Relay V2)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Modular P2P networking for browser/node; transports WebSocket/WebTransport/WebRTC-direct; browser-to-browser via WebRTC private-to-private with Circuit Relay V2 for signaling.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Option for a fully decentralized agent mesh; Circuit Relay V2 is a trustless, resource-constrained alternative to TURN for signaling. Heavier and more complex than a managed E2EE relay.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free, OSS (libp2p.io, github.com/libp2p/js-libp2p).

itemsOrTools.name: ntfy
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Simple HTTP pub-sub push service; OSS server + iOS/Android apps; UnifiedPush distributor; uses Firebase only for ntfy.sh Play flavor (F-Droid/self-host avoids Google).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Self-hostable, no-Google push channel for scheduler/approval-gate alerts; the escape hatch when we want to avoid FCM/APNs dependency or for power-user self-host deployments.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free, OSS (github.com/binwiederhier/ntfy); hosted ntfy.sh free tier + paid plans.

itemsOrTools.name: Expo Push Notifications (FCM/APNs)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Cross-platform push wrapper over FCM and APNs; expo-notifications exposes native device token for direct FCM/APNs use.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Default push path for the RN/Expo mobile client to receive approval-gate prompts and per-project scheduler events; free and unlimited at the FCM layer.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free. FCM limits: ~5,000 msg/s upstream, ~1,000 msg/s downstream burst/project, 1,000 topic subs/device, 4KB APNs payload cap.

itemsOrTools.name: WireGuard
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Fast, modern kernel/userspace VPN protocol; the cryptographic substrate under Tailscale/Headscale.
itemsOrTools.relevance: If we embed our own VPN into the commercial product (avoiding Tailscale ToS/lock-in), raw WireGuard + our own coordination is the path; more engineering but fully owned.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free, OSS (wireguard.com); kernel module + userspace impls.

recommendations: Adopt the relay-brokered E2EE WebSocket model as the PRIMARY mobile control plane (copy Happy Coder / Anthropic Remote Control): desktop CLI daemon and mobile app each make OUTBOUND WSS to a dumb relay that forwards only encrypted blobs. This gives zero-inbound-port operation, CGNAT/firewall traversal, and sleep-survival without the 15-25% symmetric-NAT failure tax of pure WebRTC.

Fork or closely study Happy Coder (MIT) for the CLI daemon + self-hostable relay + Expo app + X25519/ECDH/AES-256-GCM E2EE; it already implements ~80% of our mobile+pairing+push requirement and ships on both stores.

Build the mobile client in React Native + Expo, not Flutter, to maximize TypeScript/JS code reuse with the Node-based Claude Code engine, Yjs/Automerge JS, the Anthropic+OpenAI JS SDKs, and react-native-webrtc; accept Flutter's raw-rendering edge as a non-decisive tradeoff.

Use Yjs as the default CRDT for session/scheduler/skill-marketplace state sync (y-websocket over the relay + op-sqlite/MMKV offline persistence); reserve Automerge 3.0 for any subsystem needing rich JSON history/branching on-device.

Treat reachability and exposure as TWO complementary layers: Tailscale/WireGuard (or Headscale for sovereignty) for private admin access to self-hosted servers, and Cloudflare Tunnel for any publicly shareable web surface (image/video studio dashboard).

Implement secure pairing as QR-out-of-band carrying the desktop's X25519 public key plus a short-lived (≈2 min) one-time pairing token, then run a Noise/ECDH handshake to derive the E2EE channel — defeats MITM and matches Anthropic/Happy patterns.

Only add true WebRTC/iroh/libp2p P2P as an OPTIONAL fast-path (local-network low latency, large media transfer) layered on top of the relay; if you ship WebRTC, you MUST also run coturn for the 15-25% relay-fallback case and budget bandwidth + DDoS protection.
For self-host/power-user deployments and approval-gate alerts, default to Expo Push (FCM/APNs) and offer ntfy as the no-Google self-hostable alternative wired to the per-project scheduler.

De-risk VPN vendor lock-in early: design the network layer so Tailscale SaaS, Headscale, or raw WireGuard are swappable; do not hard-couple the product to Tailscale's control plane or per-user pricing.
risks: Anthropic Remote Control is a Research Preview (Feb 2026), Max-tier-gated, Anthropic-hosted with no self-host or public relay SDK — depending on it for the mobile control plane risks API/ToS changes, tier-gating, and vendor lock-in; treat it as complementary, not foundational.

Pure WebRTC/libp2p P2P fails for 15-25% of clients (symmetric NAT/CGNAT/corporate firewalls), forcing a TURN relay; self-hosted coturn carries real cost (~$149/mo+ compute, bandwidth, multi-region, mandatory DDoS protection) and operational burden — a relay-first design avoids most of this.

Embedding Tailscale in a commercial product is ToS/lock-in risk (it is a SaaS-coordinated commercial product with per-user pricing at scale); Headscale is single-tailnet/small-org-focused and not built for multi-tenant SaaS, so neither is a turnkey enterprise control plane — raw WireGuard shifts the burden to us.

FCM/APNs dependency: APNs 4KB payload cap causes MessageTooBig on rich payloads; FCM soft limits (~5k msg/s upstream, 1k topic subs/device) and Google account coupling can bite at scale or in privacy-sensitive self-host deployments — ntfy/UnifiedPush mitigates but adds surface area.

iroh is pre-1.0 and js-libp2p browser transports are uneven (WebTransport unsupported on Safari) — betting the core transport on these now risks churn and Safari/iOS gaps; keep them optional behind the stable relay path.

Driving the host with a phone-controlled agent that has full filesystem/MCP/env access is a serious security surface: a compromised pairing or relay-side bug = remote code execution on the desktop. E2EE, short-lived scoped credentials, explicit approval gates, and timeboxed pairing QRs are mandatory, not optional.

CRDT ecosystem is shifting fast (Automerge 3.0 just landed Aug 2025, Loro rising, native RN JSI bindings new) — APIs and performance leaders are moving; isolate sync behind an abstraction so Yjs/Automerge/Loro stay swappable.

Self-hosted relay/TURN/VPN infrastructure that the whole product depends on is a single point of failure and an attack/DDoS target; reliability requires redundancy, monitoring, and a managed fallback, which adds ongoing operational cost.

summary: As of mid-2026, WhatsApp integration splits into two worlds. (1) The OFFICIAL WhatsApp Business Platform (Cloud API, hosted by Meta, free to call) is the only ToS-safe path: per-message pricing (since July 1 2025, replacing conversation-based billing), with service messages free, but it requires business verification, a registered display name, template pre-approval for proactive messages, and a 24-hour customer-service window. Critically, Meta amended its Business Solution Terms (effective Jan 15 2026) to BAN "AI Providers" from using the platform when general-purpose AI is the primary product — directly hostile to a universal AI-agent assistant; this ban is being challenged (Brazil's CADE suspended it domestically Jan 13 2026; Italy got carve-outs). (2) UNOFFICIAL libraries that drive a personal/linked WhatsApp account via the WhatsApp Web multidevice protocol — Baileys (TS, v7.x), whatsmeow (Go), WPPConnect (browser-based), wrapped by Evolution API / WAHA / Whapi.Cloud and dozens of MCP servers. These give full personal-account capability (groups, status/stories, polls, reactions, view-once, channels) with QR or pairing-code login and NO per-message fees, but explicitly violate WhatsApp ToS and carry real ban risk (accounts often survive only 2-8 weeks under heavy automation; Meta uses protocol fingerprinting, velocity, and reply-ratio ML). A late-2025 supply-chain attack (poisoned Baileys wrapper, 50k downloads) underscores ecosystem risk. For a universal AI-agent desktop+mobile platform, the pragmatic pattern is: use unofficial libraries (Baileys/whatsmeow via an MCP bridge) for the user's OWN account to drive agent jobs (the user opts into their own ban risk on their own number), and offer the official Cloud API as an opt-in compliant lane for business/broadcast use — never present a general-purpose AI assistant ON the official API to third parties.

keyFindings.point: Official pricing moved to per-message in 2025; service messages are free
keyFindings.detail: Effective July 1 2025 Meta deprecated conversation-based pricing; now each delivered TEMPLATE message bills individually by category. Service (user-initiated reply) conversations are FREE and unlimited since Nov 1 2024. Example US rates (2026): Marketing $0.025, Utility $0.004, Authentication $0.004, Service free. India (Jan 2026 local-currency switch): Marketing ~₹0.86 (+~10%), Utility/Auth ~₹0.115. Brazil Marketing ~$0.0625. Utility templates sent inside an open service window are free; a 72h free-entry-point window applies to Click-to-WhatsApp ad conversations.

keyFindings.point: Meta BANNED general-purpose AI chatbots on the official platform (Jan 15 2026)
keyFindings.detail: Meta amended the WhatsApp Business Solution Terms: 'Providers and developers of artificial intelligence...are strictly prohibited from accessing or using the WhatsApp Business Solution...when such technologies are the primary...functionality.' Hits OpenAI/ChatGPT, Perplexity, Luzia, Poke. Applies to new API users from Oct 15 2025; enforcement Jan 15 2026. Meta says the API is for business-to-customer comms, not an AI distribution channel (cited message-volume burden). Business-specific AI (support, orders, bookings) remains allowed.

keyFindings.point: The AI-chatbot ban is under active regulatory challenge — status uncertain
keyFindings.detail: Brazil's competition regulator CADE suspended the policy domestically on Jan 13 2026, opening an antitrust probe into whether terms are 'exclusionary to competitors and unduly favor Meta AI.' Italy obtained continued rival-chatbot access in Dec 2025. Meta AI itself is NOT banned. Net: the rule is real but fast-moving and may be narrowed by region — do not architect around it being permanently enforced or permanently lifted.

keyFindings.point: Official messaging limits are tiered by unique conversations/24h with throughput caps
keyFindings.detail: Tiers: 250 (unverified) -> 1,000 -> 10,000 -> 100,000 -> Unlimited unique recipients per rolling 24h. Auto-upgrade requires ~50% utilization of current tier within 7 days plus a stable/green quality rating; Meta re-evaluates ~every 6h. Quality is Green/Yellow/Red; Red blocks scaling. Throughput ~80 messages/sec standard, up to 1,000 MPS on Unlimited. Since Oct 7 2025 limits apply at the BUSINESS PORTFOLIO level — every number inherits the portfolio's highest tier, removing per-number warm-up.

keyFindings.point: Unofficial libraries violate ToS and carry concrete, measurable ban risk
keyFindings.detail: Baileys, whatsmeow, WPPConnect, Evolution API all drive the WhatsApp Web multidevice protocol on a personal/linked account. Detection: protocol fingerprinting, send-velocity, reply-ratio ML (<10% replies = high risk in 2025-26 research), contact-graph distance (strangers = risk), 24/7 server-IP activity, robotic timing, and recipient block/report signals. Heavily-automated numbers commonly last only ~2-8 weeks. Login is QR scan or 8-char pairing code; WhatsApp caps linked devices and re-auth is needed roughly every ~20 days.

keyFindings.point: Real supply-chain attacks exist in the unofficial ecosystem
keyFindings.detail: In Dec 2025 a malicious npm package 'lotusbail' trojanized the genuine Baileys client (>50k downloads). It embedded a hardcoded pairing code to silently link the attacker's device as a trusted endpoint, exfiltrating messages; persisted even after uninstall until the victim manually unlinked all devices. Pin dependencies, vendor/audit the library, and lock the linked-device list.

keyFindings.point: Mature MCP servers already bridge personal WhatsApp to Claude agents
keyFindings.detail: lharries/whatsapp-mcp (~5.8k stars, MIT): Go whatsmeow bridge + Python MCP server, QR login, local SQLite store, tools to search/list chats, read history, send text/media to chats and groups. jlucaso1/whatsapp-mcp-ts is a pure-TS Baileys equivalent. A WhatsApp Claude Plugin was published on Anthropic's official plugin marketplace. These docs explicitly warn of the 'lethal trifecta' (prompt-injection -> private-data exfiltration) when an agent has WhatsApp + web + tool access.

keyFindings.point: This environment already ships an Evolution-API-style WhatsApp MCP toolset
keyFindings.detail: The runtime exposes mcp__whatsapp__* tools mirroring Evolution API: create/connect/restart/delete instance, get QR code + get pairing code, set_cloud_credentials (official Cloud API lane), send text/image/video/audio/document/location/contact/poll/reaction/link-preview/view-once, image/video/text status (stories), full group management, newsletters (channels), privacy/profile, presence, call rejection. This implies the platform's WhatsApp layer is built on a dual-mode (Baileys unofficial + Cloud API official) gateway.

keyFindings.point: Onboarding the official API is heavyweight; BSPs add cost and markup
keyFindings.detail: Cloud API itself is free to call but requires a Meta business portfolio, business verification, a WABA, an approved display name, and template approval. Embedded Signup / Tech Provider program lets you onboard customers' numbers; advanced access to whatsapp_business_management is needed for client WABAs. BSPs (Twilio, 360dialog, Gupshup, WATI, Vonage, 1msg) add fees: e.g. 360dialog ~$49/mo per number + ~$0.005/msg markup. Effective official cost is often 2-5x raw Meta rates after markup, template delays, and spam-blocked messages.

keyFindings.point: Capability differs sharply between official and unofficial
keyFindings.detail: Official Cloud API CANNOT: post status/stories, freely create/manage arbitrary groups at scale, send to non-opted-in users, or act as a personal account — it is template-gated and business-scoped. Unofficial (Baileys v7) CAN: send/receive all message types, polls (with vote aggregation), reactions, view-once, status/stories, newsletters/channels, full group + community ops, exactly like the WhatsApp app. whatsmeow supports text/media/groups/communities/QR+pairing/status(experimental) but NOT calls or (historically) broadcast lists; channel support is partial. WPPConnect automates a real Chromium WhatsApp Web session (heavier RAM/CPU, more fragile than socket libs).

itemsOrTools.name: WhatsApp Cloud API (WhatsApp Business Platform)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Meta-hosted official REST API for sending/receiving template + session messages, media, interactive buttons/lists, and webhooks. Free to call (Meta hosts); billed per delivered template message by category. Tiered limits 250->unlimited unique conversations/24h, ~80-1000 MPS. Requires business verification, WABA, approved display name, template approval, 24h service window.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The only ToS-compliant lane and the right choice for any business/broadcast/notification use of the platform. BUT the Jan 15 2026 'AI Providers' ban makes it legally hostile to offering a GENERAL-PURPOSE agent assistant to third parties over WhatsApp — so use it only for the user's own business comms, not as the agent's distribution channel.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: API free; pay-per-message (US: Marketing $0.025, Utility/Auth $0.004, Service free). Access via Meta directly (Embedded Signup/Tech Provider) or a BSP (Twilio/360dialog/Gupshup/WATI). REST + webhooks.

itemsOrTools.name: Baileys (WhiskeySockets/Baileys)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: WebSocket-based TypeScript implementation of the WhatsApp Web multidevice protocol (NO browser). v7.x (v7.0.0-rc13 May 2026, MIT, maintainer Rajeh Taher). Full personal-account capability: all message types, media, polls+vote aggregation, reactions, view-once, status/stories, newsletters/channels, full group/community management. Login via QR or pairing code; supports Node/Bun/Deno.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best-in-class OSS engine to drive the USER'S OWN WhatsApp for agent jobs (read incoming, reply, run skills, post status). Lets the platform offer 'connect your WhatsApp' with zero per-message cost and full feature parity. The user owns the ban risk on their own number. Pin/audit dependencies after the 2025 supply-chain attack.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free OSS (MIT), npm 'baileys'. Self-host; you persist auth/creds. Violates WhatsApp ToS — ban risk.

itemsOrTools.name: whatsmeow (tulir/mautrix)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Go library for the WhatsApp Web multidevice API (MPL-2.0, ~6.4k stars, actively maintained, last publish Jun 2026). Single static binary, no browser, no JS bridge. Supports text/media, groups+communities, invite links, typing/receipts, app-state sync, QR + pairing-code login, status (experimental). Does NOT do voice/video calls or broadcast lists; channel/newsletter support partial.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Most robust/lightweight backend for a desktop+mobile agent: compiles into a Go sidecar, low memory, stable. Powers the most popular WhatsApp MCP (lharries). Ideal if the platform wants a single self-contained binary per linked account rather than a Node service. Same personal-account ToS/ban caveats as Baileys.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free OSS (MPL-2.0), go.mau.fi/whatsmeow. Self-host. Violates ToS — ban risk.

itemsOrTools.name: Evolution API (evolution-foundation)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Production REST gateway (v2.3.7, Dec 2025; Apache-2.0 + brand-protection clause; Node 20/TS5/Express, Postgres/MySQL via Prisma). DUAL mode: Baileys-based WhatsApp Web OR official Cloud API behind one API. Multi-instance with per-instance tokens, webhooks/WebSocket, native integrations: n8n, Typebot, Chatwoot, Dify, OpenAI, RabbitMQ, Kafka, Amazon SQS, S3/MinIO.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Strong candidate for THIS platform's WhatsApp service layer — its dual Baileys/Cloud architecture exactly matches the recommended two-lane strategy, and the runtime's mcp__whatsapp__* tools mirror its surface (instances, QR/pairing, set_cloud_credentials, send media/status/poll, groups, newsletters). Gives multi-account, queue-backed, webhook-driven agent jobs out of the box.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free OSS (Apache-2.0 w/ logo/notification clause). Self-host (Docker/Railway/Hostinger). Baileys lane violates ToS; Cloud lane is compliant.

itemsOrTools.name: WPPConnect (wppconnect-team)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Node/TS project + ready-to-use wppconnect-server REST API that AUTOMATES a real Chromium WhatsApp Web session (Puppeteer-style), tracking WhatsApp Web alpha builds (2.3000.x). Token-based auth, webhooks/WebSockets, Chatwoot integration, catalogs/communities/status/groups.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Lower priority for this platform: browser automation is heavier (RAM/CPU) and more fragile/breakage-prone than socket libraries (Baileys/whatsmeow) as WhatsApp Web updates frequently. Useful only if you need a feature exposed via the web UI but not yet in the socket libs.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free OSS. Self-host. Violates ToS — ban risk; higher infra cost per session.

itemsOrTools.name: WhatsApp MCP servers (lharries/whatsapp-mcp, jlucaso1/whatsapp-mcp-ts)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: lharries: Go whatsmeow bridge + Python MCP server (~5.8k stars, MIT), QR login, local SQLite history, tools to search contacts/messages, list/retrieve chats, send text+media to chats/groups, download media. jlucaso1: pure-TS Baileys MCP equivalent. A WhatsApp Claude Plugin is on Anthropic's official plugin marketplace.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Direct drop-in pattern: expose the user's WhatsApp to Claude Code as the primary engine via MCP tools, so the agent can read/triage/reply and trigger per-project scheduler jobs. Local-first SQLite keeps data on-device (good for privacy + P2P). Mind the documented 'lethal trifecta' — sandbox the agent and gate outbound sends behind allowlists/confirmation.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free OSS (MIT). Run locally; connect via stdio MCP to Claude Desktop/Code/Cursor.

itemsOrTools.name: Managed unofficial gateways (Whapi.Cloud, WAHA, WasenderApi)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Hosted SaaS wrappers over the unofficial WhatsApp Web protocol. Flat MONTHLY price per number, no per-message/per-conversation fees; QR-scan onboarding with 'no approval needed'; include groups, channels, webhooks. WAHA is self-hostable; WasenderApi/Whapi are cloud.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Fast path to ship if you don't want to operate Baileys/whatsmeow yourself — predictable cost, instant onboarding. Trade-offs: third-party holds the session (privacy/lock-in), same ToS/ban exposure, and you depend on their protocol-update cadence. Reasonable as an optional managed backend behind the same abstraction.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Flat monthly per number (varies by vendor). Cloud SaaS or self-host (WAHA). Unofficial — violates ToS.

itemsOrTools.name: Official BSPs (Twilio, 360dialog, Gupshup, WATI, Vonage)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Meta-approved Business Solution Providers that sit on top of Cloud API and ship onboarding, inbox, broadcast, template management, CRM/flow builders. Handle business verification paperwork and Embedded Signup.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Use ONE of these only if you want the compliant lane without operating Meta onboarding yourself, e.g. for a business/broadcast feature. Expect markup (e.g. 360dialog ~$49/mo/number + ~$0.005/msg) and template-approval latency; effective cost ~2-5x raw Meta rates. Vendor lock-in to their tooling.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Per-vendor platform fee + Meta per-message + markup. REST APIs + embedded signup.
