recommendations: Adopt a TWO-LANE WhatsApp architecture behind one internal abstraction: (A) UNOFFICIAL personal-account lane (Baileys or whatsmeow via an MCP/sidecar) as the DEFAULT for driving agent jobs on the user's OWN number — full feature parity, zero per-message cost, user opts into their own ban risk; (B) OFFICIAL Cloud API lane as an opt-in compliant mode for business/broadcast/notification use. The runtime's mcp__whatsapp__* toolset (set_cloud_credentials + QR/pairing instance tools) already encodes exactly this dual design — build on it.

Pick whatsmeow (Go sidecar) as the primary engine for the desktop+mobile platform: single static binary, low RAM, no browser, powers the leading MCP, and is friendlier to mobile/P2P packaging than a Node service. Keep Baileys as a secondary engine where you need newsletter/channel/view-once features that are ahead in the TS lib. Avoid WPPConnect's browser automation as a primary path (heavy, fragile).

Do NOT expose a general-purpose AI assistant to third parties over the OFFICIAL Cloud API — the Jan 15 2026 'AI Providers' ban prohibits it and risks WABA termination. Confine the official lane to the user's own business-to-customer messaging (support/orders/notifications via approved templates). General agent conversation should run over the user's personal account (unofficial lane) or over Telegram, which has no such restriction.

Engineer for ban-avoidance on the unofficial lane: human-like pacing/jitter, no cold outreach to non-contacts, keep reply-ratio high, gradual 7-day warm-up for new numbers, respect linked-device limits, persist and reuse auth creds, and surface clear in-product warnings that this is unofficial and the user's number can be banned. Make the official lane a one-click upgrade for users who need reliability/scale.

Treat the supply chain as hostile: pin exact versions of Baileys/whatsmeow, vendor and audit the dependency (recall the Dec 2025 'lotusbail' trojan with a hardcoded pairing code), monitor the linked-device list and alert/auto-unlink unexpected devices, and store session creds encrypted on-device (SQLite local-first aligns with your P2P/privacy goals).

Harden the agent against the 'lethal trifecta': WhatsApp + web access + tool execution invites prompt-injection-driven exfiltration. Gate all OUTBOUND sends and destructive group ops behind allowlists and explicit user confirmation, run untrusted message content through the GPT review model before the agent acts, and sandbox per-project scheduler jobs that touch WhatsApp.

Abstract the provider so you can swap engines (whatsmeow <-> Baileys <-> Evolution API <-> a managed gateway like WAHA/Whapi <-> official Cloud API) without changing app code. This hedges protocol breakage (WhatsApp Web updates frequently) and the regulatory volatility around the AI-chatbot ban, and avoids lock-in to any single BSP or gateway.
risks: ToS / account-ban risk (HIGH): all unofficial libraries (Baileys, whatsmeow, WPPConnect, Evolution API, managed gateways) violate WhatsApp's Terms; heavily-automated numbers commonly survive only ~2-8 weeks. Meta detects via protocol fingerprinting, send-velocity, reply-ratio ML, contact-graph distance, server-IP 24/7 patterns, and recipient reports. A banned number can lose chat history and is hard to appeal.

Regulatory/policy whiplash on the official lane (HIGH uncertainty): Meta's Jan 15 2026 ban on general-purpose AI 'Providers' over Cloud API could terminate a non-compliant WABA, yet Brazil's CADE suspended it (Jan 13 2026) and Italy won carve-outs — the rule's scope is in flux. Architecting for either a permanent ban or a permanent reprieve is risky; build for both.

Protocol breakage / maintenance burden (MEDIUM-HIGH): unofficial libs track an undocumented, frequently-changing WhatsApp Web protocol (WPPConnect tracks alpha builds expiring ~2 months out). Outages until the community patches; you must track upstream releases and ship updates fast on desktop AND mobile.

Supply-chain compromise (MEDIUM-HIGH): demonstrated real attack (poisoned Baileys npm wrapper, >50k installs, hardcoded pairing code added attacker as a linked device, persisted after uninstall). Unpinned/unaudited deps can silently exfiltrate every message.

Prompt-injection / data exfiltration (MEDIUM-HIGH): an agent with WhatsApp read+send plus web/tools is the classic 'lethal trifecta'; a malicious inbound message could induce the agent to leak private chats or send unauthorized messages. Requires send-gating, content review, and sandboxing.

Cost & vendor lock-in on the official lane (MEDIUM): Cloud API itself is free but per-message marketing fees add up at scale (US $0.025/marketing msg), and BSPs add platform fees + markup (e.g. 360dialog ~$49/mo/number + ~$0.005/msg), pushing effective cost 2-5x. Template approval latency and quality-rating downgrades (Yellow/Red) can throttle or block sending. Managed unofficial gateways add session-custody/privacy and lock-in risk.

Privacy / linked-device limits (MEDIUM): personal-account integration stores message history locally (good) but WhatsApp caps linked devices and forces periodic re-auth (~every 20 days), and putting a user's full personal graph into an AI agent raises consent/compliance concerns you must disclose and control.

technicalOptions.option: Official WhatsApp Cloud API (direct via Meta Embedded Signup / Tech Provider)
technicalOptions.pros: Only ToS-safe path; Meta-hosted (no infra to run); reliable delivery; tiered scale to unlimited at up to 1000 MPS; service messages free; webhooks; encrypted; no per-number ban risk.
technicalOptions.cons: Heavy onboarding (business verification, WABA, display-name + template approval); per-message marketing/utility fees; 24h window + template gating blocks free-form agent chat; Jan 2026 ban on general-purpose AI assistants; no status/stories or arbitrary group ops.

technicalOptions.option: whatsmeow (Go) personal-account engine, exposed via MCP sidecar
technicalOptions.pros: Lightweight single static binary, low RAM, no browser; QR+pairing login; powers the top WhatsApp MCP; ideal for desktop+mobile/P2P packaging; full personal-account features incl. groups/communities/status; local SQLite (privacy/local-first); no per-message cost.
technicalOptions.cons: Violates ToS / ban risk on the user's number; no voice/video calls, no broadcast lists, partial channel support; tracks an undocumented protocol that can break; periodic re-auth and linked-device limits.

technicalOptions.option: Baileys (TypeScript) personal-account engine
technicalOptions.pros: Most feature-complete personal-account lib (v7): all message types, polls+vote aggregation, reactions, view-once, status/stories, newsletters/channels, full group/community ops; pure WebSocket (no browser); huge community; Node/Bun/Deno.
technicalOptions.cons: Violates ToS / ban risk; JS/Node runtime heavier to bundle on mobile than a Go binary; documented supply-chain attack in the npm ecosystem; protocol breakage; v7 introduced breaking changes (migration cost).

technicalOptions.option: Evolution API (self-hosted dual Baileys + Cloud gateway)
technicalOptions.pros: One REST/webhook surface for BOTH unofficial and official lanes; multi-instance with per-instance tokens; native n8n/Typebot/Chatwoot/Dify/OpenAI + RabbitMQ/Kafka/SQS/S3 integrations; matches the platform's existing mcp__whatsapp__* tool surface; fast to stand up (Docker).
technicalOptions.cons: Another service to operate (Node + Postgres/Prisma); Baileys lane still violates ToS; Apache-2.0 brand-protection clause adds logo/notification obligations; you inherit Baileys breakage and ban exposure for the unofficial lane.

technicalOptions.option: Managed unofficial gateway (Whapi.Cloud / WAHA / WasenderApi)
technicalOptions.pros: Fastest to ship; flat monthly per-number price (no per-message fees); instant QR onboarding, no Meta approval; includes groups/channels/webhooks; offloads protocol-update maintenance.
technicalOptions.cons: Third party holds your WhatsApp session (privacy + lock-in); same ToS/ban risk; dependent on vendor uptime and patch cadence; less control than self-hosting; cloud vendors see message content.

technicalOptions.option: WPPConnect (browser-automation engine)
technicalOptions.pros: Exposes features available in the WhatsApp Web UI; ready-to-use REST server; token auth + webhooks; Chatwoot integration.
technicalOptions.cons: Drives a real Chromium session — high RAM/CPU per account, hardest to scale, most fragile to WhatsApp Web updates (tracks alpha builds expiring ~2 months); violates ToS / ban risk; poor fit for lightweight desktop+mobile packaging.

summary: As of mid-2026, Telegram is the most AI-agent-friendly major messaging platform: zero per-message cost, no business verification, free bot creation via @BotFather, and a Bot API that gained explicit agentic capabilities in 2026. Bot API 10.0 (May 8, 2026) added guest mode (bots reply in chats they aren't members of), bot-to-bot communication (mutual opt-in, enabling multi-agent workflows), and managed bots (bots that spawn/configure sub-bots). Bot API 9.5 (March 1, 2026) added sendMessageDraft for native ChatGPT-style streaming in DMs. The core tradeoff is unchanged: the hosted Bot API is the safe, ToS-blessed path but caps uploads at 50 MB / downloads at 20 MB and works only with bot accounts; a self-hosted local Bot API server lifts limits to 2 GB and returns local file paths (ideal for an image/video studio); MTProto/TDLib userbots (GramJS, Telethon, Pyrogram) act as real user accounts with up to ~4 GB files and full-history access but carry real account-ban risk and ToS exposure. For a universal AI-agent platform, the recommended stack is grammY (TypeScript) on the hosted Bot API plus a self-hosted local Bot API server for large media, reserving userbots only for explicit, user-consented account-automation features. Note this space is moving fast: the agentic Bot API features are all 2026-fresh and still maturing.

keyFindings.point: Hosted Bot API file limits are the hard wall: 50 MB up / 20 MB down (10 MB photos)
keyFindings.detail: The cloud Bot API caps uploads at 50 MB and downloads at 20 MB per file; photos capped at 10 MB. This is the single biggest constraint for an image/video studio. getFile + download is a two-step flow. Telegram itself supports much larger user-uploaded files, but a hosted bot literally cannot fetch a >20 MB file a user sends it.

keyFindings.point: Self-hosted local Bot API server raises limits to 2 GB and returns local file paths
keyFindings.detail: Running telegram-bot-api yourself (Docker) lifts upload to 2000 MB and download to effectively unlimited (up to 2000 MB), allows HTTP webhooks on ANY port/IP (cloud requires HTTPS on 443/80/88/8443), and supports up to 100,000 concurrent connections. With --local it serves files via local filesystem path / file:// URI instead of HTTP, eliminating re-upload of large media. Requires api_id/api_hash from my.telegram.org. Ready images: ghcr.io/gramiojs/telegram-bot-api (multi-arch, signed, auto-rebuilt), heilkit/telegram-bot-api.

keyFindings.point: Bot API 10.0 (May 8 2026) is the current version and made bots agentic
keyFindings.detail: Guest mode: bots receive and reply to messages in chats they aren't members of (only the tagged message + replies; cannot enumerate members or read other messages). Bot-to-bot communication: a bot can DM another bot by @username, but ONLY when BOTH sides explicitly opt in (anti-spam). Managed bots: getManagedBotToken/replaceManagedBotToken let one bot create/configure sub-bots. Also deleteMessageReaction, LivePhoto/sendLivePhoto, poll media. Telegram explicitly warns bot-to-bot loops can be infinite — you must add termination safeguards.

keyFindings.point: Native streaming for AI replies arrived in Bot API 9.5 (March 1 2026) via sendMessageDraft
keyFindings.detail: sendMessageDraft is now available to ALL bots and lets a bot push partial content to the UI in real time (ChatGPT-style typing bubble) in private chats. In groups/topics the pattern is send-a-preview-then-editMessageText in place. This replaces the old hacky 'send empty message then spam editMessageText' approach that tripped rate limits. 9.5 also added member tags (<=16 chars) and date_time message entities.

keyFindings.point: Chat Automation lets a USER bot reply on their behalf without a userbot library
keyFindings.detail: Announced May 7 2026 (with 10.0): users go to Settings > Chat Automation and connect a bot to their profile to auto-respond to messages on their behalf, with access controls (exclude existing contacts, limit to new chats). This is a sanctioned, in-app way to get 'reply as me' behavior without running a risky MTProto userbot — relevant if the platform wants a personal-assistant mode.

keyFindings.point: Rate limits: 30 msg/s global per bot, ~1 msg/s per chat, 20 msg/min per group
keyFindings.detail: Global cap ~30 messages/second per bot token (shared across sendMessage, sendMediaGroup, editMessage, sendChatAction). Per-chat ~1 msg/s with short bursts; groups ~20 msg/min. Exceeding returns HTTP 429 with retry_after; repeated abuse triggers a ~30s IP cooldown. Paid Broadcasts (enable in @BotFather) raise the ceiling to 1000 msg/s, costing 0.1 Stars per message above the free 30/s.

keyFindings.point: Userbots (MTProto/TDLib) carry real, immediate ban risk and ToS exposure
keyFindings.detail: Running Telethon/GramJS/Pyrogram on a user account can trigger bans, sometimes immediately on first connect even if the script does nothing; mitigated by aged accounts, occasional official-client use, residential IPs/proxies, and human-like pacing. Telegram API ToS bans 'making actions on behalf of the user without the user's knowledge and consent' and gives a 10-day cure period for client-app violations. FLOOD_WAIT errors throttle aggressive automation. Treat userbots as a calculated risk for opt-in account-automation only, never as primary transport.

keyFindings.point: Mini Apps (Web Apps) provide rich in-Telegram UI with 7 launch surfaces
keyFindings.detail: Mini Apps launch from: profile button, keyboard button (web_app KeyboardButton -> Telegram.WebApp.sendData), inline button (web_app InlineKeyboardButton -> answerWebAppQuery), bot menu button, inline mode, direct link, and attachment menu. Apps receive user id/name/username/language_code + query_id (to send messages back on behalf of user). Bot API 9.0 added DeviceStorage and SecureStorage for client-side persistence. Buttons gained icon_custom_emoji_id and style (color). Ideal for project dashboards, skills marketplace UI, scheduler config inside Telegram.

keyFindings.point: Telegram Stars (XTR) is the only allowed currency for digital goods in bots
keyFindings.detail: All digital-goods/services payments inside bots/Mini Apps MUST use Telegram Stars (currency code XTR) via sendInvoice. ~$0.013 USD per Star for creator revenue planning (live rate via stars_usd_sell_rate_x1000 config). Star subscriptions supported (only allowed period = 30 days, auto-debited monthly). Users buy Stars via Apple/Google IAP or @PremiumBot — meaning Apple/Google take their 30% cut on Star purchases. Max paid-media price rose to 25,000 Stars in 9.3. Directly relevant to monetizing a skills marketplace inside Telegram.

keyFindings.point: Webhook security uses a secret token header; long polling is the low-friction default
keyFindings.detail: setWebhook accepts secret_token; Telegram echoes it in the X-Telegram-Bot-Api-Secret-Token header so you can verify requests are genuinely from Telegram. Webhook needs HTTPS on 443/80/88/8443 (cloud) — or any port on a self-hosted server. Long polling (getUpdates) needs no public endpoint, ideal for desktop/mobile clients behind NAT. grammY runner pulls updates concurrently for high throughput; webhooks suit serverless/edge (Cloudflare Workers, Deno, Bun).

itemsOrTools.name: Telegram Bot API (hosted, v10.0)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Official Telegram-hosted HTTPS interface for bot accounts. Under the hood it runs TDLib over MTProto. Send/receive text, media, inline keyboards, Mini Apps, payments; updates via webhook or long polling. v10.0 (May 8 2026) adds guest mode, bot-to-bot comms, managed bots; v9.5 adds streaming.
itemsOrTools.relevance: This is the primary, ToS-safe transport for the platform's Telegram channel. No ban risk, free, no per-message cost, instant onboarding via @BotFather. The agentic 2026 features (streaming, bot-to-bot, guest mode) map directly onto an AI-agent product.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free. Token from @BotFather. Digital-goods payments via Stars only. Optional Paid Broadcasts: 0.1 Star/msg above 30/s.

itemsOrTools.name: Self-hosted Local Bot API server (tdlib/telegram-bot-api)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Open-source C++ server you run yourself implementing the same Bot API but with 2 GB up / unlimited-up-to-2GB down, HTTP webhooks on any port, 100k concurrent connections, and local file-path / file:// returns instead of HTTP download URLs.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Essential for the image+video studio: lets bots ingest and emit large media (videos, hi-res images, model outputs) that the 50/20 MB cloud limits make impossible. Local file paths avoid double-uploading big files through HTTP.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS (free), self-hosted. Needs api_id/api_hash from my.telegram.org. Docker images: ghcr.io/gramiojs/telegram-bot-api, heilkit/telegram-bot-api.

itemsOrTools.name: grammY (+ @grammyjs/runner, conversations, plugins)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Modern TypeScript-first Telegram bot framework. Best-in-class types, always tracks the latest Bot API version, middleware/composer model, API transformers, conversations plugin for stateful flows, runner for concurrent long-polling, built-in rate-limiting/flood-control plugins.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Recommended primary SDK for the platform's Telegram layer. Runs on Node, Deno, Bun, and Cloudflare Workers/edge — fits desktop, server, and serverless deployments. Type safety and currency reduce maintenance for an AI-agent codebase.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS (MIT), free. npm: grammy. ~1.26M weekly downloads.

itemsOrTools.name: Telegraf
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Long-established Node.js Telegram bot framework, middleware-based, large ecosystem and docs.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Viable alternative to grammY but frequently lags behind on Bot API versions and its TypeScript types are widely criticized as over-complex. Acceptable if team already knows it; otherwise grammY is the better 2026 choice.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS (MIT), free. npm: telegraf. ~857k weekly downloads.

itemsOrTools.name: node-telegram-bot-api (NTBA)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Minimal EventEmitter-based polling library for Telegram bots.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Fine for tiny scripts but does not scale; codebases past ~50 lines become unmaintainable. Avoid for a production AI-agent platform.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS, free. npm: node-telegram-bot-api. ~231k weekly downloads.

itemsOrTools.name: GramJS
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Node.js/browser MTProto client (core based on Telethon). Acts as a full USER account: read full history, large files, features absent from Bot API, raw API invoke.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Only path for true userbot/account-automation from Node (e.g., import a user's own chat history, act as the user). High ban risk; use strictly for explicit, consented account features, never as default transport. Note: latest npm release lineage is older (v2.17.x era) — verify maintenance before depending on it.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS, free. npm: telegram. Needs api_id/api_hash + phone login at my.telegram.org.

itemsOrTools.name: Telethon
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Mature pure-Python asyncio MTProto library for user and bot accounts; large-file support, full API.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Python-side userbot option for account automation or migration/history-import features. Same ban-risk caveats as GramJS.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS, free. PyPI: Telethon (stable 1.43.x). IMPORTANT: the main GitHub repo (LonamiWebs/Telethon) was archived read-only on Feb 21 2026; v1 is maintenance-mode and Telethon 2.0 is not yet released — treat as slowing/uncertain.

itemsOrTools.name: Pyrogram
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Async Python MTProto framework for users and bots; clean API, large-file handling.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Alternative Python userbot library; same use cases and same ban-risk/ToS caveats as Telethon. Verify maintenance cadence before committing.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS, free. PyPI: Pyrogram. Needs api_id/api_hash.

itemsOrTools.name: TDLib (tdlib/td)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Telegram's official cross-platform C++ client library (powers the hosted Bot API). Exposes a C/JSON interface (tdjson) callable from any language; handles MTProto, caching, updates, large files.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The most robust foundation for a full-featured native userbot or client embedded in the desktop/mobile app, with proper caching and reliability. Heavier to integrate (C++ build, JSON layer) than GramJS but far more battle-tested for high-load.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS (Boost license), free. Bindings: tdl (npm, Node), python-telegram (Python), etc. JSON interface is semver-versioned.

itemsOrTools.name: Telegram Mini Apps (Web Apps)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Embed full web UIs inside Telegram launched from 7 surfaces; receive user identity + query_id, send data back via sendData/answerWebAppQuery; client-side DeviceStorage/SecureStorage (9.0).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Native in-Telegram surface for the platform's project dashboards, skills marketplace, scheduler config, and studio previews — no app-store install needed, runs identically on mobile and desktop Telegram.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free; just host the web app and reference its URL in buttons/menu. JS SDK: @telegram-apps/sdk / telegram-web-app.js.

itemsOrTools.name: Telegram Stars (XTR) payments
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: In-app virtual currency; the mandatory currency for digital goods/services in bots and Mini Apps via sendInvoice; supports 30-day subscriptions.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The monetization rail for a skills marketplace / paid agent jobs delivered through Telegram. ~$0.013/Star to creators; remember Apple/Google take ~30% on Star purchases, so factor margin.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Built into Bot API. Users buy Stars via Apple/Google IAP or @PremiumBot. Withdrawals/payouts via Fragment.

recommendations: Use the hosted Bot API + grammY (TypeScript) as the DEFAULT Telegram transport for the platform: zero cost, zero ban risk, instant onboarding via @BotFather, always-current Bot API support, and runs on Node/Deno/Bun/Cloudflare Workers to match desktop, server, and edge deployments.

Stand up a self-hosted local Bot API server (Docker, e.g. ghcr.io/gramiojs/telegram-bot-api) for the image+video studio path so bots can ingest/emit files up to 2 GB and use local file-path returns instead of being capped at 50 MB up / 20 MB down. Route only large-media flows through it; keep everyday messaging on the cloud API or the same local server.

Implement command-to-job triggering via webhooks with a secret_token (verify the X-Telegram-Bot-Api-Secret-Token header) for server/cloud deploys, and fall back to long polling with @grammyjs/runner for desktop/mobile clients behind NAT. Map slash commands and @-mentions (guest mode) to agent jobs.

Adopt Bot API 9.5 sendMessageDraft for streaming AI replies in DMs, and the send-preview-then-editMessageText pattern in groups/topics, to render Claude/GPT output ChatGPT-style without tripping the 30 msg/s and ~1 msg/s-per-chat rate limits. Build a central rate-limiter/queue (grammY's flood-control/throttler plugins) around all sends.

Leverage bot-to-bot communication (10.0, mutual opt-in) to model multi-agent workflows (e.g., Claude Code engine bot delegating to a GPT review bot), but hard-code loop/termination guards (max-hops, dedup) since Telegram explicitly warns bot-to-bot can loop infinitely.

Build the in-Telegram control surface (project dashboards, skills marketplace, scheduler) as a Mini App launched from the bot menu/keyboard buttons; use DeviceStorage/SecureStorage for client-side state and answerWebAppQuery to push results back. This gives identical mobile+desktop UX without separate native builds.

For any 'act as the user / personal assistant' feature, PREFER Telegram's sanctioned Chat Automation (Settings > Chat Automation, connect a bot to the user profile) over an MTProto userbot — it gets reply-as-me behavior with no account-ban risk.

Restrict MTProto/TDLib userbots (GramJS/Telethon/Pyrogram/TDLib) to explicit, user-consented, opt-in account-automation or history-import features only. Isolate them per-user, use aged accounts + residential proxies + human-like pacing, respect FLOOD_WAIT, and surface clear ban-risk warnings in the UI. Never make a userbot the platform's default Telegram path.

Monetize Telegram-delivered skills/jobs with Telegram Stars (XTR) via sendInvoice/subscriptions, but model the Apple/Google ~30% IAP cut into pricing; consider directing higher-value purchases to web/Fragment where possible.
risks: Userbot account-ban risk is real and can be immediate: Telethon/GramJS/Pyrogram on a user account can get the account banned on first connect, and the API ToS bans acting on behalf of a user without consent. Any userbot feature risks losing the user's Telegram account and the api_id/api_hash.

Hosted Bot API hard caps (50 MB up / 20 MB down, 10 MB photos) make a media studio impossible without the self-hosted local server — but the local server adds ops burden (Docker, api_id/api_hash, scaling, file storage, keeping the binary current with TDLib/Bot API releases).

Fast-moving, uncertain surface: the headline agentic features (guest mode, bot-to-bot, managed bots in 10.0; streaming in 9.5) are all 2026-fresh, semantics may shift, and behavior/limits are still settling — build abstractions so you can adapt to changelog churn.

Rate limits (30 msg/s global, ~1 msg/s per chat, 20 msg/min per group) will throttle streaming/broadcast-heavy agents; mis-tuned editMessageText streaming triggers 429s and 30s IP cooldowns. Paid Broadcasts (0.1 Star/msg) is the only sanctioned escape and adds cost.
Bot-to-bot loops: Telegram explicitly warns automated bot-to-bot chains can loop infinitely; without termination guards a multi-agent setup can spam, burn rate limits, or incur Star costs.

Vendor/lock-in: Stars (XTR) is the only allowed in-bot currency for digital goods and routes through Apple/Google IAP (~30% cut), constraining marketplace economics; Mini Apps and payments are Telegram-proprietary surfaces.

Library maintenance uncertainty: Telethon's main repo was archived read-only (Feb 2026) with v1 in maintenance mode and 2.0 unreleased; GramJS's released versions lag. Depending on userbot libraries risks running on slowing/forked codebases — validate maintenance and pin versions.

Self-hosted local Bot API server requires api_id/api_hash tied to a personal Telegram account; abuse or ToS issues there could jeopardize that account/credentials, and you own all reliability/uptime for large-file flows.

technicalOptions.option: Hosted Bot API + grammY (cloud)
technicalOptions.pros: ToS-safe, zero ban risk, free, no per-message cost, instant @BotFather onboarding, always-current Bot API (streaming, guest mode, bot-to-bot), runs on Node/Deno/Bun/Cloudflare Workers, webhook secret-token security.
technicalOptions.cons: Hard file caps (50 MB up / 20 MB down / 10 MB photos), bot accounts only (no acting as a real user beyond sanctioned Chat Automation), 30 msg/s global rate ceiling.

technicalOptions.option: Self-hosted Local Bot API server (tdlib/telegram-bot-api)
technicalOptions.pros: 2 GB uploads, ~unlimited (to 2 GB) downloads, HTTP webhooks on any port, 100k concurrent connections, local file-path/file:// returns (no double upload) — unlocks the image+video studio.
technicalOptions.cons: Ops/maintenance burden (Docker, scaling, storage), needs personal api_id/api_hash, you own uptime, must keep binary current with upstream TDLib/Bot API.

technicalOptions.option: MTProto userbot via GramJS / Telethon / Pyrogram
technicalOptions.pros: Full user-account powers: full chat history, larger files (~up to ~4 GB on user accounts), features absent from Bot API, act as the actual user, history import/migration.
technicalOptions.cons: Real and possibly immediate account-ban risk; ToS prohibits non-consented automation; FLOOD_WAIT throttling; library maintenance uncertainty (Telethon archived/maintenance-mode, GramJS lagging); per-user session/credential management complexity.

technicalOptions.option: TDLib (tdjson) embedded native client
technicalOptions.pros: Official, most robust/battle-tested client (powers the Bot API), full caching, reliable under high load, callable from any language via JSON interface, large-file support, ideal embedded in desktop/mobile app.
technicalOptions.cons: Heaviest integration (C++ build + JSON layer), steeper learning curve; as a userbot still carries account-ban/ToS exposure; overkill if only sending/receiving messages.

technicalOptions.option: Sanctioned Chat Automation (user connects a bot to their profile)
technicalOptions.pros: Gets 'reply as me' behavior with NO ban risk and no userbot library; in-app access controls; officially supported (May 2026).
technicalOptions.cons: Newer/uncertain capability surface; less flexible than a raw userbot; still bound by Bot API limits and what the user grants.

summary: Build a per-project scheduler on BullMQ (Redis: cron Job Schedulers, global rate limiter, native OTel) or pg-boss (Postgres-only: cron lock, singleton dedup, opt-out retries). Claude Code Routines is a research preview with a one-hour minimum interval and per-account daily cap, so use it only as a dispatch backend, not the scheduler. Managed options: Trigger.dev v4, Inngest, Temporal, Cloudflare DO Alarms. Adopt Quartz misfire policies plus idempotency keys. Messaging egress is the top risk: unofficial WhatsApp (Baileys/Evolution) is high ban risk and Meta blocks AI chatbots from January 2026, so use the official WhatsApp Cloud API; Telegram (30 per sec global, 1 per sec per chat) is the safe free default.

keyFindings.point: Routines one-hour minimum interval and daily run cap; green status is not task success
keyFindings.detail: self-host BullMQ or pg-boss as your scheduler and use Routines as a dispatch backend

itemsOrTools.name: BullMQ or pg-boss
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: self-hosted durable cron queues on Redis or Postgres
itemsOrTools.relevance: strongest engines for a per-project scheduler you control

recommendations: Run BullMQ or pg-boss as the scheduler; dispatch heavy work to Routines; lead messaging with Telegram and the official WhatsApp Cloud API; use Quartz misfire policies plus idempotency keys
risks: Unofficial WhatsApp ban risk and Meta AI-chatbot block January 2026; Routines too coarse for multi-tenant; at-least-once delivery double-sends without idempotency keys

summary: As of mid-2026, AI video generation has matured into a commodity API layer with several distinct tiers: frontier closed models (Google Veo 3.1, OpenAI Sora 2, Kling 3.0, Runway Gen-4.5), strong open-weight models (Alibaba Wan 2.5/2.6 under Apache 2.0, Lightricks LTX-2 with native 4K+audio), and budget high-volume models (ByteDance Seedance 2.0, MiniMax Hailuo 2.3). Clips are still short (8s native for Veo, 4-20s for Sora 2, up to 15s for Kling 3.0), but native synchronized audio and image-to-video are now table stakes on the leading models. Critically, OpenAI is sunsetting the Sora 2 API on September 24, 2026 (consumer app already killed April 26, 2026), so building hard against Sora is a dead end. The decisive architectural choice for a universal agent platform is to NOT integrate any single vendor directly, but to route everything through an aggregator (fal.ai is the clear leader, "OpenRouter for media," 600+ models, one key, webhook async, swap-by-string; Replicate is the alternative). Pricing spans roughly $0.02-0.10/sec for budget models up to $0.40-0.70/sec for top-tier Veo 3.1 4K-with-audio and Sora 2 Pro 1080p. The space changes monthly: Kling 3.0 (Feb 2026) currently tops the ELO leaderboard, Seedance 2.0 and LTX-2 landed in early 2026, and Veo/Wan are on fast release cadences.

keyFindings.point: Sora 2 API is being killed Sept 24, 2026 - do not build directly against it
keyFindings.detail: OpenAI officially announced (notification March 24, 2026) that sora-2, sora-2-pro and the Videos API (POST /v1/videos) sunset September 24, 2026; the consumer Sora app was discontinued April 26, 2026. Official API priced $0.10/sec (sora-2 720p), $0.30/sec (sora-2-pro 720p), $0.50/sec (Pro 1024p/1792x1024), $0.70/sec (Pro true 1080p); batch tier is ~half. Durations 4/8/12/16/20s. Strong content guardrails reject real people/public figures, copyrighted characters/music, and input images containing human faces by default. Reverse-engineered third-party Sora APIs were largely killed by OpenAI's Jan 2026 Cloudflare hardening (return 403) and account-pool terminations - high ban/disruption risk, ToS-violating, avoid.

keyFindings.point: Google Veo 3.1 is the most production-credible frontier model with a clean official API
keyFindings.detail: Model IDs in Gemini API: veo-3.1-generate-preview, veo-3.1-fast-generate-preview, veo-3.1-lite-generate-preview, plus veo-3.0-generate-001 / veo-3.0-fast-generate-001 and veo-2.0-generate-001. Max native duration 8s; resolutions 720p/1080p/4K (Lite 720p/1080p); native audio + image-to-video on all 3.1 tiers. Pricing tiers: Lite ~$0.03-0.05/sec (no audio), Fast ~$0.10-0.15/sec, Quality ~$0.20-0.40/sec; 4K with audio ~$0.60/sec. Available via Gemini Developer API and Vertex AI - first-party, reliable, enterprise SLAs.

keyFindings.point: Kling 3.0 currently leads quality benchmarks; accessed via fal.ai not a clean first-party Western API
keyFindings.detail: Kling 3.0 (released Feb 5, 2026) holds #1 ELO (1243), ahead of Veo 3.1, Runway Gen-4.5, Pika 2.2. Adds up to 15s clips, 4K image gen, chain-of-thought scene coherence, multi-shot consistent characters, multi-language native audio, multi-character lip-synced dialogue. Initially early-access to Ultra subscribers. Best programmatic access is via fal.ai: Kling 2.5 Turbo Pro $0.07/sec (i2v, no audio, best value); Kling 2.6 Pro $0.07/sec no-audio, $0.14/sec with native audio, $0.168/sec with voice control. Kuaishou (China-owned) - geopolitical/data-residency consideration.

keyFindings.point: fal.ai is the strategic integration point - one API, 600+ models, swap-by-string, webhook async
keyFindings.detail: fal.ai ('OpenRouter of media') exposes Veo 3.1, Sora 2/2 Pro, Kling 2.5/2.6/3.0, Wan 2.2/2.5/2.6, Seedance 2.0, Hailuo 2.3, Pika 2.2, LTX-2, Luma Ray behind one key/one billing dashboard/one code pattern - changing models is swapping an endpoint string. Pay-as-you-go, no subscription/minimums, no hidden CDN/storage fees. Async queue with webhook_url (POSTs result on completion) plus polling. Per-model licensing - most carry a 'Commercial use' badge but must be checked per model. This decouples our app from any single vendor and absorbs the fast model churn.

keyFindings.point: Open-weight models (Wan, LTX-2) enable self-hosting / cost control / no-ToS-ban escape hatch
keyFindings.detail: Alibaba Wan 2.1/2.2/2.5 (and 2.6 narrative model) are Apache 2.0 - weights+training+inference code on GitHub/HuggingFace, no royalties, commercial use allowed; official DashScope API ~$0.10-0.15 per 5s 720p clip. Wan 3.0 (~60B params, 4K, 30s single-pass) expected mid-2026. Lightricks LTX-2 (open-sourced Jan 6, 2026) is first production-ready OPEN model with native synchronized 4K audio+video up to 20s at 50fps; free for academic + commercial under $10M ARR, paid enterprise license above; available self-serve and via fal/Replicate/ComfyUI. These give us a fallback that no vendor can revoke.

keyFindings.point: Budget high-volume tier is led by Seedance 2.0 and Hailuo 2.3 for drafts/iteration
keyFindings.detail: ByteDance Seedance 2.0 (live on fal April 9, 2026): 480p/720p/1080p, 4-15s, $0.022/sec (Fast) to $0.247/sec (Pro) per official fal listing (some sources cite $0.682/sec for 1080p Pro - pricing volatile). Frequently cited as cheapest production-quality 1080p API. MiniMax Hailuo 02/2.3 on fal: 768p i2v ~$0.045/sec, 512p ~$0.017/sec, Pro 1080p ~$0.08/sec; Hailuo 2.3 Fast cuts batch cost up to 50%. Use these for thumbnails/previews/drafts, reserve Veo/Kling for final renders.

keyFindings.point: Runway offers a mature first-party credit-based API but is comparatively expensive
keyFindings.detail: Runway API: credits = $0.01 each. Per-second credit costs: gen4_turbo 5 c/s ($0.05/s), gen4.5 12 c/s ($0.12/s), gen4_aleph 15 c/s, aleph2 28 c/s (56-credit min/gen), veo3/veo3.1 40 c/s (resold), seedance2 36-40 c/s, act_two 5 c/s. Also image models (gen4_image 5-8 c, gen4_image_turbo 2 c) and audio/real-time avatars. Strong for video-to-video editing (Aleph), Act-Two performance capture, and character/style consistency. Good as a specialty provider, not the default volume path.

keyFindings.point: Luma Ray3/Ray3.14 is a solid mid-tier with separate API billing and good speed
keyFindings.detail: Luma Ray3 supports 540p/720p/1080p, durations 5/10/15/20s; Ray3.14 update adds native 1080p, 4x faster, 3x lower cost. Public API rates (~May 2026): Ray-2 ~$0.08/sec; Scale provisioned-throughput plans $2,100-$3,800/mo (8-unit min). Note: Dream Machine app credits and API wallet are billed separately and do NOT transfer - fund the API wallet independently. Available on fal as well.

itemsOrTools.name: fal.ai (aggregator)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Unified inference API + CDN for 600+ generative media models (Veo, Sora, Kling, Wan, Seedance, Hailuo, Pika, LTX-2, Luma). One API key, one billing dashboard, swap models by changing an endpoint string. Async queue with webhook_url callbacks and polling; hosts storage/CDN at no extra fee.
itemsOrTools.relevance: THE recommended integration layer for our video studio. Decouples us from any single vendor, absorbs the monthly model churn, gives consistent async/webhook semantics that fit our per-project scheduler. Lets the dynamic skills marketplace expose 'video model' skills that just point at different fal endpoints.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Pay-as-you-go, no subscription/minimums, per-second or per-megapixel by model. API key auth. e.g. Kling 2.5 Turbo Pro $0.07/s, Veo 3.1 Fast $0.10/s, Sora 2 Pro $0.30-0.50/s, Seedance 2.0 Fast $0.022/s.

itemsOrTools.name: Replicate (aggregator)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Alternative multi-model hosting platform; runs hosted versions of major and open video models with per-run/per-second billing, async predictions + webhooks, and the ability to deploy custom/open models (Wan, LTX-2) on managed GPUs.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Secondary aggregator / failover to fal.ai to avoid single-aggregator lock-in, and the path for self-deploying open-weight models (Wan, LTX-2) without managing raw GPUs. Use as redundancy for reliability SLAs.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Pay-per-run/per-second; API token auth; webhooks on prediction completion. Per-model pricing varies.

itemsOrTools.name: Google Veo 3.1 (+ Fast/Lite)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Frontier text-to-video and image-to-video with native synchronized audio. Model IDs veo-3.1-generate-preview / -fast- / -lite-. 8s native, 720p/1080p/4K, strong realism and character consistency.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Default 'quality with audio' engine for final renders in our video studio. First-party Google API = reliability, enterprise SLA, clearest ToS. Also reachable via fal for unified billing.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Gemini Developer API + Vertex AI. Lite ~$0.03-0.05/s, Fast ~$0.10-0.15/s, Quality ~$0.20-0.40/s, 4K+audio ~$0.60/s. Google API key / GCP project.

itemsOrTools.name: OpenAI Sora 2 / Sora 2 Pro
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Text/image-to-video with synchronized audio, 4-20s, up to true 1080p (Pro). POST /v1/videos async job model with input_reference image, webhooks (video.completed/failed), thumbnail+spritesheet assets.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Currently high quality but DEPRECATED - shuts down Sept 24, 2026. Do NOT build a hard dependency. At most, offer behind a feature flag that auto-falls-back to Veo/Kling, and plan for removal. Heavy guardrails (no real people, no input faces) limit use cases.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Official OpenAI API, Tier-2 (>$10 spend) required. $0.10/s (720p) to $0.70/s (Pro 1080p); batch ~half. Sunsets 2026-09-24.

itemsOrTools.name: Kling 3.0 (Kuaishou)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Current #1 ELO (1243) quality model. Up to 15s clips, 4K image gen, chain-of-thought scene coherence, multi-shot consistent characters, multi-language native audio, multi-character lip-synced dialogue. Earlier 2.5/2.6 widely available.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best-in-class 'hero shot' / cinematic + dialogue engine to offer as a premium option. Access via fal.ai (cleanest path). Flag: Chinese-owned (Kuaishou) - data-residency/geopolitical review needed before enterprise/default use.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Via fal: Kling 2.5 Turbo Pro $0.07/s; 2.6 Pro $0.07/s (no audio), $0.14/s (audio), $0.168/s (voice control). Kling 3.0 rolling out from Ultra early-access.

itemsOrTools.name: Runway Gen-4 / Gen-4.5 / Aleph / Act-Two
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: First-party credit-based API. Gen-4.5 t2v/i2v, Aleph video-to-video editing, Act-Two performance/lip-sync capture, gen4_image/turbo image models, audio + real-time avatars. Strong character/style consistency and editing.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Specialty provider for video EDITING (Aleph), performance capture (Act-Two), and consistent-character workflows our image+video studio can expose as distinct skills. Not the cost-leader for raw t2v volume.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Credits $0.01 each via dev portal. gen4_turbo $0.05/s, gen4.5 $0.12/s, aleph2 $0.28/s (56c min), act_two $0.05/s, veo3.1 resold $0.40/s. API key auth.

itemsOrTools.name: Alibaba Wan 2.5 / 2.6 (+ Wan 3.0 expected)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Open-weight (Apache 2.0) text/image-to-video family with native audio (2.5+) and multi-shot narrative storytelling + consistent characters (2.6). Weights/training/inference on GitHub+HuggingFace. Wan 3.0 (~60B, 4K, 30s single-pass) expected mid-2026.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Strategic self-host / no-vendor-lock escape hatch and cost floor. Run on our own/rented GPUs or via Replicate/fal for surge. Apache 2.0 = clean commercial rights, no per-call ToS-ban risk. Good default 'free/cheap tier' engine for the skills marketplace.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Open weights, free, Apache 2.0. Official DashScope API ~$0.10-0.15 per 5s 720p clip. Self-host or via fal/Replicate.

itemsOrTools.name: Lightricks LTX-2
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: First production-ready OPEN-weights model with native synchronized 4K audio+video, up to 20s at 50fps, accurate lip-sync. Full weights+inference+training released Jan 6, 2026.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best open option when you need audio+video together at high res without a closed vendor. Self-host for unlimited internal generation, or use via fal/Replicate. License is generous (free under $10M ARR) - check threshold for our entity.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Open weights; free academic + commercial under $10M ARR, enterprise license above. Self-serve LTX API + fal/Replicate/ComfyUI/OpenArt.

itemsOrTools.name: ByteDance Seedance 2.0
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: High-volume, low-cost t2v/i2v. 480p/720p/1080p, 4-15s. Frequently cited as cheapest production-quality 1080p API. Fast and Pro variants.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Default 'draft / high-volume iteration' engine. Generate many cheap previews, then upscale the chosen one with Veo/Kling. Pairs well with our per-project scheduler for batch jobs. ByteDance-owned - same geopolitical note as Kling.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Via fal (live Apr 9, 2026): Fast $0.022/s, Pro $0.247/s (some sources cite 1080p Pro ~$0.682/s - pricing volatile). API key.

itemsOrTools.name: MiniMax Hailuo 02 / 2.3
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Budget cinematic i2v/t2v with complex-motion handling and a media-agent mode. 512p/768p/1080p (Pro), ~6-10s clips, Fast variant for cheaper batch.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Another cheap drafting/iteration engine and i2v option for the studio's low-cost tier. Useful for motion-heavy shots where budget models often fail.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Via fal: 768p i2v ~$0.045/s, 512p ~$0.017/s, Pro 1080p ~$0.08/s; Hailuo 2.3 Fast ~50% cheaper batch. Consumer plans $7.99-$199.99/mo.

itemsOrTools.name: Luma Ray3 / Ray3.14 (Dream Machine)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Reasoning-driven t2v/i2v. 540p/720p/1080p, 5/10/15/20s. Ray3.14 adds native 1080p, 4x speed, 3x lower cost.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Solid mid-tier alternative to round out model choice and provide redundancy. Note separate API billing wallet from the consumer app. Available on fal for unified billing.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Dream Machine API separate from app; Ray-2 ~$0.08/s; Scale provisioned plans $2,100-$3,800/mo (8-unit min). API key / via fal.

itemsOrTools.name: Pika 2.2
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: t2v/i2v plus Pikascenes and Pikaframes (keyframe transitions/effects). Modern self-serve access hosted on fal.ai.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Niche creative-effects engine (transitions, frame interpolation) to expose as a specialty studio skill. Not a default quality leader; use selectively.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Modern API via fal.ai (clean endpoints, usage dashboard, billing); legacy Pika API also exists. Per-second pricing via fal.

technicalOptions.option: Aggregator-first (fal.ai primary, Replicate secondary) - RECOMMENDED
technicalOptions.pros: Single integration absorbs the monthly model churn; swap models by changing a string; uniform async/webhook semantics fit our per-project scheduler; built-in CDN/storage; no GPU ops; lets the skills marketplace expose 'pick your model' cheaply; easy multi-vendor failover.
technicalOptions.cons: ~10-30% markup vs raw first-party in some cases; dependent on aggregator uptime/policy; still inherits each underlying model's ToS and per-model commercial-license check; another vendor in the chain.

technicalOptions.option: Direct first-party APIs (Google Veo via Vertex, Runway, Luma)
technicalOptions.pros: Best reliability/SLA and clearest ToS for enterprise; sometimes cheaper than aggregator markup; first access to new model versions; direct support.
technicalOptions.cons: N separate integrations, keys, billing, and auth models; you eat every model launch/deprecation yourself (cf. Sora sunset); slows the skills marketplace; more code to maintain.

technicalOptions.option: Self-host open-weight models (Wan 2.x Apache-2.0, LTX-2 open weights)
technicalOptions.pros: No per-call ToS/ban risk; cost floor at scale; full data privacy/residency control; no vendor can revoke; works offline/on-prem and supports our P2P/mobile-sovereignty story; unlimited internal/draft generation.
technicalOptions.cons: Real GPU ops (A100/H100-class), cold-start latency, scaling and queueing burden; quality lags top closed models (Veo/Kling); LTX-2 commercial license threshold ($10M ARR) must be tracked; engineering-heavy.

technicalOptions.option: Hybrid tiered routing (RECOMMENDED end-state)
technicalOptions.pros: Cheap models (Seedance/Hailuo/Wan) for drafts+previews, frontier (Veo 3.1/Kling 3.0) for final renders, open self-host (Wan/LTX-2) for free-tier+privacy+failover; cost-optimized per request; resilient to any single vendor change; matches per-project budgets.
technicalOptions.cons: Routing/orchestration complexity; need a normalized capability+price abstraction layer and consistent moderation across heterogeneous models; more testing surface.

recommendations: Build the video studio on an aggregator abstraction (fal.ai primary, Replicate as failover) rather than wiring vendors directly. Define an internal normalized 'VideoJob' interface (prompt, init/reference image, duration, resolution, audio bool, model-id, webhook) so models are swappable by config - this is what the dynamic skills marketplace should wrap, with each 'video model' as a skill that just selects an endpoint.

Do NOT take a hard dependency on OpenAI Sora 2 - it sunsets September 24, 2026. If offered at all, gate it behind a feature flag with automatic fallback to Veo 3.1 / Kling, and plan code removal before that date.

Default routing tiers: drafts/previews/high-volume -> Seedance 2.0 Fast (~$0.022/s) or Hailuo 2.3 Fast / Wan; final 'with audio' renders -> Veo 3.1 (Fast for cost, Quality for hero shots); cinematic/dialogue hero shots -> Kling 3.0; video editing/performance-capture -> Runway Aleph/Act-Two. Expose model choice + a live cost estimate to the user before generation.

Stand up a self-hosted open-weight path (Wan 2.x Apache-2.0 now, LTX-2 for 4K+audio, Wan 3.0 when it ships) on Replicate or our own GPUs. Use it for the free tier, for offline/P2P/mobile-sovereignty scenarios, and as a no-ToS-ban failover. Track LTX-2's $10M-ARR commercial-license threshold.

Implement async-by-default: submit job + webhook_url, persist job IDs in the per-project scheduler, poll as fallback, and surface progress in WhatsApp/Telegram and the desktop+mobile UI. Plan for multi-minute latency (1080p/long jobs can take materially longer) - never block a chat turn on a render.

Centralize content moderation and rights handling in OUR layer: enforce no-real-people / no-copyrighted-character / no-impersonation policy uniformly before dispatch (Sora-style guardrails are inconsistent across vendors). Log model, version, cost, and license per generation for audit and per-project billing.

Abstract pricing into a config-driven cost table refreshed regularly - prices and model rankings shift monthly (Kling 3.0 Feb 2026, Seedance 2.0 + LTX-2 early 2026). Use GPT (our review model) plus a small eval set to periodically re-rank models on quality/cost and auto-update default routing.

For enterprise/data-sensitive customers, prefer first-party Google Veo on Vertex AI or self-hosted open models, and add a geopolitical/data-residency flag that excludes Chinese-owned models (Kling/Kuaishou, Seedance/ByteDance, Wan/Alibaba-hosted) when required.
risks: Vendor deprecation: OpenAI Sora 2 API hard-shutdown Sept 24, 2026; any direct Sora integration becomes dead code. Fast model churn (new versions monthly) means hardcoded model IDs rot quickly - mitigate with config-driven routing.

ToS / ban risk: Third-party 'reverse-engineered' Sora/Veo APIs violate provider ToS and were largely killed by OpenAI's Jan 2026 Cloudflare hardening (403s) + account-pool bans. Never route through unofficial reverse APIs - only official first-party or legitimate aggregators (fal/Replicate).

Cost blowups: Top-tier 4K+audio (Veo 3.1 ~$0.60/s, Sora 2 Pro 1080p $0.70/s, Seedance 1080p Pro up to ~$0.68/s) can make a single minute of video cost $35-45. Without per-project budgets, rate limits, and cheap-draft-then-upscale flows, costs spike fast. Aggregators add markup.

Vendor lock-in: Building directly against one vendor's bespoke API (or even one aggregator) is fragile. Mitigate with a normalized interface, fal+Replicate redundancy, and an always-available open-weight self-host path (Wan/LTX-2).

Reliability/latency: Renders take minutes; 1080p/long jobs much longer; queues back up under load. Synchronous UX will time out. Requires async jobs, webhooks, retries, and graceful degradation/fallback across models.

Content/legal liability: Generated video can produce real-person likenesses, copyrighted characters, or deceptive deepfakes. Guardrails differ wildly across models (Sora strict, open models permissive). We must own moderation, provenance/watermarking (e.g., C2PA/SynthID where available), and usage logging or carry impersonation/IP-infringement liability.

Commercial-rights ambiguity: Each aggregator model has its own license - 'Commercial use' must be verified per model. Open models have ARR thresholds (LTX-2 <$10M). Misuse risks license breach. Track license + version per generation.

Geopolitical/data-residency: Best-value and top-quality models (Kling/Kuaishou, Seedance/ByteDance, Wan/Alibaba) are Chinese-owned; prompts/images may transit Chinese infrastructure. Enterprise/government users may forbid this - need a region/ownership filter on the router.

summary: As of mid-2026, building "avatar + voice + captions + b-roll → finished video" is a solved assembly problem with mature, API-first vendors at every layer, but the market is fragmenting along two axes: batch/rendered video (HeyGen, Synthesia, Argil, Captions/Mirage) vs. real-time conversational streaming (HeyGen LiveAvatar, D-ID V4, Tavus CVI, Anam), and proprietary hosted avatars vs. composable OSS lip-sync (MuseTalk, LatentSync, Wav2Lip) over your own video gen. Voice/TTS has consolidated around ElevenLabs (quality + breadth, incl. commercially-cleared Music v2), Cartesia Sonic-3 (lowest latency ~40-90ms, cheapest at ~$0.03/min), and OpenAI (gpt-4o-mini-tts steerable; gpt-realtime/GPT-Realtime-2 for speech-to-speech agents). For programmatic kinetic typography and HTML-driven motion graphics, Remotion (React video) is the de-facto engine, rendering server-side or on Lambda for pennies per clip, but it requires a paid Company License above 3 employees. The biggest 2026 risks are music licensing (Suno/Udio still in active UMG/Sony litigation; ElevenLabs Music v2 is the clean alternative), avatar consent/ban risk (HeyGen/D-ID strictly require verified consent and ban likeness/celebrity cloning), and vendor lock-in on hosted avatars. For a universal AI-agent platform, the recommended stack is composable: ElevenLabs/Cartesia for voice, Sync.so or self-hosted MuseTalk/LatentSync for lip-sync, HeyGen/Argil API for turnkey avatar clips, fal.ai as a single gateway to video models (Kling, Veo, Wan), and Remotion as the deterministic assembler/caption/typography layer.

keyFindings.point: HeyGen is the most complete avatar API but is mid-migration on real-time
keyFindings.detail: REST API uses X-Api-Key header, POST https://api.heygen.com/v3/videos. Products: Avatar IV/Avatar V (Digital Twin face clone), Photo Avatar (talking head from one still), Video Translation/dubbing 40+ langs, Lipsync (audio replacement), Starfish TTS voices, Video Agent (prompt→video). Pricing is pay-as-you-go from $5 wallet top-up, ~$1/min for standard 720/1080p, Avatar IV $4/min 1080p, Avatar V $0.05/sec ($3/min), translation $2/min, Video Agent $2/min; no free API tier. CRITICAL: the @heygen/streaming-avatar SDK is deprecated and Interactive Avatar sunsets 2026-03-31, replaced by LiveAvatar (@heygen/liveavatar-web-sdk, WebRTC, LiveKit input support). v1/v2 REST endpoints supported through 2026-10-31; v3 is active.

keyFindings.point: ElevenLabs Music v2 is the only AI music with explicit commercial clearance — decisive for a platform
keyFindings.detail: Launched ~May 2026, trained exclusively on licensed stems (deals with labels/publishers/artists), cleared for broad online+offline commercial use on all paid plans (film/TV/studio-games require Enterprise). Music v1 (model_id music_v1) is live in API; v2 adds long-form section-by-section composition, mid-track genre transitions, inpainting. This sidesteps the Suno/Udio legal cloud entirely.

keyFindings.point: Suno and Udio carry unresolved copyright litigation despite partial settlements
keyFindings.detail: Suno settled with Warner Music (Nov 2025), retired/deprecated ALL unlicensed models permanently, acquired Songkick; Udio settled with UMG (Oct 2025) for a new licensed platform. BUT Sony's claims vs Udio remain active, UMG+Sony vs Suno has no trial date (stalled Apr 2026), and the musicians' union (AFM) is now suing UMG/WMG over the settlements. Suno has NO official public API (only third-party resellers $0.014-$0.111/song); Udio also lacks a public dev API. Commercial rights only attach to tracks made WHILE subscribed to Pro/Premier — not retroactive.

keyFindings.point: Avatar consent and likeness are hard ToS gates with real ban risk
keyFindings.detail: HeyGen (and D-ID similarly) REQUIRE verified explicit consent of any real person before custom-avatar creation; cloning real individuals/celebrities/public figures without consent is strictly prohibited and ML+human moderation suspends/terminates accounts and may report to authorities. Prohibited content: deceptive, political campaigning, sexual, infringing, scam/misinformation. API access is Business/Enterprise-tier only. You own rights to avatars you legitimately create. For a multi-user platform, you must build a consent-capture + verification flow or you inherit ban liability for users' misuse.

keyFindings.point: Real-time conversational avatars are a distinct sub-market with sub-1s latency targets
keyFindings.detail: For natural turn-taking you need audio round-trip <300ms and mouth response <1s. D-ID V4 Expressive Visual Agents: sub-0.5s turns, up to 4K, 120+ languages, from $5.90/mo, Build API tier from $18/mo (32 min streaming or 16 min video); API ~$5.90/min. Tavus CVI: free 25 live min, Starter $59/mo (~100 conversational min, 3 concurrent streams), Growth $299/mo (500+ min), custom LLM+TTS injection, replica creation from short samples, SOC2/HIPAA. Anam: API-first, ~180ms model latency / sub-900ms turn-taking, per-minute pricing, SOC2 Type II + HIPAA, US+EU regions, zero-retention enterprise.

keyFindings.point: Lip-sync is available both as managed API (Sync.so) and strong OSS for self-hosting
keyFindings.detail: Sync.so (founders of Wav2Lip): lipsync-2 / lipsync-2-pro, frame-accurate, per-second billing (legacy ~$0.02-0.025/sec at 25fps; ~$3/min via 3rd-party), API on free tier + cost-estimation endpoint, plans Hobbyist $5 / Creator $19 / Growth $49 / Scale $249. Also on fal.ai and Replicate (sync/lipsync-2-pro). OSS: MuseTalk (real-time 30fps+ on V100, latent-space inpainting, near-photoreal), ByteDance LatentSync 1.6 (512x512 diffusion, best visuals), Wav2Lip (most accurate sync, lightweight). Self-hosting OSS removes per-minute fees and ToS exposure but needs GPU ops.

keyFindings.point: Remotion is the programmatic motion-graphics/caption engine but has license + AWS cost layers
keyFindings.detail: React-based programmatic video; renders locally or via Remotion Lambda. Lambda AWS costs are tiny: 1-min embedded video ~$0.017-0.021, 10-min HD ~$0.10, 10s 4K ~$0.013 (2048MB RAM default, us-east-1) PLUS S3 egress/storage/CloudWatch. LICENSE: free for individuals and companies <=3 employees; Company License required above that, ~$25/dev/mo, min $100/mo (4 seats) or $1000/yr; Enterprise min $500/mo. Cloud rendering at scale needs @remotion/licensing per-render webhook reporting. Ideal for deterministic kinetic typography, animated captions/karaoke subtitles, b-roll overlay, branded templates.

keyFindings.point: Captioning needs word-level timestamps; ElevenLabs Scribe v2, Deepgram, AssemblyAI lead
keyFindings.detail: For karaoke/animated captions you need word-level (ms-accurate) timestamps. ElevenLabs Scribe v2 (scribe_v2): 90+ langs, word timestamps, diarization, keyterm prompting (up to 1000 terms); scribe_v2_realtime ~150ms. Deepgram Nova-3 leads hosted accuracy (5.26% WER); Nova-2 real-time streaming sub-300ms. AssemblyAI leads transcript intelligence + ms-accurate word/sentence timestamps. Raw OpenAI Whisper API does NOT return word-level timestamps (needs whisper-timestamped). Deepgram ships OSS WebVTT/SRT converters for all three.

keyFindings.point: Video-generation models (b-roll) are best accessed through an aggregator, not direct
keyFindings.detail: Per-second pricing $0.05-$0.75/sec. Google Veo 3.1 Standard $0.75/sec (native 4K, best lip-sync, audio included) via Vertex AI/Gemini (region+tier gated). Kling 3.0 ~$0.09-0.14/sec (best cost/quality) self-serve via ModelsLab. Runway Gen-4.5 ~$1.50/10s clip, API on enterprise waitlist. OpenAI Sora 2 API is being DISCONTINUED 2026-09-24 (web/app already ended 2026-04-26) — do not build on it. fal.ai is the recommended single gateway: 600+ models incl. Kling, Veo 3.1, Wan 2.6, Seedance 1.5 Pro at $0.05-0.40/sec, no waitlists.

keyFindings.point: Captions rebranded to Mirage — now a foundation-model lab with bulk video + UGC-ad API
keyFindings.detail: Captions ($500M valuation, $100M+ raised) rebranded to Mirage (Sept 2025), pivoting to multimodal video foundation models for short-form (TikTok/Reels/Shorts). Mirage Studio generates full video from an audio file with AI avatar+background; API (help.mirage.app/api-reference/ai-ads) powers bulk generation, captioning, and programmatic UGC-style ads. Business plan $399/mo for 8,000 credits (50% off first month). Strong for automated ad/UGC creative; less a general avatar-API than HeyGen.

keyFindings.point: Argil is the cheapest turnkey 'avatar + voice + b-roll' assembly API for short-form
keyFindings.detail: Credit-based: Video 160 credits/min, Voice 20 credits/min (skippable by linking your own ElevenLabs voices), Avatar royalty 20 credits/video (v1 standard avatars only; custom avatars avoid it), B-roll AI image 10 credits, B-roll stock video 20 credits. Example: 30s video + 3 image b-rolls = 130 credits. API on Classic plan ($39/mo, 1 clone, 25 min) and up; Pro $149/mo (100 min, AI Influencer builder). Custom avatar from a 2-min selfie video (face+voice+mannerisms). Enterprise >60k credits/mo via enterprise@argil.ai.

keyFindings.point: Voice/TTS pricing and latency are now commodity-tier and predictable
keyFindings.detail: ElevenLabs: Flash v2.5 (eleven_flash_v2_5) ~75ms, 32 langs, 40k char limit, $0.05/1k chars (0.5 credit/char); Multilingual v2 (eleven_multilingual_v2) 29 langs, $0.10/1k chars; Eleven v3 (eleven_v3) 70+ langs, most expressive, 5k char limit. Turbo v2.5 deprecated → use Flash. OpenAI: gpt-4o-mini-tts $0.60/1M text-in + $12/1M audio-out (~$0.015/min) with steerable 'instructions' prosody; tts-1 $15/1M chars, tts-1-hd $30/1M. Cartesia Sonic-3 90ms TTFA / Sonic Turbo ~40ms (SSM-based, latency leader), $0.03/min, instant clone from 10s audio, Pro Voice Clone tier, 42-lang localization, Pro plan $4-5/mo. OpenAI gpt-realtime speech-to-speech ~$0.06/min in, ~$0.24/min out (~$0.30/min all-in, 80x cheaper input w/ caching); GPT-Realtime-Translate (70+ langs) and GPT-Realtime-Whisper also available.

itemsOrTools.name: HeyGen API (v3 + LiveAvatar SDK)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Turnkey avatar video API: Avatar IV/V digital-twin clones, Photo Avatar (talking head from one image), 40+ lang dubbing/translation, Lipsync, Starfish TTS, prompt-to-video Video Agent. Real-time via LiveAvatar (WebRTC, LiveKit input).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Primary turnkey avatar engine for the image+video studio. Best breadth + quality. Note: deprecating streaming-avatar SDK (sunset 2026-03-31) → migrate to LiveAvatar; v1/v2 REST supported to 2026-10-31.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: X-Api-Key REST, POST api.heygen.com/v3/videos. PAYG from $5 wallet: ~$1/min std 720/1080p, Avatar IV $4/min, Avatar V $0.05/sec, translation $2/min. API on Business/Enterprise tiers. No free API tier.

itemsOrTools.name: D-ID API (V4 Expressive Visual Agents)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Real-time, LLM-connected talking-head agents with sub-0.5s conversational turns, up to 4K, accurate lip-sync, 120+ languages; also batch talking-head generation from photos/audio.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best-in-class for the conversational/streaming avatar mode of the platform (e.g., interactive desktop/mobile assistant face). Strong real-time pipeline.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: From $5.90/mo; Build API tier from $18/mo (32 min streaming or 16 min video); API ~$5.90/min; Enterprise custom. REST + streaming SDK.

itemsOrTools.name: Synthesia API
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Enterprise-grade avatar video: 240+ avatars in 160+ languages, lip-sync, image-to-video, voice clone, interactive video. Credit = 1 min of video.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best for polished, enterprise/corporate-style avatar content (training, explainers) where realism and language breadth matter; less suited to high-volume cheap short-form.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: API access starts at Creator plan (~$64/mo annual, ~360 min/yr). Same credit balance as UI. Studio Avatars +$1,000/yr. Enterprise low-five-figures/yr.

itemsOrTools.name: Argil API
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Short-form (TikTok/Reels) avatar video assembly: AI clone avatars from 2-min selfie video, TTS, AI-image + stock-video b-roll, all in one call.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Cheapest end-to-end 'avatar+voice+b-roll' assembler for creator/influencer short-form; can BYO ElevenLabs voices to cut cost. Good default for the video studio's short-form lane.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Credits: video 160/min, voice 20/min, b-roll 10-20. API on Classic $39/mo (25 min) and up; Pro $149/mo (100 min). Enterprise enterprise@argil.ai.

itemsOrTools.name: Mirage (formerly Captions) API
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Multimodal video foundation models; Mirage Studio turns an audio file into a full AI-avatar video with background; API does bulk video generation, captioning, and programmatic UGC-style ads.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Strong for automated UGC/ad creative generation at scale; complements rather than replaces HeyGen for general avatars.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Business plan $399/mo for 8,000 credits (50% off first month). API at help.mirage.app/api-reference/ai-ads.

itemsOrTools.name: Tavus CVI
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Conversational Video Interface: real-time vision+speech avatars, BYO-LLM and BYO-TTS, 100+ replicas, replica creation from short video, memory ('PALs'), white-label, SOC2/HIPAA.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Top option if the platform's interactive assistant needs a hosted, batteries-included conversational-video pattern with compliance.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free 25 live min; Starter $59/mo (~100 min, 3 concurrent); Growth $299/mo (500+ min); Enterprise custom SLA.

itemsOrTools.name: Anam
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: API-first real-time interactive avatars; ~180ms model latency, sub-900ms turn-taking; transparent per-minute pricing.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best low-latency, developer-first real-time avatar if realism + API control + EU data residency are priorities; cleaner than Tavus for pure embedding.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Per-minute with volume tiers; SOC2 Type II + HIPAA; US+EU regions; zero data retention (enterprise). API/SDK.

itemsOrTools.name: ElevenLabs (Voice + Music + Scribe)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: TTS (Eleven v3, Flash v2.5 ~75ms, Multilingual v2), instant + professional voice cloning, Eleven Music v1/v2 (commercially cleared), Scribe v2 STT (word-level timestamps), text-to-sound-effects, dubbing.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Default voice layer AND the legally-safe music source (Music v2 commercial clearance dodges Suno/Udio lawsuits). Scribe v2 gives caption timestamps. Single vendor for voice+music+captions.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Flash $0.05/1k chars; Multilingual v2 / v3 $0.10/1k chars. Music commercial on all paid plans (film/TV = Enterprise). REST + streaming + websockets. Model IDs: eleven_v3, eleven_flash_v2_5, eleven_multilingual_v2, music_v1, scribe_v2.

itemsOrTools.name: Cartesia Sonic-3 / Sonic Turbo
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Ultra-low-latency TTS via State Space Models: 90ms TTFA (Sonic-3), ~40ms (Turbo); instant voice clone from 10s audio; 42-language localization; laughter/emotion.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best choice when latency dominates (real-time avatar turn-taking, voice agents) and for cheapest per-minute voice ($0.03/min). Lower lock-in via low cost.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: $0.03/min; 1 credit/char (1.5 for Pro clone). Pro plan $4-5/mo. REST + websocket streaming.

itemsOrTools.name: OpenAI Audio (gpt-4o-mini-tts + gpt-realtime/GPT-Realtime-2)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Steerable TTS (gpt-4o-mini-tts with 'instructions' prosody control), speech-to-speech realtime agents (gpt-realtime, GPT-Realtime-2 with GPT-5-class reasoning + tool use, 128K ctx), GPT-Realtime-Translate (70+ langs), GPT-Realtime-Whisper STT. WebRTC + WebSocket.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Use as the GPT 'review/secondary' voice + the speech-to-speech brain for the interactive assistant. Realtime API pairs naturally with an avatar renderer.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: gpt-4o-mini-tts ~$0.015/min ($0.60/1M text-in + $12/1M audio-out). Realtime ~$0.30/min all-in (audio $0.06/min in, $0.24/min out; cached input 80x cheaper). /v1/realtime/calls WebRTC endpoint.

itemsOrTools.name: Sync.so (lipsync-2 / lipsync-2-pro)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Managed frame-accurate lip-sync API (founders of Wav2Lip); replace/dub audio on any real video with accurate mouth motion; cost-estimation endpoint.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best managed lip-sync to bolt onto your own video gen or real footage without hosting GPUs. Also reachable via fal.ai/Replicate as a model.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Per-second billing (legacy ~$0.02-0.025/sec @25fps; ~$3/min via resellers). API on free tier. Plans Hobbyist $5 / Creator $19 / Growth $49 / Scale $249.

itemsOrTools.name: MuseTalk / LatentSync / Wav2Lip (OSS lip-sync)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Self-hostable lip-sync: MuseTalk (real-time 30fps+ latent inpainting, near-photoreal), ByteDance LatentSync 1.6 (512x512 diffusion, best visuals), Wav2Lip (most accurate sync, lightweight).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Removes per-minute fees and avatar-ToS exposure at scale; lets the platform own the lip-sync pipeline on its own GPUs. Trade-off: GPU ops + quality tuning.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free OSS (check individual licenses for commercial use; MuseTalk/LatentSync MIT-ish but verify). Self-host on GPU (V100/A10G+).

itemsOrTools.name: Remotion (React video) + Remotion Lambda
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Programmatic, deterministic video from React/HTML/CSS: kinetic typography, animated/karaoke captions, b-roll compositing, branded templates, data-driven video. Renders locally or distributed on AWS Lambda.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The deterministic ASSEMBLER layer that stitches avatar clip + voice + captions + b-roll into the final video, and the engine for HTML-driven motion graphics. Best fit for the platform's video studio output stage.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free for individuals + companies <=3 employees; Company License ~$25/dev/mo, min $100/mo (4 seats)/$1000/yr; Enterprise min $500/mo. Lambda AWS cost ~$0.02/min of video + S3/egress. Per-render webhook via @remotion/licensing for cloud scale.

itemsOrTools.name: fal.ai (video model gateway)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Single API/gateway to 600+ generative media models incl. Kling 3.0, Veo 3.1, Wan 2.6, Seedance 1.5 Pro, plus Sync lip-sync, at $0.05-0.40/sec; no waitlists.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Use as the b-roll / generative-video abstraction so the platform isn't locked to one model vendor and can route by cost/quality/region.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Pay-per-use per-second; API keys, queue + webhook async. No waitlist.

itemsOrTools.name: Deepgram / AssemblyAI (captions STT)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Speech-to-text with ms word-level timestamps for caption sync. Deepgram Nova-3 (5.26% WER) + Nova-2 real-time streaming sub-300ms; AssemblyAI ms word/sentence timestamps + transcript intelligence. OSS WebVTT/SRT converters.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Feeds word-level timing into Remotion for animated/karaoke captions. Alternative/backup to ElevenLabs Scribe v2.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Usage-based per-minute audio; generous free tiers; REST + streaming WebSocket. OSS caption formatters on GitHub.

itemsOrTools.name: Suno / Udio (music — caution)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Text-to-music generation (Suno v5.5, Udio). Highest creative quality but tangled in copyright litigation.
itemsOrTools.relevance: AVOID as core dependency: no official public API (third-party resellers only, $0.014-0.111/song), active UMG/Sony lawsuits, commercial rights only on tracks made while subscribed and not retroactive. Use ElevenLabs Music v2 instead for legal safety.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Suno Pro $10/mo (2,500 credits, commercial), Premier $30/mo. Udio paid $10-30/mo. No first-party dev API.

recommendations: Adopt a COMPOSABLE pipeline, not a single hosted vendor: [script/LLM: Claude Code primary, GPT review] -> [voice: ElevenLabs Flash v2.5 default, Cartesia Sonic-3 for low-latency, OpenAI gpt-4o-mini-tts as GPT-side option] -> [avatar clip: HeyGen API for quality / Argil API for cheap short-form / self-hosted MuseTalk for scale] -> [captions: ElevenLabs Scribe v2 or Deepgram word timestamps] -> [b-roll: fal.ai gateway to Kling/Veo/Wan] -> [final assembly + kinetic typography + animated captions: Remotion, rendered on Lambda]. This minimizes lock-in and lets you route per cost/quality/region.

Use Remotion as the single deterministic assembly + motion-graphics layer. It is the only mature React/HTML-driven engine and gives reproducible, data-driven output (captions, lower-thirds, b-roll timing) that pure avatar APIs cannot. Budget the Company License (min $100/mo / $1000/yr above 3 employees) and the per-render @remotion/licensing webhook for cloud rendering — factor this into unit economics early.

For AI MUSIC, standardize on ElevenLabs Music v2 (commercially cleared, has a real API). Treat Suno/Udio as optional, user-pasted assets only — never a server-side dependency — because they lack official APIs and remain in active UMG/Sony litigation as of mid-2026.

Pick the avatar tier by use case: HeyGen API for premium/brand avatar clips; Argil API for high-volume cheap short-form (BYO ElevenLabs voice to cut credits); D-ID V4 or Anam/Tavus for the REAL-TIME interactive assistant face. Migrate any HeyGen streaming work to LiveAvatar now — the old streaming-avatar SDK sunsets 2026-03-31.

Build a first-class CONSENT + likeness-verification flow before letting users create custom avatars/voices. HeyGen/D-ID require verified consent and will suspend accounts for unauthorized likeness or celebrity cloning. For a multi-user marketplace platform, capture explicit consent records, watermark/disclose AI content, and gate voice cloning behind identity checks to avoid inheriting users' ban/legal liability.

For the interactive desktop+mobile assistant, pair OpenAI Realtime (gpt-realtime / GPT-Realtime-2, WebRTC) or Cartesia for the voice loop with a streaming avatar (D-ID V4 / Anam) — target audio round-trip <300ms and mouth response <1s. Use WebRTC (not WebSockets) on mobile clients for stable latency; this aligns with the P2P/mobile requirement.

Do NOT build on OpenAI Sora — its API is being discontinued 2026-09-24 (web/app already ended 2026-04-26). Route generative b-roll through fal.ai so you can swap models (Kling 3.0 best cost/quality, Veo 3.1 best lip-sync/4K) without code changes.

Model costs explicitly in the per-project scheduler: avatar (~$1-4/min HeyGen), voice (~$0.05/1k chars EL Flash or $0.03/min Cartesia), lip-sync (~$0.02-0.025/sec Sync.so), video b-roll ($0.05-0.40/sec via fal.ai), Remotion render (~$0.02/min Lambda). Self-hosting MuseTalk/LatentSync becomes worthwhile above roughly tens of thousands of avatar-minutes/month.
risks: Music licensing is the single biggest legal landmine: Suno and Udio have unresolved UMG/Sony litigation (and now an AFM union suit over the settlements). Tracks made on free tiers carry NO commercial rights, and neither offers an official API. Mitigate by defaulting to ElevenLabs Music v2 (licensed/cleared) and treating Suno/Udio output as user-supplied, not platform-generated.

Avatar consent & deepfake/ban risk: HeyGen and D-ID strictly prohibit cloning real people/celebrities without verified consent and use ML+human moderation to suspend/terminate and even report to authorities. A multi-user platform that lets users upload faces/voices inherits this risk and could have its API keys/account banned for users' misuse. Requires consent capture, identity verification, and content moderation.

Vendor lock-in on hosted avatars: HeyGen avatar/voice IDs, Tavus replicas, and D-ID agents are non-portable. Mitigate with an internal abstraction layer and by keeping self-hostable OSS lip-sync (MuseTalk/LatentSync) as a fallback path.

Fast-moving deprecations break builds: HeyGen streaming-avatar SDK sunsets 2026-03-31 (→ LiveAvatar); HeyGen v1/v2 REST end 2026-10-31; OpenAI Sora API discontinued 2026-09-24. Pin versions, monitor changelogs, and avoid building core flows on announced-sunset products.

Cost blow-ups at scale: per-second video gen ($0.50-$7.50 per 10s clip on premium models like Veo 3.1) and per-minute avatar APIs can dominate unit economics. Real-time conversational avatars (D-ID/Tavus at $3-6/min equivalent) are especially expensive. Enforce per-project budgets/quotas in the scheduler and prefer cheap tiers (Kling, EL Flash, Cartesia) by default.

Remotion licensing obligation: free only up to 3 employees; a real company must pay (min $100/mo, $500/mo Enterprise) and implement the @remotion/licensing per-render webhook for cloud rendering. Non-compliance is a license violation. Budget and implement this from day one.

Latency reliability for real-time avatars: end-to-end conversational pipelines run 1-3s (LLM + render + network); only sub-300ms audio / sub-1s mouth response feels natural. Mobile/P2P networks worsen this. Hard to guarantee SLA across third-party hosted avatar + voice + LLM hops; consider self-hosted lip-sync + local TTS for the lowest-latency tiers.

Data residency / privacy: uploading user faces and voices to US-hosted APIs (HeyGen, ElevenLabs, OpenAI) raises GDPR/biometric-consent issues. Anam/Tavus offer EU regions, SOC2/HIPAA, zero-retention — prefer these for regulated or EU users; otherwise self-host.

summary: As of mid-2026 the API image-generation market splits into four tiers: (1) closed flagship multimodal models that excel at editing/character-consistency and conversational iteration — Google's Nano Banana family (Gemini 3 Pro Image / 3.1 Flash Image) and OpenAI's GPT Image 2 / 1.5; (2) FLUX from Black Forest Labs (FLUX.2 pro/flex/dev/klein, plus FLUX.1 Kontext pro/max for editing), which uniquely ships open weights AND a clean first-party API and leads on creative quality and up-to-10-image multi-reference; (3) specialists — Ideogram 3 (best in-image text), Recraft V3 (only true SVG/vector + brand styles), and ByteDance Seedream 4.5 (cinematic quality, strong character consistency, very cheap); (4) self-host/OSS via ComfyUI + FLUX.1-dev/SDXL/SD3.5 on RunPod/fal serverless. Pricing is now $0.005–0.24/image depending on model and resolution, with batch APIs cutting 50%. Aggregators fal.ai (~985 endpoints) and Replicate provide one unified API across nearly all of these, which is the pragmatic integration path for a multi-model studio. Midjourney remains the notable holdout — no broad public API (enterprise-only, automation against ToS, ban risk) — so it should be excluded from programmatic wrapping. The space moves fast: Nano Banana 2/Pro, FLUX.2, GPT Image 2, and Seedream 4.5 all shipped within ~6 months, so abstract behind a provider-agnostic layer.

keyFindings.point: Google Nano Banana family leads conversational editing + character consistency
keyFindings.detail: gemini-3-pro-image (Nano Banana Pro) and gemini-3.1-flash-image (Nano Banana 2, launched ~Feb 26 2026) and gemini-2.5-flash-image. Pro supports up to 6 object + 5 character reference images; 3.1 Flash up to 10 objects + 4 characters.
