Multi-turn conversational editing, advanced text rendering, Google Search grounding, 'thinking' mode, 512/1K/2K/4K. All outputs carry SynthID watermark. Conversational editing cuts refinement iterations ~60-70% per third-party tests.

keyFindings.point: FLUX is the only frontier model that is BOTH open-weight and first-party-API
keyFindings.detail: FLUX.2 (released Nov 25 2025): variants pro/flex (API), dev (32B open weights, HuggingFace), klein (Apache-2.0, coming). Up to 10 reference images for character/product/style consistency, editing+inpainting+text-to-image unified in one checkpoint, up to 4MP edits, reworked typography/UI-mockup text rendering. Architecture pairs a Mistral-3 24B VLM with a Rectified Flow Transformer. FLUX.1 Kontext pro/max remain the targeted editing models (regional/inpaint edits, multi-turn, strong character consistency).

keyFindings.point: OpenAI GPT Image now a 4-model lineup with token-based billing
keyFindings.detail: GPT Image 2 (current flagship), GPT Image 1.5 (prior flagship, quality leader ~1264 Elo), GPT Image 1 (deprecating Oct 23 2026), GPT Image 1 Mini (cheapest). Token-priced (separate text+image tokens). 3 quality tiers (low/med/high) x 3 sizes (1024², 1024x1536, 1536x1024). Editing/inpainting via prompt-guided mask (soft guidance, not hard pixel boundary). 'High input fidelity' mode preserves faces/logos across first 5 input images. All outputs carry C2PA provenance metadata. Character consistency degrades as scene complexity grows.

keyFindings.point: Ideogram 3 owns in-image text rendering
keyFindings.detail: Ideogram 3.0 ~$0.06/image (Turbo $0.03, Quality $0.09); claims 90-95% text accuracy — best-in-class for posters, infographics, logos with legible copy. Separate API billing at developer.ideogram.ai via Stripe, no subscription required.

keyFindings.point: Recraft V3 is the only API producing true scalable SVG/vector + brand-style control
keyFindings.detail: $0.04/raster image, $0.08/vector (SVG); Recraft 20B cheaper at $0.022 raster / $0.044 vector. Unique for logos, icons, design systems and consistent brand styles — no other major API outputs editable vectors.

keyFindings.point: ByteDance Seedream 4.5 is the cheap quality/consistency dark horse
keyFindings.detail: ~$0.03-0.035/image via fal; unified text-to-image + editing, cinematic lighting, strong character consistency (swap background/outfit while preserving face), multi-image generation. Caveat: Chinese-origin model — evaluate data-residency/compliance for enterprise.

keyFindings.point: Midjourney has NO broad public API — ban risk for automation
keyFindings.detail: As of 2026 Midjourney offers no official public REST API/SDK/webhooks; API keys restricted to Enterprise dashboard by application. Subscription-only ($10/$30/$60/$120/mo, Discord+web). Third-party wrappers (PiAPI, ImagineAPI, APIFRAME) violate ToS and risk account bans. Commercial-use license exists for paid subs but >$1M/yr revenue requires Pro/Mega.

keyFindings.point: Aggregators (fal.ai, Replicate) are the pragmatic multi-model integration layer
keyFindings.detail: fal.ai exposes ~985 endpoints (406 image) under one API: FLUX, SDXL, Nano Banana, Seedream, Qwen, etc.; per-output pricing (e.g. $0.03/image Seedream, $0.03/MP FLUX.2 pro) plus optional GPU-second (H100 $1.89/h). Replicate bills by GPU-second; FLUX.2 pro = $0.015 + $0.015/input+output MP. Both give near-zero cold starts and one billing relationship vs. integrating 6+ vendors.

keyFindings.point: Self-host path is mature via ComfyUI on serverless GPUs
keyFindings.detail: ComfyUI (~114k GitHub stars by May 2026) runs FLUX.1-dev/schnell, SDXL, SD3.5 with LoRA/ControlNet/inpaint. Deploy as serverless API on RunPod (worker-comfyui: /run, /runsync, /health; returns base64 or S3 URL). VRAM: SDXL 8GB+, FLUX.1-dev fp8 12GB+, SD3.5-Large bf16 16GB+. Best for cost control, privacy, and custom fine-tunes/LoRAs at scale.

keyFindings.point: Batch APIs and tiering cut cost ~50%
keyFindings.detail: Google Batch API halves any model's price (Gemini 2.5 Flash Image $0.039 → $0.0195/image). Resolution drives Gemini cost: 3.1 Flash 0.5K $0.045 / 1K $0.067 / 2K $0.101 / 4K $0.151; Pro 1-2K $0.134 / 4K $0.24. Imagen 4 Fast/Standard/Ultra = $0.02/$0.04/$0.06. Use async/batch for non-interactive studio jobs.

itemsOrTools.name: FLUX.2 [pro] / [flex] (Black Forest Labs)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Frontier text-to-image + unified editing/inpainting with up to 10 reference images for character/product/style consistency, 4MP edits, strong typography/UI-mockup text. [flex] exposes steps+guidance control. ~1265 Elo, best creative quality tier.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Primary default engine for the image studio: top quality, multi-reference character consistency, and a clean first-party API. [flex] gives the studio fine-grained control sliders; [dev] open weights let us self-host the exact same model family to avoid vendor lock-in.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: BFL API (credit-based, 1 credit=$0.01, pay-as-you-go) at docs.bfl.ai; also fal ($0.03/MP pro, $0.06/MP flex) and Replicate ($0.015 + $0.015/in+out MP pro). [dev] 32B open weights on HuggingFace.

itemsOrTools.name: FLUX.1 Kontext [pro] / [max]
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Dedicated instruction-based image editing: regional/local edits and inpainting that preserve unmodified regions, multi-turn iterative editing, strong character consistency across iterations, full-scene transforms.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The studio's 'edit existing image' and inpainting backbone. Pairs with FLUX.2 for generate→edit loops; consistent identity across turns is essential for the avatar/character features.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: $0.04/MP (Kontext pro) via Together AI / fal / Replicate; max tier higher. API + open dev weights.

itemsOrTools.name: Google Nano Banana — Gemini 3 Pro Image / 3.1 Flash Image / 2.5 Flash Image
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Native multimodal image gen+edit inside Gemini: conversational multi-turn editing, multi-image fusion, up to 6 object+5 character refs (Pro), Search grounding, 'thinking' mode, advanced text, 512–4K output. SynthID watermark on all outputs.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best-in-class for the chat-driven editing UX (the platform already does WhatsApp/Telegram chat) — users iterate in natural language. Flash tier is cheap enough for high-volume; Pro for hero assets. Same Gemini key the agent likely already uses.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Gemini API (ai.google.dev). Pro ~$0.134/img (1-2K), $0.24 (4K); 3.1 Flash $0.045–0.151 by res; 2.5 Flash $0.039 ($0.0195 batch). Batch API -50%. No free tier on these image models (some 500/day free reported on 2.5 Flash).

itemsOrTools.name: OpenAI GPT Image 2 / 1.5 / 1 / 1 Mini
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Multimodal generate + edit + inpaint (prompt-guided mask) + multi-image combine; high-input-fidelity mode for faces/logos; 3 quality x 3 sizes. C2PA provenance metadata on outputs.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Natural fit since GPT is already our 'review model' — single OpenAI account covers review + image gen. Strong prompt adherence; Mini ($0.005/img) is ideal for cheap thumbnails/drafts in the scheduler.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OpenAI Images API (token-priced): Mini ~$0.005–0.011, GPT Image 1 low/med/high ≈$0.02/$0.07/$0.19, GPT Image 2 $0.006–0.211. GPT Image 1 deprecates Oct 23 2026.

itemsOrTools.name: Ideogram 3.0
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Specialist in legible in-image text/typography (claims 90-95% accuracy) — posters, infographics, ad creatives, logos with copy. Turbo/Default/Quality tiers.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Route any 'image must contain specific text' job here from the studio's model-router; FLUX/Gemini are good but Ideogram is the safest for dense/accurate text.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Ideogram API at developer.ideogram.ai (Stripe, no sub). ~$0.06/img (Turbo $0.03, Quality $0.09). Also on fal/Together.

itemsOrTools.name: Recraft V3
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Only major API outputting true scalable SVG/vector images plus consistent brand styles and raster — logos, icons, design systems.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Unique capability for the studio's 'design/brand asset' lane; nothing else gives editable vectors. Use for logo/icon generation features.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Recraft API: $0.04 raster / $0.08 vector (V3); 20B cheaper. Also fal/Replicate/Segmind.

itemsOrTools.name: ByteDance Seedream 4.5
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Unified text-to-image + editing with cinematic lighting, strong character consistency (preserve face across background/outfit changes), multi-image generation, ~30+ styles.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Cheapest high-quality option for character-consistent multi-image storyboards/product shoots; good cost-saving router target. Flag for compliance (Chinese model) before enterprise/regulated use.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: ~$0.03–0.035/image via fal / WaveSpeed / Replicate.

itemsOrTools.name: Google Imagen 4 (Fast/Standard/Ultra)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Pure text-to-image at fixed per-image price, no token math; Fast is cheapest high-quality T2I on market.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Predictable-cost workhorse for bulk text-to-image where editing isn't needed; simplest pricing for scheduler/batch jobs.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Gemini API: Fast $0.02, Standard $0.04, Ultra $0.06 per image. imagen-4.0-fast/generate/ultra-generate-001.

itemsOrTools.name: fal.ai (aggregator)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Unified API over ~985 endpoints (406 image): FLUX, SDXL, Nano Banana, Seedream, Ideogram, Recraft, Qwen, plus video/audio/3D. Optimized runtime (up to 10x), near-zero cold starts, per-output OR GPU-second pricing.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Strongest single integration to wrap most models behind one SDK/billing for the studio — cuts engineering vs. 6+ direct vendors. Also covers video studio needs (Kling/Wan/Veo) in the same API.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: fal.ai API/SDK, pay-as-you-go. e.g. $0.03/img Seedream, $0.03/MP FLUX.2 pro; GPU H100 $1.89/h, A100 $0.99/h. ~$20 free credits.

itemsOrTools.name: Replicate (aggregator)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Pay-per-GPU-second hosting for open + commercial models (FLUX.2, SDXL, SD3.5, Recraft, Ideogram). Custom model deploy + LoRA hosting.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Backup/secondary aggregator and the place to host custom fine-tunes/LoRAs; predictable for low volume, good for our self-host-adjacent custom models.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Replicate API; GPU-second billing. FLUX.2 pro $0.015 + $0.015/in+out MP.

itemsOrTools.name: ComfyUI + FLUX.1-dev/SDXL/SD3.5 (self-host)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Node-based pipeline (~114k stars) running open diffusion models locally with LoRA, ControlNet, inpainting, img2img; deployable as serverless API (RunPod worker-comfyui: /run, /runsync, /health).
itemsOrTools.relevance: The escape hatch for cost control, privacy/data-residency, custom LoRAs and unlimited generations at scale — important for the P2P/mobile-self-host and 'no per-image vendor fee' modes. Higher ops burden.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS (free); pay only GPU compute on RunPod/AWS. VRAM: SDXL 8GB+, FLUX.1-dev fp8 12GB+, SD3.5-Large bf16 16GB+.

itemsOrTools.name: Midjourney v7/v8
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Top-tier aesthetic image generation via Discord/web subscription; v8 in 2026.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best raw aesthetics but NOT programmatically wrappable safely — exclude from the studio's automated pipeline. At most, document as a manual/external option.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: No public API (enterprise-only by application). Subs $10/$30/$60/$120/mo. Third-party wrappers violate ToS and risk bans.

recommendations: Build a provider-agnostic image abstraction layer (one internal interface: generate, edit/inpaint, multi-reference, upscale) and route per-job to the best model. Do NOT hard-code a single vendor — the leaderboard changed 4x in 6 months.

Default model matrix: FLUX.2 [pro] for hero text-to-image and multi-reference consistency; FLUX.1 Kontext for instruction edits/inpainting; Gemini Nano Banana (Flash) for chat-driven conversational editing (fits the WhatsApp/Telegram surface); GPT Image 1 Mini for cheap drafts/thumbnails in the scheduler; Ideogram 3 when the image must contain accurate text; Recraft V3 for logos/icons/SVG; Seedream 4.5 as the cheap character-consistent option.

Integrate primarily through fal.ai as the aggregator (covers FLUX, Nano Banana, Seedream, Ideogram, Recraft AND the video studio's Kling/Wan/Veo) to minimize vendor count, with direct OpenAI (GPT Image, already our review model) and direct Gemini (already our agent key) as first-party fallbacks. Keep Replicate as secondary/LoRA-hosting.

Ship a self-host lane: ComfyUI + FLUX.1-dev/SDXL on RunPod serverless for cost-sensitive, private, or high-volume users and for the P2P/mobile-self-host story. Expose it behind the same internal interface so users can flip between 'cloud' and 'local/BYO-GPU' without UI change.

Use batch/async APIs (Google Batch -50%, OpenAI/fal async) for all scheduler-driven, non-interactive generations to roughly halve cost; reserve premium tiers (Nano Banana Pro, GPT Image High, FLUX.2 flex) for interactive hero requests only.
Exclude Midjourney from any automated pipeline. If users demand it, treat it as a manual external link, never a wrapped API — automation breaches ToS and risks account bans.

Surface and persist provenance: outputs carry C2PA (OpenAI) and SynthID (Google) — store these, expose 'AI-generated' labeling, and don't strip metadata; this is becoming a compliance/marketplace-trust requirement.

Implement per-project cost metering and a model-cost estimator in the studio UI (prices range 40x from $0.005 to $0.24/image); let the per-project scheduler enforce budget caps and auto-downgrade to cheaper models.
risks: Vendor lock-in / rapid churn: GPT Image 2, FLUX.2, Nano Banana 2/Pro, Seedream 4.5 all shipped within ~6 months and GPT Image 1 deprecates Oct 23 2026 — any single-vendor or single-model-ID hard-coding will rot fast. Mitigate with the abstraction layer and version pinning + migration tests.

Midjourney ban risk: no official API; third-party wrappers (PiAPI/ImagineAPI/APIFRAME) violate ToS and get accounts banned. Wrapping them would put user accounts and our platform at legal/operational risk.

Cost blow-ups: token-billed models (GPT Image, Gemini) make per-image cost non-obvious; high-quality/4K can hit $0.19–0.24/image. Without budget caps and batch routing, the studio's costs scale unpredictably with usage.

Content policy / refusal + provider moderation: each API has its own (differing) safety filters and may refuse prompts; the studio needs graceful fallback + clear user messaging, and must handle that the same prompt passes on one model and is blocked on another.

Provenance & legal: AI-image copyright is contested (e.g. Midjourney/Disney litigation referenced in 2026; human-authorship requirements mean generated portions may be unprotectable). C2PA/SynthID watermarks are now expected; stripping them or mislabeling could create liability.

Data residency / compliance for non-US models: Seedream/Hunyuan are Chinese-origin — uploaded reference images (faces, brand assets) leaving to those endpoints may violate enterprise/GDPR policies. Gate behind a compliance flag.
Reliability / latency: aggregators add a dependency layer; cold starts, rate limits, and provider outages cascade. Need multi-provider failover and the self-host lane as a hard fallback.

Reference-image privacy: character-consistency features require users to upload faces/IDs to third-party APIs — a privacy and consent surface that must be governed (especially on the mobile + WhatsApp surfaces).

summary: As of mid-2026, programmatic auto-publishing of finished short-form video to all major platforms is technically possible but gated by heavy per-platform review/audit walls, divergent rate limits, and rising costs — making a unified aggregator (Ayrshare, Blotato, Postiz, upload-post) the pragmatic path versus integrating eight raw APIs directly. The hardest gates are TikTok (mandatory audit; unaudited apps can only post privately/SELF_ONLY; 2-6 week review; ~15-25 posts/creator/24h), Meta (Instagram Reels + Facebook Reels + Threads all require App Review + Business Verification; IG capped at 100 API posts/24h, Threads 250/24h), LinkedIn (Community Management API requires Partner Program approval, weeks-to-months, screencast demo for Standard tier), and X (free tier killed Feb 2026; now pay-per-use ~$0.01-0.015/post, no new Basic/Pro signups). YouTube Shorts upload is just videos.insert (1600 quota units; default 10,000/day = ~6 uploads/day until you pass a free but slow compliance audit). Trend/virality intelligence is fragmenting: TikTok closed its open trends API (Creative Center is browser-only; Research API is academic-gated), pushing teams toward scraper APIs (Apify) or purpose-built tools (HookMafia, VidIQ, Metricool trending-audio). For our universal AI-agent platform, the recommended architecture is a thin internal publishing-abstraction layer that defaults to an aggregator (Ayrshare or self-hosted Postiz/AGPL) for breadth, with direct YouTube + Meta integrations where economics justify it, plus a separate trend-research module combining scraper APIs and LLM hook/title generation. Everything here is changing fast — X pricing, TikTok scopes, and Meta API versions all shifted in the last 6 months.

keyFindings.point: TikTok Content Posting API requires an audit; unaudited apps can ONLY post privately
keyFindings.detail: All content from unaudited clients is forced to private (SELF_ONLY) viewing mode regardless of Direct Post vs Draft. To publish public content you must pass a manual audit (reported 2-6 weeks) verifying ToS compliance. Two modes: Direct Post (publishes immediately/scheduled) and Creator Draft (lands in user's TikTok inbox for manual approval). Required scope: video.publish (plus video.upload for draft flow). Each user access_token limited to ~6 requests/minute; posts capped at ~15-25 per creator per 24h window, shared across all API clients touching that creator. Access tokens expire in 24h (refresh token lasts 365 days). Audit also negotiates a per-client 24h active-creator cap.

keyFindings.point: Instagram, Facebook, and Threads each need separate Meta App Review + Business Verification
keyFindings.detail: Instagram Reels: two-step container flow (POST /<IG_ID>/media with media_type=REELS, then POST /<IG_ID>/media_publish); limited to 100 API-published posts per rolling 24h (carousels = 1); permission instagram_business_content_publish (IG Login) or instagram_content_publish (FB Login); check usage via GET /<IG_ID>/content_publishing_limit. Facebook Reels: separate 3-step flow via /{page-id}/video_reels on rupload.facebook.com; needs pages_manage_posts + pages_show_list + pages_read_engagement + Page token + App Review + Business Verification; Pages API rate limit = 4,800 x engaged-users calls/24h; video limits 1GB/20min single-request, 1.5GB/45min resumable. Threads: 250 posts + 1,000 replies + 100 deletions per user per rolling 24h (replies don't count toward the 250).

keyFindings.point: YouTube Shorts upload is videos.insert at 1600 quota units; default cap is ~6 uploads/day
keyFindings.detail: videos.insert costs 1600 units, the highest of any Data API v3 method. Default project quota is 10,000 units/day (resets midnight PT), so ~6 uploads/day before exhaustion. Shorts are not a separate endpoint — upload as normal video <=3 min (vertical, #Shorts in title/description optional). Higher quota is FREE but requires passing the Audit and Quota Extension Form (compliance audit, not payment); reported wait times range weeks to ~5 months and approval is not guaranteed. Note: docs reportedly being updated to drop upload cost from ~1600 to ~100 units, but most 2025-2026 sources still cite 1600 — verify live before building capacity assumptions.

keyFindings.point: X (Twitter) killed the free tier Feb 6 2026 and moved new devs to pay-per-use
keyFindings.detail: As of Feb 2026, new developers default to pay-per-use: ~$0.01 per post created, ~$0.005 per read (read cap 2M/month). An April 2026 adjustment raised standard writes to ~$0.015/post and posts containing a URL to ~$0.20/post (a major cost trap for link-in-post publishing). Legacy Basic ($200/mo, 50,000 writes) and Pro ($5,000/mo) remain only for existing subscribers; no new Basic/Pro signups. Migrated free-tier users got a one-time $10 credit. Video posting uses v2 chunked media upload (INIT/APPEND/FINALIZE); videos >10 min require a verified/premium account. This is the most volatile pricing of any platform here.

keyFindings.point: LinkedIn requires Partner Program approval; no casual API access
keyFindings.detail: All publishing goes through the Community Management API, available only to registered legal organizations for commercial use. Application requires verified business email, legal name, registered address, website, privacy policy; LinkedIn manually reviews (weeks to months). Tiers: Development (limited volume on approval) and Standard (full access, requires a screencast video demonstrating each declared use case). Video flow: upload via /rest/videos (chunked, ETag tracking) then reference the returned URN in a Posts API create call. Supports text, image, video w/ thumbnails+captions, carousels, polls.

keyFindings.point: Pinterest API v5 is free but Trial-access is heavily throttled
keyFindings.detail: No published price card, no per-call meter, no subscription. Every approved dev gets Trial access after first review: ~1,000 requests/day per app across endpoints (ads_write/org_write drop to 300/day). Standard access (requires upgrade review) lifts to ~100 requests/second/user/app. Video Pins created via media upload endpoint then pins/create referencing the media_id. Good low-cost surface but Trial throttle makes high-volume scheduling require Standard upgrade.

keyFindings.point: TikTok closed its open trends API — trend research now requires scrapers or 3rd-party tools
keyFindings.detail: In 2026 TikTok Creative Center (trending songs/hashtags/keywords) is browser-only and advertiser-oriented, NOT an automatable commercial API. The Research API is gated behind academic eligibility. TikTok added Search API, Discovery API, and Hashtag Analytics API (audience breakdowns, country-of-origin, associated sound trends, growth velocity) but access is gated/limited for commercial product use. Teams needing programmatic trend data turn to scraper APIs (Apify TikTok Creative Center / Trends actors, pay-per-event) — which carry ToS/ban risk — or to creator tools like HookMafia (viral hooks + real trending-sound recs + trend intelligence) and Metricool (trending-audio scheduling for IG Reels).

keyFindings.point: Aggregator APIs are the realistic path to 'post once, publish everywhere'
keyFindings.detail: Ayrshare: Premium $149/mo (1 profile, unlimited posts/API calls), Business $599/mo (up to 30 profiles, +$2.49-8.99/extra profile), webhooks, MCP-friendly. upload-post: free 10 uploads/mo, $24/mo for 5 accounts unlimited posts, $50/mo for 25, $147/mo for 75; one API to TikTok/IG/YouTube/LinkedIn/FB/X/Threads/Pinterest/Reddit/Bluesky; Python+JS SDKs. Blotato: $29/mo Starter (1,250 AI credits)/$97 Creator/$499 Agency; one REST API + MCP server to 9+ platforms, plus faceless AI video (a 30s video ~52 credits). Postiz: AGPL-3.0 open-source, self-hostable on single Docker+Postgres (NestJS/Next.js/Prisma/Temporal), 20+ networks, ships its own MCP server + public API; hosted from $29/mo; ~30k GitHub stars, v2.21.7 (Apr 2026). These absorb the per-platform audit/token/refresh burden for you.

itemsOrTools.name: TikTok Content Posting API (Direct Post + Draft)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Official API to upload/publish video and photo posts to a creator's TikTok. Direct Post publishes immediately or scheduled; Creator Draft sends to the user's inbox for manual approval. Returns X-RateLimit headers; supports MP4/H.264.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Core target for our auto-publish pipeline, but the audit wall (unaudited = private-only) means our app must either pass TikTok's audit per client app or proxy through an already-audited aggregator. Draft mode is a safer launch path (human approves in-app) and avoids public-visibility audit dependency.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free API; requires app registration + Content Posting API product + manual audit (2-6 wks) for public posts. Scopes: video.publish, video.upload. ~6 req/min/token, ~15-25 posts/creator/24h, token expires 24h.

itemsOrTools.name: YouTube Data API v3 (videos.insert)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Uploads finished videos (incl. Shorts as <=3min vertical videos) with title, description, tags, privacy, scheduling, and thumbnail (thumbnails.set, +50 units). Resumable upload supported.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Cleanest of the big platforms for us — no separate Shorts endpoint, no audit needed to start. The 1600-unit cost vs 10k/day default cap is the binding constraint; we must batch quota planning and apply early for the free quota-extension audit to scale beyond ~6 uploads/day/project.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free; OAuth 2.0. Default 10,000 units/day. videos.insert=1600 units. Higher quota via free Audit & Quota Extension Form (slow, weeks-months, not guaranteed).

itemsOrTools.name: Instagram Graph API — Content Publishing (Reels)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Publishes Reels/feed/stories to IG Business/Creator accounts via two-step container then media_publish flow. content_publishing_limit endpoint reports remaining 24h budget.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Required for Reels distribution. Demands Meta App Review + Business Verification and a connected FB Page; the 100-post/24h cap is generous enough for our per-project scheduler. Reels media must be hosted at a public URL we serve.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free; permission instagram_business_content_publish or instagram_content_publish. 100 API posts/24h/account; ~200 calls/hour/account.

itemsOrTools.name: Facebook Pages Video/Reels API
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Publishes Reels (/{page-id}/video_reels, 3-step on rupload.facebook.com) and standard videos to FB Pages. Video limits 1GB/20min single, 1.5GB/45min resumable.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Lets us cross-post the same vertical video to Facebook with minimal extra work once Meta review is passed (shared verification with IG). Lower marginal effort given IG integration already done.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free; needs pages_manage_posts + pages_show_list + pages_read_engagement, App Review, Business Verification, Page token. Rate 4,800 x engaged-users/24h.

itemsOrTools.name: Threads API
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Publishes text/image/video posts and replies to Threads via Meta Graph infra. Generous publishing budget per user.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Easy incremental add under the same Meta umbrella; useful for text hooks + clip teasers driving traffic. 250 posts/24h is ample for our scheduler.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free; Meta app + Threads permissions. 250 posts + 1,000 replies + 100 deletions per user/24h (replies excluded from the 250).

itemsOrTools.name: X (Twitter) API v2 (pay-per-use)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Creates posts with text/media via POST /2/tweets; video via v2 chunked media upload (INIT/APPEND/FINALIZE). Pay-per-use metering as of Feb 2026.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Highest cost-volatility risk for us. The ~$0.20/post-with-URL charge is a real trap if our posts include links — design to put links in replies or bios. Budget carefully; consider routing X through an aggregator that holds legacy Pro access.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: No free tier for new devs. ~$0.01-0.015/post (URL posts ~$0.20), ~$0.005/read (2M/mo cap). Legacy Basic $200/mo & Pro $5,000/mo (existing subscribers only). Video >10min needs verified account.

itemsOrTools.name: LinkedIn Community Management API
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Publishes text/image/video/carousel/poll posts on behalf of organizations; video via /rest/videos chunked upload then URN reference in Posts API.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Needed for B2B/professional reach but the highest approval friction. For our app, default to an aggregator for LinkedIn rather than pursuing Partner Program approval ourselves unless LinkedIn is a primary channel.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free API but requires LinkedIn Partner Program approval (legal org, verified business email, privacy policy; weeks-months). Dev tier limited; Standard tier needs screencast demo of each use case.

itemsOrTools.name: Pinterest API v5 (Create Pin / Media)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Creates image and video Pins via media upload then pins/create. Also analytics endpoints.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Low-cost surface for evergreen vertical content and traffic. Trial throttle (~1,000 req/day) is fine for low-volume; needs Standard upgrade for high-volume scheduling.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free; OAuth. Trial ~1,000 req/day/app (ads_write/org_write 300/day); Standard ~100 req/sec/user/app after upgrade review.

itemsOrTools.name: Ayrshare API
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Unified social posting/scheduling/analytics API + MCP across all major networks; handles OAuth, tokens, refresh, and platform quirks. Webhooks on Business+.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Strongest turnkey way for us to ship 'post everywhere' fast without owning 8 audits. Per-profile pricing aligns with our per-project model. Good default for breadth; downside is per-profile cost at scale and vendor lock-in.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free (20 posts/mo, images). Premium $149/mo (1 profile, unlimited). Business $599/mo (30 profiles, +$2.49-8.99/extra). Enterprise custom.

itemsOrTools.name: Postiz (gitroomhq/postiz-app)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Open-source (AGPL-3.0) agentic social scheduler for 20+ networks; ships its own MCP server + public REST API, AI ideation, AI image/video (agent-media.ai). Self-hostable on Docker+Postgres; stack NestJS/Next.js/Prisma/Temporal.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Best fit for our self-hosted, no-vendor-lock-in, agent-driven model — we can run it inside our platform, drive it via its MCP server from Claude Code, and avoid per-profile SaaS fees. AGPL-3.0 has copyleft implications for distribution (mind for a commercial desktop/mobile product).
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free self-hosted (AGPL-3.0, ~30k GitHub stars, v2.21.7 Apr 2026). Hosted SaaS from $29/mo, 7-day trial.

itemsOrTools.name: upload-post.com
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Single API to upload once and publish/schedule to TikTok, IG, YouTube, LinkedIn, FB, X, Threads, Pinterest, Reddit, Bluesky. Python + JS SDKs.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Cheapest unified option for early stage — account-based not per-profile pricing scales better than Ayrshare for many small creators (our likely user base). Good candidate for MVP publishing layer.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free 10 uploads/mo. $24/mo (5 accounts), $50/mo (25), $147/mo (75), $438/mo (225) — unlimited posts.

itemsOrTools.name: Blotato
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: One REST API + MCP server to publish across 9+ platforms; plus faceless AI video generation, AI images, ElevenLabs voiceovers (credit-metered). Integrates with n8n/Make/Claude.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Combines our image+video studio need with the publishing layer in one vendor and exposes an MCP server (fits Claude Code engine). Credit model couples video generation cost to posting — useful for an integrated 'studio + publish' flow but watch credit burn.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Starter $29/mo (1,250 credits), Creator $97/mo (5,000), Agency $499/mo (28,000). ~52 credits per 30s faceless video. API + MCP optional on all plans.

itemsOrTools.name: Metricool
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Scheduler + analytics with best-time-to-post recommendations and trending-audio scheduling for IG Reels. API/Looker Studio access on Advanced.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Covers our virality-timing and trending-audio needs (legally, via official IG audio) better than scraping. Useful as a data/timing signal source feeding our scheduler, not as the primary publisher.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Starter $22/mo ($18 annual, 5 brands); Advanced $54/mo ($45 annual, 15 brands) with API + Looker Studio.

itemsOrTools.name: Buffer
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Per-channel scheduler with free-forever plan, AI assistant, basic analytics, community inbox.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Reference competitor and a cheap entry option; per-channel pricing gets expensive vs Metricool/upload-post at scale. Not API-first enough to be our backbone, but informative on UX/best-time features.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free (3 channels, 10 scheduled/channel). Paid from $5-6/channel/mo.

itemsOrTools.name: HookMafia
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: AI hook + full-script generator trained on viral TikTok patterns; real trending-sound recommendations, trend prediction, viral-video analysis, Creator Intelligence connected to a TikTok account.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Directly serves our virality-optimization module (hooks, scripts, trending audio) without us building a trend-data pipeline from scratch. Candidate API/integration partner or inspiration for our own GPT-review-model-assisted hook generator.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: SaaS (web). Pricing on site; check for API availability before integrating.

itemsOrTools.name: Apify TikTok Trends / Creative Center actors
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Scraper actors that extract trending hashtags, songs, and time-series velocity from TikTok Creative Center / trends pages via pay-per-event API.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Fills the gap left by TikTok closing its open trends API. Lets our trend-research module pull programmatic signals — but carries ToS/scraping/ban risk and reliability risk if TikTok changes its DOM/anti-bot.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Pay-per-event via Apify platform; usage-metered.

recommendations: Adopt a two-tier publishing architecture: an internal PublishingProvider abstraction with (a) an aggregator backend as default (start with upload-post.com for low-cost account-based pricing, or self-hosted Postiz for zero-vendor-lock-in + MCP integration with Claude Code), and (b) direct native integrations only for YouTube Data API and Meta (IG/FB/Threads) where we have volume and the audit ROI is clear. This avoids owning 8 separate platform audits at launch.

Default to self-hosted Postiz (AGPL-3.0, ships an MCP server) as the engine our Claude Code agent drives, because it eliminates per-profile SaaS cost, gives us source control, and fits the agent-driven model — but get legal review on AGPL-3.0 copyleft before shipping it inside a distributed commercial desktop/mobile app (consider keeping it as a network-served backend to limit copyleft reach).

For TikTok, launch with Draft/inbox mode (no public-visibility audit dependency, human approves in-app) and pursue the full Content Posting API audit in parallel; or route TikTok through an already-audited aggregator. Never assume unaudited = public — it is private-only.

Apply for the YouTube Audit & Quota Extension Form immediately (it's free but takes weeks-to-months) since the default 10k units/day = only ~6 uploads/day will throttle any multi-user product. Architect per-Google-Cloud-project quota sharding as a fallback while waiting.

Treat X as cost-volatile and link-hostile: put links in replies/bio not in the main post to avoid the ~$0.20/URL-post charge, meter X usage per project with hard budget caps, and consider proxying X through an aggregator holding legacy Pro access rather than going pay-per-use directly.

Build the trend/virality module on legal/official signals first — Metricool trending-audio + best-time-to-post, YouTube/IG official insights, and an LLM (GPT as review model per spec) for hook/title/thumbnail generation. Use scraper APIs (Apify) only as a clearly-isolated, optional, risk-flagged data source, not a core dependency, because TikTok closed its open trends API and scraping carries ban/ToS risk.

Sequence platform rollout by friction: Phase 1 YouTube + Pinterest + Threads + Bluesky (low gate); Phase 2 Instagram + Facebook Reels (one Meta App Review + Business Verification covers all three); Phase 3 TikTok (audit) and LinkedIn (Partner Program) — the two slowest approvals — or cover them via aggregator from day one.

Implement per-platform rate-limit + quota tracking in the scheduler (IG 100/24h via content_publishing_limit, Threads 250/24h, TikTok ~15-25/creator/24h shared, YouTube 1600 units/upload, Pinterest Trial 1000/day) so the per-project scheduler can pace posts and surface 'budget remaining' to users.
risks: ToS / ban risk: TikTok forces unaudited apps to private-only; scraping trend data (Apify, TikTok Creative Center) violates platform terms and risks IP blocks and account bans. Automated posting that looks like spam can get creator accounts flagged/suspended — implement human-in-the-loop and per-creator rate pacing.

Pricing volatility & cost: X eliminated its free tier in Feb 2026 and added ~$0.20/URL-post in April 2026; pay-per-use can produce surprise bills at scale. YouTube's stated upload cost (1600 vs rumored 100 units) is in flux. Aggregator per-profile fees (Ayrshare $2.49-8.99/extra profile) compound across many users.

Approval bottlenecks: Meta App Review + Business Verification, TikTok audit (2-6 wks), LinkedIn Partner Program (weeks-months), and YouTube quota audit (weeks-months) can each block launch on specific platforms. Approval is not guaranteed and can be revoked.

Vendor lock-in: Building on Ayrshare/Blotato/upload-post couples our publishing reliability and cost to a third party; if they lose a platform's access (common — platforms revoke aggregator access periodically) our users silently lose channels. Mitigate with the abstraction layer + at least one self-hosted fallback (Postiz).

Reliability / brittleness: Scraper-based trend data breaks whenever platforms change DOM/anti-bot; chunked video upload flows (X, FB Reels, LinkedIn) are error-prone (documented 'unknown processing error' / failed-to-load issues); token expiry (TikTok 24h access tokens) requires robust refresh handling or posts silently fail.

Licensing: Postiz is AGPL-3.0 — embedding/distributing it in a commercial desktop/mobile product can trigger copyleft obligations; mishandling this is a legal risk. Verify before bundling vs network-serving.

Rate-limit shared pools: TikTok's per-creator daily post cap is shared across ALL API clients touching that creator, so a creator using both our app and another tool can hit limits unexpectedly — surface clear errors and remaining-budget UI.

Fast-moving APIs: Meta Graph API versions deprecate ~yearly (currently v25.0 era), TikTok scopes/audit terms shift, and X pricing changed twice in 2026 — any hardcoded assumption (limits, costs, endpoints) needs a monitoring/alerting layer and quarterly re-verification.

summary: As of mid-2026, "enterprise-grade" for an AI developer/agent platform is a hard checklist: SAML/OIDC SSO + SCIM, RBAC, immutable queryable audit logs (exportable to SIEM), zero-data-retention + no-training contractual guarantees, regional data residency, BYOK/BYO-model and air-gapped deployment options, secrets isolation (Vault/Infisical-style agent vaults to survive prompt injection), per-user/per-project cost controls via an LLM gateway, and certifications (SOC 2 Type II baseline; ISO 27001/42001 and FedRAMP increasingly expected). Compliance pressure is rising fast: EU AI Act enforcement and Commission fining powers begin 2 Aug 2026, mandating audit trails and human checkpoints for high-risk agentic use. The business-model center of gravity has shifted from pure seat pricing to hybrid (base subscription floor + usage/outcome upside) because AI COGS compress SaaS gross margins from ~80-90% to ~50-60% (~$230K inference cost per $1M AI revenue). For our universal AI-agent desktop+mobile platform, Claude Code/Agent SDK is a strong primary engine (Opus 4.8 at $5/$25 per MTok, 1M context; but note the new 15 Jun 2026 separate Agent SDK credit pool changes economics), GPT-5.5 ($5/$30) is a credible independent review model, and the dominant risk surface is the WhatsApp channel (unofficial Baileys/Evolution libraries violate Meta ToS with critical ban risk, enforcement escalating Jan 2026). For the PRD itself, the strongest current playbook combines Amazon Working Backwards PR/FAQ (customer-first forcing function), Marty Cagan's 9-question opportunity assessment, explicit JTBD + personas, P0/P1/P2 phasing, and separated, measurable non-functional requirements with a North Star + supporting metrics. This space moves monthly (model IDs, pricing, Agent SDK billing, ToS enforcement) — treat all pricing/limits as point-in-time.

keyFindings.point: Enterprise readiness is a concrete checklist, not a tier label
keyFindings.detail: Platforms that pass 2026 IT security reviews treat SSO (SAML/OIDC), SCIM provisioning, RBAC, and immutable audit logging as core product features, not bolt-ons. Auditors now demand immutable, queryable records of every prompt, response, retrieval, and human override (not admin-panel screenshots). GitHub Copilot provides SAML SSO + SCIM + per-event audit logging; enterprise platforms expose full audit trails exportable for SIEM integration.

keyFindings.point: SOC 2 Type II is the baseline; ISO 27001/42001 and FedRAMP are differentiators
keyFindings.detail: SOC 2 Type II is the minimum enterprise customers expect. Tabnine is triple-certified (SOC 2 Type II, GDPR, ISO 27001) with a fully air-gapped tier and contractual zero-retention. First AI coding assistant achieved ISO/IEC 42001 (AI management system) + SOC 2 Type II. GitHub Copilot reached FedRAMP Moderate in April 2026. SOC 2 Trust Service Criteria for AI specifically require preventing cross-tenant info leakage, model versioning controls, and training-data protection.

keyFindings.point: EU AI Act enforcement and fines begin 2 Aug 2026 and cover AI agents
keyFindings.detail: AI Act becomes fully applicable 2 Aug 2026; Commission gains enforcement/fining powers that date (up to 3% global turnover or EUR 15M, whichever higher, for GPAI providers). GPAI model obligations already in force since 2 Aug 2025. AI agents are covered by existing AI-system/GPAI definitions; high-risk agentic deployments require audit trails and human checkpoints. High-risk system rules extend to 2 Aug 2028.

keyFindings.point: AI COGS structurally compress SaaS margins, forcing hybrid pricing
keyFindings.detail: AI features compress gross margins from ~80-90% to ~50-60%; ~$230K inference cost per $1M AI revenue. 92% of AI software companies now use hybrid pricing; pure pay-as-you-go is only ~15% vs hybrid ~46%. A power user running 1,000 queries/day can cost 100x a light user yet pay the same flat seat — seat-only pricing invites abuse. Outcome-based pricing (charge per completed job) is claimed to unlock 5-10x more revenue but is harder to meter.

keyFindings.point: WhatsApp via unofficial libraries is the platform's single biggest ToS/ban risk
keyFindings.detail: Baileys, WA-Automate, and Evolution API all run on the reverse-engineered WhatsApp Web protocol, violate Meta ToS, and carry high-to-critical ban risk (permanent number bans without warning, frequent on new/high-volume numbers). Meta enforcement escalating from ~15 Jan 2026, blocking third-party AI chatbots. Official Cloud API charges per-conversation (~EUR 0.03-0.15 by category/country; Utility rates cut ~45% late March 2026). Telegram Bot API by contrast is free, commercial-friendly, 30 msg/s/bot (paid broadcast up to 1000/s at 0.1 Stars/msg over 30/s).

keyFindings.point: Claude Agent SDK billing changes on 15 Jun 2026 materially affect unit economics
keyFindings.detail: From 15 Jun 2026, Agent SDK and `claude -p` usage on subscription plans draw from a SEPARATE monthly Agent SDK credit pool, distinct from interactive limits. Opus 4.8 (claude-opus-4-8) defaults effort=high on API and Claude Code. SDK gives headless one-shot CLI, parallel subagents (isolated context), hooks (PreToolUse/PostToolUse/Stop/SessionStart/etc.), persistent sessions, and first-class MCP client support — directly usable for our per-project scheduler, review subagents, and skills.

keyFindings.point: Secrets for agents need an agent-aware vault, not classic secret fetch
keyFindings.detail: Classic 'app fetches credential and uses it' breaks for agents: prompt injection can make an agent leak anything it can read. Infisical shipped Agent Vault, an open-source credential proxy purpose-built for agents (agent uses credential without seeing it). Vault offers dynamic, auto-rotated secrets + encryption-as-a-service (BSL license). Doppler organizes secrets by project/environment. All three (Vault, Infisical, Doppler) ship official MCP servers.

keyFindings.point: OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions are the emerging observability standard
keyFindings.detail: OTel GenAI conventions unify attributes for LLM calls, agent steps, vector queries, token usage (gen_ai.usage.input_tokens/output_tokens), cost, latency, quality — but most remain EXPERIMENTAL as of 2026, so the spec is not stabilized. Langfuse aims for OTel compliance; Helicone joined Mintlify (3 Mar 2026) and is in maintenance mode. AI Agent Application Conventions (per-agent task/action/memory tracing) are still experimental.

keyFindings.point: BYOK + air-gapped is now a shipped enterprise pattern in mainstream tools
keyFindings.detail: VS Code 1.122 (28 May 2026) removed the GitHub OAuth dependency for BYOK, enabling fully air-gapped operation routing to local inference (Ollama, vLLM, Foundry Local). Cursor Enterprise offers zero-retention + encryption + data sovereignty. GitHub Copilot data residency keeps code/prompts/responses in-region (EU, Australia, US, Japan). Note Copilot 'Zero Retention' applies only to completions/standard chat — CLI and agentic flows retain session data.

keyFindings.point: MCP + Agent Skills are the de-facto extensibility/marketplace substrate
keyFindings.detail: By March 2026 Anthropic reported 10,000+ active public MCP servers and 97M monthly SDK downloads; MCP (JSON-RPC 2.0) is supported by Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google DeepMind. Agent Skills are dynamically-discoverable folders of instructions/scripts/resources that complement MCP. This validates a dynamic skills marketplace, but note: no standardized monetization/registry-billing model exists yet — that is greenfield (and a risk).

keyFindings.point: Competitor pricing reveals the metering models we must match or beat
keyFindings.detail: Cursor Teams repriced June 2026: Standard $32/seat/mo (annual), Premium $96/seat/mo (5x usage); Enterprise adds pooled credits, SCIM, AI-code-tracking API + audit logs. Devin bills in Agentic Compute Units (1 ACU ~= 15 min autonomous work; Team $500/mo incl 250 ACUs at $2 each; entry cut to $20/mo). Claude Code ~$20/seat + API tokens; Team Premium $125/seat for Opus. Replit ~$25/seat + compute. The market norm is dollar-denominated metered agent actions, not fixed request counts.

keyFindings.point: The strongest PRD playbook is customer-first narrative + opportunity assessment + measurable NFRs
keyFindings.detail: Amazon Working Backwards uses a PR/FAQ (press release + FAQ) as a forcing function to define the customer experience before building (used for Kindle/AWS/Alexa; adopted by Stripe/Notion/Shopify/Airbnb). Marty Cagan's opportunity assessment asks 9 questions (problem, for whom, size, alternatives, why us, why now, GTM, how measure success, critical success factors). 2026 PRD best practice: start from the user/business problem (not UI), separate functional vs non-functional reqs with concrete thresholds (e.g., 'page load <2s for 95% of users'), categorize requirements P0/P1/P2, and define measurable success metrics + MVP/acceptance criteria.

keyFindings.point: LLM gateways provide the cost-control + multi-provider primitives we need
keyFindings.detail: LiteLLM offers virtual API keys with budgets at user/team/project level, per-key spend caps (returns 429 when exceeded), budget windows (24h/30d), per-agent and per-session tpm/rpm limits, per-session max_iterations and max_budget_per_session to stop runaway agentic loops, and provider failover. Portkey and Kong AI Gateway are alternatives. This is the recommended layer to sit between our app and Anthropic/OpenAI/image/video providers.

keyFindings.point: P2P/local-first mobile sync has converged on CRDTs (Yjs/Automerge) + libp2p
keyFindings.detail: By 2026 Yjs (speed) and Automerge (DX, Git-like history, JSON-like, Rust core compiles to WASM/FFI for iOS+Android) are the two dominant CRDT libraries. Pattern: each device holds its own DB, cloud acts as a relay not gatekeeper; CRDT updates stored as binary blobs in Postgres. libp2p provides modular P2P transport (TCP, QUIC, WebRTC, WebTransport). Relevant alternatives: Electric SQL, Iroh, Syncthing for E2E sync.

keyFindings.point: Image/video generation API costs are now low and well-differentiated
keyFindings.detail: Image: OpenAI GPT Image 1 Mini ~$0.005/img (cheapest), GPT Image 1.5 ~$0.04/std (quality leader, Elo ~1264), Google Imagen 4 Fast/Standard/Ultra $0.02/$0.04/$0.06, Flux 2 Pro ~$0.055 (Elo ~1265). Video: Google Veo 3.1 via paid Gemini API; ~$240 for 10 min of 1080p Veo 3.1 Standard — video is the cost driver to meter aggressively in our studio feature.

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Opus 4.8 (claude-opus-4-8) + Agent SDK
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Anthropic's most capable Opus-tier model: 1M-token context, 128k max output (300k via output-300k-2026-03-24 beta on Batch), adaptive thinking, effort=high default, $5/$25 per MTok. Agent SDK (Python/TS) provides the same harness as Claude Code: headless CLI, parallel subagents with isolated context, hooks, persistent sessions, MCP client.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Primary engine for our platform. Subagents map directly to per-project work isolation and parallel review; hooks enforce permission/audit gates; MCP client powers the skills/tool layer. 1M context fits large multi-file projects.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Claude API / AWS Bedrock / Vertex AI / Microsoft Foundry (200k ctx on Foundry). $5/$25 MTok; Batch 50% off; prompt caching ~90% off cached input. CRITICAL: from 15 Jun 2026, SDK/`claude -p` on subscription plans bill from a SEPARATE Agent SDK credit pool. Claude Code included in Pro/Max/Team Premium ($125/seat for Opus)/Enterprise.

itemsOrTools.name: Claude Fable 5 (claude-fable-5)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Anthropic's most capable widely-released model (GA 9 Jun 2026): 1M context, 128k output, always-on adaptive thinking, $10/$50 per MTok. Uses Opus-4.7-gen tokenizer (~30% more tokens for same text). Mythos 5 is invite-only via Project Glasswing.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Reserve tier for the hardest long-horizon agentic tasks where Opus 4.8 is insufficient; pricier so meter carefully. Tokenizer note matters for cost estimates.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Claude API / Bedrock / Vertex / Foundry. $10/$50 MTok.

itemsOrTools.name: OpenAI GPT-5.5 (review model)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: OpenAI flagship, $5/$30 per MTok, strong for security review, multi-file design, high-stakes correctness; GPT-5.2-Codex for coding. Smaller/cheaper tiers: GPT-5.4 $2.50/$15, GPT-5.4 Mini $0.75/$4.50, GPT Image 1 Mini for images.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Independent second-opinion / adversarial review model (different vendor reduces correlated blind spots vs Claude). Use for our 'GPT as review model' role and as multi-provider failover.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OpenAI API. $5/$30 MTok (GPT-5.5).

itemsOrTools.name: Telegram Bot API
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Free messaging bot platform: 30 msg/s/bot, 20 msg/min in groups, 1 msg/s per chat; paid broadcasts up to 1000/s at 0.1 Stars/msg over the free 30/s. MTProto (Telethon) gives user-level access bypassing some bot limits. Adaptive reputation-based limits being tested in 10.24 beta.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Lowest-risk, lowest-cost messaging channel for our platform — commercial bots explicitly allowed, no platform fees. Should be the PRIMARY chat channel; WhatsApp secondary.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free via @BotFather; Stars only for paid broadcast overflow.

itemsOrTools.name: WhatsApp Cloud API (official) vs Baileys/Evolution API (unofficial)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Official Meta Cloud API: per-conversation billing ~EUR 0.03-0.15 by category/country (Utility cut ~45% late Mar 2026). Unofficial (Baileys, WA-Automate, Evolution API): reverse-engineered WhatsApp Web, no per-msg fee but violates ToS.
itemsOrTools.relevance: WhatsApp is required by spec but is the top ban/ToS risk. Unofficial libs = critical permanent-ban risk, escalating enforcement from ~15 Jan 2026. Strongly prefer official Cloud API or a BSP (Twilio/Gupshup/360dialog) for any production/enterprise tier.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Official: Cloud API + BSP, per-conversation pricing. Unofficial: OSS (Baileys MIT, Evolution API) self-hosted — high legal/operational risk.

itemsOrTools.name: Google Veo 3.1 + Imagen 4 / OpenAI GPT Image 1.5 / Flux 2 Pro
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Image+video generation APIs. Imagen 4 Fast/Std/Ultra $0.02/$0.04/$0.06; GPT Image 1.5 ~$0.04 (quality leader Elo~1264), GPT Image 1 Mini ~$0.005; Flux 2 Pro ~$0.055 (Elo~1265). Veo 3.1 video on paid Gemini API (~$240 per 10 min 1080p Standard).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Backs the image+video studio feature. Multi-provider routing lets us optimize cost/quality per request. Video is the dominant cost driver — gate behind strict per-user/project budgets.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Gemini API (Imagen/Veo), OpenAI API (GPT Image), Black Forest Labs / fal / Replicate (Flux). Per-image/per-second metered.

itemsOrTools.name: MCP (Model Context Protocol) + Agent Skills
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Open JSON-RPC 2.0 standard connecting agents to tools/data; 10,000+ public servers, 97M monthly SDK downloads (Mar 2026), backed by Anthropic/OpenAI/Google. Agent Skills = dynamically-loaded folders of instructions/scripts/resources.
itemsOrTools.relevance: The substrate for our dynamic skills marketplace and tool integrations. Standardized, multi-vendor, future-proof. Build the marketplace on MCP + Skills rather than a proprietary plugin format.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: OSS (Apache-2.0 spec + SDKs). Free protocol; servers self-hosted or third-party. No standard monetization layer yet (build it).

itemsOrTools.name: Infisical Agent Vault / HashiCorp Vault / Doppler
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Secrets management. Infisical: OSS, E2E encryption (cloud can't read secrets), Agent Vault credential proxy purpose-built for agents (prevents prompt-injection leakage). Vault: dynamic auto-rotated secrets, encryption-as-a-service (BSL). Doppler: project/env-scoped dashboard, fast onboarding. All ship official MCP servers.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Mandatory for enterprise secrets management + BYO-key storage. Agent Vault pattern (agent uses credential without seeing it) is the right model for our agent platform to survive prompt injection.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Infisical: OSS + Cloud (free tier). Vault: OSS/BSL + HCP managed. Doppler: managed SaaS only (not OSS).

itemsOrTools.name: LiteLLM / Portkey / Kong AI Gateway
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: LLM gateway: virtual API keys with user/team/project budgets, per-key spend caps (429 on exceed), budget windows (24h/30d), per-agent + per-session tpm/rpm limits, per-session max_iterations + max_budget_per_session to stop runaway loops, multi-provider routing + failover, spend tracking in Postgres.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Core cost-control + multi-provider abstraction layer between our app and Anthropic/OpenAI/image/video providers. Directly implements per-project budgets, runaway-loop protection, and provider failover (key NFRs).
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: LiteLLM: OSS (self-host) + enterprise. Portkey/Kong: managed + OSS components.

itemsOrTools.name: Langfuse + OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: LLM/agent observability: tracing, token/cost tracking, evals. OTel GenAI semantic conventions standardize attributes (gen_ai.usage.input_tokens/output_tokens, cost, latency, quality) across vendors — but most are still EXPERIMENTAL in 2026. Langfuse targets OTel compliance. Helicone now maintenance-mode (joined Mintlify Mar 2026).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Observability stack for the platform. Instrument with OTel GenAI conventions for portability; use Langfuse (OSS, self-hostable) for traces/cost/evals. Avoid Helicone for new builds.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Langfuse: OSS (self-host) + Cloud free tier. OTel: free OSS spec/SDKs.

itemsOrTools.name: Automerge / Yjs + libp2p (local-first P2P sync)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: CRDT libraries for offline-first multi-device sync. Automerge: JSON-like, Git-like history, Rust core to WASM/FFI for iOS+Android. Yjs: fastest. Store CRDT updates as binary blobs in Postgres; cloud as relay. libp2p: modular P2P transport (TCP/QUIC/WebRTC/WebTransport).
itemsOrTools.relevance: Backbone for mobile + P2P + offline-first requirement. Automerge's Rust-to-mobile path fits a desktop+mobile platform; libp2p enables direct P2P; E2E encryption for privacy.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: All OSS (Automerge MIT, Yjs MIT, libp2p dual-licensed). Free; self-hosted relay infra.

itemsOrTools.name: Amazon Working Backwards PR/FAQ + Marty Cagan Opportunity Assessment
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: PR/FAQ: a press release + FAQ written BEFORE building, forcing customer-first clarity (Kindle/AWS/Alexa; adopted by Stripe/Notion/Shopify/Airbnb). Cagan's opportunity assessment: 9 questions (problem, for whom, size, alternatives, why us, why now, GTM, measure success, critical factors).
itemsOrTools.relevance: The PRD methodology backbone. Open our PRD with a PR/FAQ for the platform vision, then a Cagan opportunity assessment, before any feature/UI detail. Forces JTBD and metric clarity up front.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Free templates at workingbackwards.com and svpg.com; book 'Working Backwards' (Bryar/Carr).

itemsOrTools.name: Cursor / Devin / Replit Agent / GitHub Copilot (competitive benchmarks)
itemsOrTools.whatItDoes: Cursor Teams (Jun 2026): Standard $32/seat, Premium $96/seat (5x usage); Enterprise = pooled credits + SCIM + AI-code-tracking API + audit logs. Devin: ACU billing (1 ACU ~15 min; $2/ACU; Team $500/mo incl 250 ACUs; entry $20). Copilot: FedRAMP Moderate, data residency (EU/AU/US/JP), BYOK via VS Code 1.122.
itemsOrTools.relevance: Defines the pricing/metering and enterprise-feature bar we must meet. ACU-style 'agent compute unit' metering and dollar-denominated agent actions are the market norm to mirror for our scheduler/agents.
itemsOrTools.pricingOrAccess: Commercial SaaS; public self-serve + negotiated enterprise.

technicalOptions.option: Messaging channel strategy: Telegram-first, WhatsApp via official Cloud API/BSP
technicalOptions.pros: Telegram is free, commercial-friendly, generous limits (30 msg/s), no ban risk for compliant bots. Official WhatsApp Cloud API is ToS-safe, enterprise-acceptable, predictable per-conversation pricing (Utility down ~45%).
technicalOptions.cons: Official WhatsApp costs per conversation and requires Meta business verification + BSP onboarding friction; loses some features vs full-account access. Telegram has smaller business-user base than WhatsApp in many regions.

technicalOptions.option: Messaging channel: unofficial WhatsApp (Baileys/Evolution API)
technicalOptions.pros: Free, full-account access, fast to integrate, large OSS ecosystem, no per-message fee, works with personal numbers.
technicalOptions.cons: Violates Meta ToS; critical permanent-ban risk (no warning); enforcement escalating from Jan 2026; protocol can break anytime; legally indefensible for an enterprise tier. Acceptable only for opt-in hobbyist/self-hosted mode with explicit risk disclosure.

technicalOptions.option: Model strategy: Claude Code/Agent SDK primary + GPT-5.5 reviewer, behind an LLM gateway
technicalOptions.pros: Best-in-class agentic coding (Opus 4.8, 1M ctx) + independent cross-vendor review (different failure modes); gateway gives budgets, failover, multi-provider, no hard lock-in.
technicalOptions.cons: Two-vendor cost and complexity; Agent SDK separate-credit billing (15 Jun 2026) complicates subscription economics; gateway adds a latency hop and an operational component to run.

technicalOptions.option: Deployment: BYOK + optional air-gapped/self-host alongside managed cloud
technicalOptions.pros: Unlocks regulated/enterprise buyers (data sovereignty, zero-retention, FedRAMP-style needs); reduces our inference COGS when customers bring keys; matches market direction (VS Code 1.122, Cursor, Tabnine).
technicalOptions.cons: Air-gapped/self-host massively increases support, packaging, and release-engineering burden; harder to instrument observability; BYOK shifts but doesn't remove our cost when we run managed compute (scheduler, media).

technicalOptions.option: Pricing: hybrid base subscription + metered agent-compute-units (ACU-style) + budget caps
technicalOptions.pros: Protects margins against AI COGS, aligns price with value, prevents power-user abuse, matches Devin/Cursor norms, predictable floor for buyers. LiteLLM virtual keys make per-project/per-user caps trivial.
technicalOptions.cons: Metered billing is harder to explain and forecast for customers; risk of bill shock without good budget UX; outcome-based variant needs reliable 'job completed' definitions that are hard for open-ended agent work.

technicalOptions.option: Skills marketplace built on MCP + Agent Skills
technicalOptions.pros: Standards-based, multi-vendor, huge existing ecosystem (10k+ MCP servers), future-proof, avoids proprietary plugin lock-in.
technicalOptions.cons: No standard monetization/registry-billing primitives yet — we must build payments, trust/signing, sandboxing, and security review ourselves; third-party MCP servers expand the prompt-injection/supply-chain attack surface.

technicalOptions.option: Sync: local-first CRDT (Automerge/Yjs) + libp2p with cloud relay
technicalOptions.pros: True offline-first, low-latency, P2P-capable, E2E-encryptable, mature libraries, Rust-to-mobile path for iOS+Android.
technicalOptions.cons: CRDT app design is complex (schema migration, garbage collection, large histories); P2P NAT traversal/reliability is hard; harder to enforce server-side audit/compliance when data lives primarily on devices.

recommendations: PRD structure: open with an Amazon-style PR/FAQ (press release + 15-20 FAQs) for the whole platform vision, then a Marty Cagan 9-question opportunity assessment, THEN personas + JTBD (one per surface: solo dev, agency/team lead, enterprise platform admin), THEN scope split into phases with P0/P1/P2 tags. Keep functional and non-functional requirements in separate sections.

Phase the scope explicitly: Phase 0/MVP = Claude Code engine + per-project scheduler + Telegram channel + basic skills (MCP) + desktop app; Phase 1 = GPT-5.5 review model, image studio, secrets vault, cost dashboards; Phase 2 = WhatsApp (official Cloud API only), video studio, mobile app + P2P sync, skills marketplace with payments. Gate WhatsApp-unofficial and air-gapped behind clearly-labeled later phases or never-for-enterprise.

Make enterprise features first-class P0/P1, not a future 'Enterprise tier' afterthought: SAML/OIDC SSO + SCIM, RBAC, immutable+exportable audit logs, zero-retention + no-training contractual language, BYOK, per-project/per-user budget caps. Target SOC 2 Type II first; plan ISO 27001 and ISO 42001; document EU AI Act posture (audit trails + human checkpoints) given Aug 2026 enforcement.

Adopt an LLM gateway (LiteLLM, self-hosted) from day one as the single choke point for all model/image/video calls: virtual keys per project, max_budget + budget_duration, per-session max_iterations and max_budget_per_session to kill runaway agent loops, and provider failover (Anthropic <-> OpenAI). This implements cost-control NFRs and reduces vendor lock-in simultaneously.

Pricing: hybrid model = base seat ($/seat/mo) for predictability + metered 'agent compute units' for usage upside + hard budget caps per project; offer BYOK to cut our COGS and win regulated buyers. Benchmark against Cursor ($32/$96 seat), Devin ($2/ACU), Claude Code ($20 + tokens). Model gross margin assuming ~$230K inference cost per $1M AI revenue and the 15 Jun 2026 Agent SDK separate-credit-pool change.

Secrets: use an agent-aware vault (Infisical Agent Vault or Vault dynamic secrets) so agents use credentials WITHOUT reading them, defending against prompt-injection exfiltration; store BYO keys encrypted with per-tenant isolation; expose secret access via MCP server with audit logging.

Observability: instrument everything with OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions (token/cost/latency attributes) for portability, backed by self-hosted Langfuse for traces, cost, and evals. Treat the conventions as experimental and pin versions. Do not build new on Helicone (maintenance mode).

Define metrics in the PRD with one North Star (e.g., weekly active projects with >=1 successful agent run) plus a supporting basket: activation (time-to-first-successful-run), reliability (agent task success rate, p95 run latency), retention (W4 project retention), unit economics (gross margin per active project, inference $ per successful task), and trust/safety (audit-log coverage %, prompt-injection block rate). Pair each NFR with a concrete threshold (e.g., 'p95 agent step latency < X s', 'audit log immutable + queryable for 100% of agent actions').

Skills marketplace: build on MCP + Agent Skills (standards), but invest early in the missing layer: signing/provenance, sandboxed execution, automated security review of third-party servers, and a monetization/billing primitive (revenue share). Treat third-party MCP servers as untrusted input in the threat model.

Mobile + P2P: use Automerge (Rust core -> iOS/Android FFI) for CRDT sync with a cloud relay and E2E encryption; design schema-migration and history-compaction strategy up front. Keep an audit/compliance copy server-side for enterprise tenants even in local-first mode.
risks: WhatsApp ban/ToS: unofficial Baileys/Evolution API integrations violate Meta ToS with critical, permanent, no-warning ban risk; Meta enforcement against third-party AI chatbots escalating from ~15 Jan 2026. Shipping this in an enterprise tier is legally and operationally indefensible — confine to opt-in self-hosted hobbyist mode with explicit disclosure, or use official Cloud API/BSP only.

Vendor lock-in + pricing volatility: model IDs, prices, and especially the Claude Agent SDK billing model change frequently (e.g., separate Agent SDK credit pool from 15 Jun 2026; Opus 4.8 launched 28 May 2026; Fable 5 GA 9 Jun 2026). An LLM gateway with multi-provider failover is essential to avoid lock-in and absorb price/availability shocks.

Margin compression / cost runaway: AI COGS cut SaaS gross margins to ~50-60% (~$230K inference per $1M revenue). Open-ended agent loops, video generation (~$240 per 10 min 1080p Veo), and power users can blow budgets. Mitigate with per-session iteration + budget caps, hard spend limits returning 429, and metered pricing.

Compliance exposure: EU AI Act enforcement + fines (up to 3% global turnover / EUR 15M) begin 2 Aug 2026; high-risk agentic use requires audit trails + human checkpoints. SOC 2 Type II is table-stakes for enterprise deals. Lacking these blocks enterprise revenue and creates regulatory/fine risk.

Prompt-injection + supply-chain via skills/MCP: third-party MCP servers and dynamically-loaded skills expand the attack surface; an injected prompt can make an agent exfiltrate any secret it can read. Requires agent-aware secret vaults, sandboxing, signing/provenance, and treating all external tool content as untrusted.

Observability standard immaturity: OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions are still mostly experimental in 2026 — attribute names/semantics may change, requiring re-instrumentation. Pin versions and isolate the instrumentation layer.

Reliability of P2P/local-first: CRDT + libp2p stacks are powerful but operationally hard (NAT traversal, history bloat, schema migration, conflict edge cases). Underestimating this risks data-loss/sync bugs and undercuts the offline-first promise; also complicates server-side audit/compliance.

Reviewer-model correlation + cost: using GPT-5.5 as an independent reviewer adds a second vendor's cost, latency, and ToS surface; if both models share training-data blind spots, the 'independent review' value is reduced. Validate reviewer lift empirically before pricing it as a feature.

Marketplace monetization is greenfield: no standard MCP/Skills registry-billing exists, so payments, trust, ratings, and security review are all build-it-yourself — significant scope and a potential security liability if rushed.

Self-host/air-gapped support burden: offering air-gapped/BYO-model deployments to win regulated buyers multiplies release-engineering, support, and observability complexity; scope it as a high-touch enterprise SKU, not a default.
